title: Bootstrap admin row written via hand-built HashMap with hardcoded legacy columns instead of repo::users
description: bootstrap_with_email_password bypasses repo::users::insert and assembles the users row by hand, hardcoding both Plan A2 columns and legacy columns ('name', 'disabled', 'deleted_at'). The comment acknowledges this is deliberate, but it means the canonical column set for a user row now lives in two places (repo/users.rs::insert and here) and they already drift: repo::users::insert writes display_name+name but not email_verified/disabled/deleted_at, while bootstrap writes all of them. Any future schema change to the users row must be remembered in both spots. This is the kind of duplicated, prefix-laden mapping the conventions warn against.
recommendation: Extend repo::users::NewUser (and/or add a `repo::users::insert_admin` constructor) so the single source of truth for the users column set lives in repo/users.rs, then call it from bootstrap with role='admin' and email_verified=true. If the legacy columns are still required for downstream readers, materialize them inside repo::users::insert (one place) rather than re-listing them at the bootstrap call site.
verifierNote:
Partially real but materially overstated; downgraded medium→low. CONFIRMED: bootstrap_with_email_password (bootstrap.rs:58-122) hand-builds the users HashMap and inserts name/disabled/deleted_at explicitly (lines 110-115), so the users column set is assembled in two places (here + repo/users.rs::insert). That is a mild architecture nicety — a future *required* column would need both spots.

But the reviewer's supporting claims are largely inaccurate:
1. "Drift" is not a bug. repo::users::insert omits email_verified/disabled/deleted_at, but migration 001 + 006 (both sqlite + postgres) define those with defaults: email_verified INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0, disabled ... DEFAULT 0/FALSE, deleted_at TEXT (nullable). Omitted columns get migration defaults — correct, not latent drift. The two functions legitimately differ because bootstrap creates an admin (role='admin', email_verified=true verified operator) vs insert's ordinary user.
2. name/disabled/deleted_at are NOT dead legacy columns the insert is "forced to materialize." Migration 006_user_extended_fields creates them as real columns; admin/pages/users.rs:500 reads name, admin/users.rs:162 updates name/disabled/avatar_url. The bootstrap comment's rationale ("this insert materializes the legacy columns for downstream readers" via ensure_table) is stale/misleading since migration 006 already adds them — and disabled=false + deleted_at=null are redundant with the migration defaults.
3. "prefix-laden mapping the conventions warn against" — FALSE. The no-magic/no-mapping convention is about config-var representation translation (env↔D1↔API prefix); there is zero prefix translation here.

No actual convention violation: bootstrap uses the typed wafer_core::clients::database::create client (no exec_raw/query_raw), no block_on/poll_once, no hardcoded domain config. Verdict: a low-severity DX cleanup (consolidate the column set, drop redundant defaulted inserts and the stale comment), not a medium correctness/architecture issue.

title: auth_grants() duplicates 7+ table names as string literals to work around the audit script
description: auth_grants() intentionally hardcodes table-name string literals ('suppers_ai__auth__*', 'suppers_ai__auth__jwt_blocklist', 'suppers_ai__auth__sessions', 'suppers_ai__auth__provider_links', 'suppers_ai__auth__users', 'suppers_ai__auth__rate_limits') rather than referencing the repo::*::TABLE consts, because the WRAP-grant audit script's const-resolver only follows top-level `super::NAME` paths. This is a tooling limitation driving a code-quality compromise: each literal must be kept manually in sync with a `pub const TABLE` in repo/*.rs, exactly the central-vs-owned table-name drift the conventions forbid.
recommendation: Fix at the tooling root cause: teach scripts/audit-wrap-grants.sh's resolver to follow nested `repo::module::TABLE` paths (or re-export the consts at top level as already done for USERS_TABLE/API_KEYS_TABLE/RATE_LIMITS_TABLE in mod.rs and use those), then reference the consts here so the grant targets can't silently drift from the table definitions.

title: Bootstrap fast-path returns a sentinel UserId("bootstrap") that is not a real user
description: require_role's bootstrap-token branch returns Ok(UserId("bootstrap")) — a magic string standing in for a user id. The comment notes admin-gated handlers only read `role` not `id` at this stage, but UserId is otherwise always a real users.id, so any handler that records the acting user (audit log, ownership write) under this UserId would attribute the action to a non-existent 'bootstrap' user. This is primitive-obsession/magic-value coupling: the type system can't distinguish a real user from the bootstrap sentinel.
recommendation: Model the bootstrap principal explicitly rather than overloading UserId with a magic string — e.g. return an enum Principal { User(UserId), Bootstrap } from the require_* methods, or at minimum centralize the sentinel as a named const (BOOTSTRAP_PRINCIPAL_ID) so callers can detect it deliberately instead of string-comparing "bootstrap". This makes write-attribution paths fail loudly instead of silently writing rows owned by a fake user.
verifierNote:
Code is quoted accurately: service.rs:335 returns Ok(UserId("bootstrap".to_string())) on the Admin bootstrap-token fast path, a magic-string sentinel overloading the UserId newtype. This is a genuine primitive-obsession smell and brushes the project's "no magic code/no implicit mapping" convention — so the finding holds as a low-severity maintainability item.

However the reviewer's data-integrity framing is overstated and largely hypothetical. I verified: (1) NO production caller consumes require_role's returned UserId. Grepping all of solobase, the only references to auth.require_role / AUTH_REQUIRE_ROLE / AuthService::require_role outside service.rs are in wafer-core (trait/handler/client) and tests; no solobase handler reads the wire user_id from a role check, let alone persists it. (2) The actual HTTP admin gate (routing.rs:228) uses a DIFFERENT path — helpers::is_admin(msg), which reads the JWT-derived `auth.user_roles` metadata and never invokes require_role, so the sentinel is unreachable there. (3) The bootstrap-token redemption that actually creates a user (auth_ui/api/bootstrap.rs) does NOT use require_role: it calls bootstrap_tokens::is_valid directly, creates a real admin via bootstrap_with_email_password (real uuid::Uuid::now_v7 id at bootstrap.rs:64), and mints sessions on that real id. The sentinel never reaches an ownership/audit write.

So there is no present correctness or security defect — the "writes rows owned by a fake user" scenario requires a future handler that does not exist today. The inline comment (service.rs:324-327) explicitly documents the id-coupling deferral ("Plan A2"). Real, but a low-severity latent smell, not the data-corruption risk implied. The recommendation (named const / Principal enum) is reasonable hardening. Confidence high — grep was exhaustive across both repos.

title: access_token_lifetime_secs read from config twice per login/refresh and is not part of AuthConfig
description: generate_tokens calls access_token_lifetime_secs(ctx) (a config round-trip) on every token mint, and the login/signup/refresh/oauth-callback handlers then call access_token_lifetime_secs(ctx) AGAIN to compute the cookie Max-Age (login.rs:111, signup.rs:246, refresh.rs:179, callback.rs:404) — two config reads of the same key per authentication. AuthConfig is already built once at Init from config but deliberately omits this key, so the value can't be reused. Unlike the documented early-return optimization in ensure_admin_role, here the repeated read has no offsetting benefit.
recommendation: Have generate_tokens return the access lifetime it used (e.g. as a fourth tuple element or a small struct) so handlers reuse it for the cookie instead of re-reading config; or add access_token_lifetime_secs to AuthConfig and thread the already-loaded value through. Either removes the per-auth double config read.

title: store_refresh_token swallows write failures with only a warn log
description: store_refresh_token returns () and logs a tracing::warn on failure, so callers cannot tell whether the refresh token was persisted. If the insert fails (WRAP denial, DB blip), the user receives a refresh JWT in their cookie that has no backing row — every refresh attempt will then 401 because find_by_token returns None, and reuse-detection (family_has_live_row) is also defeated. The minted access token still works until expiry, masking the broken refresh path until later.
recommendation: Return Result from store_refresh_token and let the login/signup/refresh handlers decide (e.g. fail the request or at least surface a structured error) rather than minting a refresh cookie that silently has no DB backing. At minimum, escalate the log to error! and document the degraded behavior.

title: generate_tokens claim-building is ~60 lines of repetitive HashMap inserts with no shared helper
description: The access-claims and refresh-claims maps are built with ~20 individual `.insert(..., serde_json::Value::String(...))` calls, duplicating the user_id/sub/auth_method/iss claims across both maps. This is verbose primitive-string claim assembly that's easy to get out of sync (e.g. access carries email+roles+type+jti, refresh carries family+type — a future claim added to one and forgotten on the other is a silent bug).
recommendation: Introduce a small claims builder (or serialize a typed AccessClaims/RefreshClaims struct via serde_json::to_value into the HashMap) so the shared claims (user_id, sub, iss, auth_method) are written once and the per-token-type claims are added explicitly. Reduces the boilerplate and the drift risk between the two token types.

title: is_expired docstring claims mixed timestamp formats that the repo layer never actually writes
description: is_expired's doc comment justifies RFC3339 parsing by claiming the auth tables intermix `+00:00` and `Z` formats because 'some repo helpers write …Z and others use to_rfc3339()'. In the current repo layer, every expires_at writer (sessions::now_iso/create_for_user, pats::issue, bootstrap_tokens, mod::store_refresh_token) uses the `%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ` (`…Z`) format — no to_rfc3339() writer exists. The parse-based approach is still correct/defensive, but the rationale is stale and misleads future readers into thinking mixed formats are present.
recommendation: Update the comment to reflect that all current writers use the `…Z` format and that RFC3339 parsing is kept as defensive normalization (not because mixed formats exist today), or drop the false claim. Keeps the documented invariant honest.

title: Inconsistent timestamp/format helpers duplicated across repo modules and issuance helpers
description: The `chrono::Utc::now().format("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")` idiom is re-implemented inline or as a private now_iso() in at least sessions.rs, pats.rs, tokens.rs, bootstrap_tokens.rs, users.rs, bootstrap.rs, session.rs (issue_for), and mod.rs (store_refresh_token). crate::blocks::helpers already exposes now_rfc3339() (used by tokens.rs) — so the codebase has two competing canonical 'now' formatters and several private copies. session.rs/pat.rs/bootstrap.rs format expiries inline rather than via a shared helper.
recommendation: Standardize on a single shared timestamp helper (e.g. crate::blocks::helpers for both 'now' and 'format an instant') and have all auth repo/issuance modules call it, removing the duplicated private now_iso() functions and inline format strings. Guarantees one timestamp format across the auth surface (which is what is_expired/string comparisons rely on).

title: Two sequential crypto::random_bytes round-trips per token mint
description: generate_tokens makes two awaited crypto::random_bytes(ctx, 16) host calls in sequence — one for `family`, one for `jti`. On the WASM/Cloudflare path each crypto client call is a host round-trip; requesting 32 bytes once and splitting into two 16-byte halves halves the round-trips on the login hot path.
recommendation: Request a single crypto::random_bytes(ctx, 32) and split into family = hex_encode(&bytes[..16]) and jti = hex_encode(&bytes[16..]). One host round-trip instead of two, same entropy.

title: must_reset written as integer 1/0 while every other bool column is written as a JSON boolean
description: local_credentials::insert stores must_reset as a JSON integer (1/0), but every other auth repo writes bool columns as JSON booleans (tokens.rs revoked = json!(false), orgs.rs is_reserved = json!(false), users.rs email_verified = json!(verified)). On the postgres backend these columns are typed BOOLEAN; inserting an integer literal into a BOOLEAN column is a type error (postgres does not implicitly cast int->bool on INSERT), so local_credentials inserts will fail under postgres while the others succeed. On sqlite it happens to work because everything is loosely typed, which masks the bug. The defensive int-accepting read in row_from_map (lines 35-40) only papers over the write-side inconsistency.
recommendation: Write the boolean directly: data.insert("must_reset".into(), json!(must_reset)); to match the json!(false)/json!(verified) convention used by tokens/orgs/users. This makes the column portable to the typed BOOLEAN postgres schema and lets the read path rely on a single representation.
verifierNote:
Confirmed real and accurate on every claim. local_credentials.rs:64 writes `data.insert("must_reset".into(), json!(if must_reset { 1 } else { 0 }))` — a JSON integer. The other auth repos all write JSON booleans: tokens.rs:56 `json!(false)`, orgs.rs:169 `json!(false)`, users.rs:143 `json!(verified)`. So this is a genuine deviation from the established convention (a "magic mapping" the CLAUDE.md rules forbid).

The postgres failure mode checks out. migrations/001_auth_schema.postgres.sql line 22 declares `must_reset BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE` (sqlite variant uses INTEGER). The postgres backend (wafer-run/crates/wafer-block-postgres/src/service.rs) builds a parameterized `INSERT ... VALUES ($n)` in create_async (lines 90-93) and binds each value via bind_json_value_query, which for a JSON Number calls `q.bind(i)` with i: i64 (line 778) — sent to postgres as bigint/int8. Postgres has no implicit cast from integer/bigint to boolean in a parameterized VALUES context, so this INSERT fails with SQLSTATE 42804. The json!(bool) values used by the other repos bind via `q.bind(*b)` (line 775) as native bool and succeed. There is no cast/coercion in the create path that would rescue the integer (ensure_columns_from_data only types NEW columns; must_reset already exists from the migration). Postgres is a real wired backend (dedicated .postgres.sql, migration_helper.rs branches on backend=="postgres", multiple solobase crates depend on the postgres block) — not dead code.

The bug is currently masked because TestContext uses in-memory SQLite (test_support.rs:56), which is loosely typed and accepts 0/1 into the INTEGER column, so insert_then_find_round_trip_under_wrap passes and never exercises postgres. The defensive int-accepting read in row_from_map (lines 35-40) only papers over the write-side inconsistency, exactly as stated.

Downgraded high→medium: it is a real latent correctness/portability defect that would break every password-credential write under postgres (signup-with-password, admin password set, must-reset), but production runs on Cloudflare D1 (sqlite-typed) and the failure is a hard, loud error rather than silent corruption — so it is not actively breaking the live deployment. Recommended fix is exactly the reviewer's: `data.insert("must_reset".into(), json!(must_reset));`.

title: Identical now_iso() helper copy-pasted across 10 repo modules
description: The exact same `fn now_iso() -> String { chrono::Utc::now().format("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ").to_string() }` is defined privately in 10 of the repo modules (users, sessions, orgs, pats, local_credentials, oauth_pkce, cli_codes, jwt_blocklist, bootstrap_tokens, and the test helper in tokens). This is pure copy-paste; a format change (e.g. to include fractional seconds) would have to be made in 10 places and is exactly the kind of drift that already produced the tokens.rs/now_rfc3339 inconsistency below. blocks/helpers.rs already hosts shared time/format helpers (now_rfc3339, stamp_updated, hex_encode, sha256_hex).
recommendation: Add a single `now_iso()` (or `now_iso_z()`) to crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/helpers.rs next to now_rfc3339() and import it in each repo module, deleting the 10 private copies. Keeps the auth timestamp format defined once.
verifierNote: Confirmed real duplication. grep found 10 production repo modules each with a private `fn now_iso() -> String` whose body is byte-identical: `chrono::Utc::now().format("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ").to_string()` (only users.rs carries an extra comment). The 10 are: jwt_blocklist, oauth_pkce, users, cli_codes, bootstrap_tokens, sessions, local_credentials, pats, orgs, and provider_links. CORRECTION to the reviewer's prose: the 10th member is provider_links.rs, NOT a test helper in tokens.rs — tokens.rs does not define now_iso; it calls the shared crate::blocks::helpers::now_rfc3339() (lines 59/203/207). The reviewer's count of 10 is right; the named list is off by one. helpers.rs is verified to already host shared, wasm-safe time/format helpers (now_rfc3339, now_millis, stamp_updated, hex_encode, sha256_hex) plus a test, so a single now_iso() belongs there and there is no technical obstacle (the module is explicitly wasm-safe). This violates the solobase CLAUDE.md 'Fix at root cause. No code smells, no shortcuts' rule, so it is a legitimate finding. The latent format-drift the reviewer flags is also real: now_rfc3339() yields `to_rfc3339()` (offset/fractional form) while the 10 copies emit plain `...Z`, so two timestamp representations already coexist in the auth block — but that divergence is pre-existing and not caused by these copies, and all 10 copies currently produce identical output, so there is no functional bug today. Downgraded medium→low: pure duplication of an infallible one-liner is a maintainability/cleanup item, not a correctness or security risk. Recommendation (consolidate into helpers.rs, delete the 10 copies) is correct and safe.

title: Timestamp format split: tokens uses now_rfc3339() while sibling repos use now_iso()
description: tokens::insert stamps created_at with helpers::now_rfc3339() (full RFC3339: fractional seconds + '+00:00' offset, e.g. 2026-06-05T12:34:56.123456+00:00), whereas every other auth repo writes timestamps via the local now_iso() ('...Z', no fractional/offset). The auth tables therefore store two different ISO-8601 spellings for conceptually identical columns. tokens' created_at is never string-compared so it is currently harmless, but the divergence is a trap: jwt_blocklist/oauth_pkce/cli_codes/sessions/bootstrap_tokens all rely on lexicographic string comparison of timestamps for expiry, and any future code that compares a now_rfc3339-format value against a now_iso-format value would order incorrectly (the offset/fractional spellings do not sort consistently against the 'Z' spelling).
recommendation: Pick one format for the whole auth surface (preferably the shared helper introduced above) and use it everywhere, so all auth-table timestamps are byte-for-byte comparable. Update tokens.rs to the same now_iso() the rest of the repos use.

title: Boolean JSON decode logic reimplemented inline in 3 repos instead of using RecordExt::bool_field
description: users.rs (row_from_map's `b` closure), orgs.rs (is_reserved match, lines 49-54), and local_credentials.rs (must_reset match, lines 35-40) each hand-roll the same 'accept JSON bool / number / string' boolean decode. blocks/helpers.rs already exports exactly this as RecordExt::bool_field (helpers.rs:48-55). Worse, the copies have drifted: users.rs accepts s.eq_ignore_ascii_case("true") (case-insensitive) while local_credentials.rs and the canonical RecordExt only accept exact s == "true". So the three tables disagree on whether 'TRUE' decodes as true — a subtle behavioral fork born purely of copy-paste.
recommendation: These row_from_map functions operate on HashMap<String, Value> rather than a Record, so RecordExt isn't directly callable. Extract a single free helper (e.g. helpers::json_bool(&Value) -> bool) and have both RecordExt::bool_field and all three row_from_map sites call it, so there is exactly one definition of how a stored boolean is interpreted across the auth surface.
verifierNote: Verified real. All three cited sites exist exactly as quoted: users.rs:45-50 (`b` closure), orgs.rs:49-54 (`is_reserved`), local_credentials.rs:35-40 (`must_reset`); canonical RecordExt::bool_field is at helpers.rs:48-55. The drift claim is accurate: users.rs AND orgs.rs use `s.eq_ignore_ascii_case("true")` (case-insensitive), while local_credentials.rs and RecordExt::bool_field use exact `s == "true"` — so "TRUE" decodes differently across tables. The reviewer actually UNDERCOUNTED: there are 5 inline copies, not 3 — two more live in the LLM block (llm/schema.rs:114-120 and llm/migrations/legacy_providers.rs:95-101), both using `matches!(s.as_str(), "1" | "true")` and additionally defaulting missing/None to `true` (a third semantic variant). The recommendation's premise is correct: row_from_map takes `&HashMap<String,Value>` while RecordExt is impl'd on wafer-core `Record { id, data: HashMap }`, so RecordExt isn't directly callable from these sites — extracting a free `helpers::json_bool` that both RecordExt::bool_field and the map sites delegate to is the right single-source fix, consistent with the project's 'fix at root cause / no code smells' rule. Downgraded medium→low on actual risk: sqlite stores bools as INTEGER 0/1 and postgres as JSON Bool, so the string branch is a defensive fallback these columns don't normally hit with an uppercase literal; the drift is latent, not an active bug. The DRY violation across 5 sites is genuine, hence still a real finding.

title: RepoError::NotFound variant is dead — never constructed
description: RepoError::NotFound is documented ('requested row was not visible after an insert/update') but no code in any repo module ever constructs it: every lookup that can miss returns Ok(None) (e.g. find_by_email, find_by_id, find_by_user_id) and the post-insert-invisibility paths return RepoError::Db('insert returned no row') (orgs.rs:177) instead. The variant is pure dead surface that misleads readers about the error contract described in the module doc.
recommendation: Either delete the NotFound variant (and fix the orgs.rs:177 'insert returned no row' path to use whatever is left), or actually route the post-insert-invisibility cases through it. Don't keep a documented-but-unconstructable variant.

title: orgs.rs uses a private OrgsRepoError instead of the shared RepoError, contradicting the module contract
description: mod.rs's module doc states 'Errors collapse into the RepoError enum so higher layers don't have to reason about the underlying db client's error type.' orgs.rs is the lone module that defines and returns its own OrgsRepoError (NameTaken / AlreadyClaimed / Db) and never touches RepoError. The two distinct unique-constraint variants are a legitimate need, but modeling them as a separate sibling type means callers handling 'a repo error' must now know about two unrelated error enums. This is inconsistent repo shape — the conflict variants belong on the shared error type (or RepoError needs a Conflict(kind) variant) rather than a parallel enum.
recommendation: Fold the two conflict cases into the shared error model — e.g. add a RepoError::Conflict(ConflictKind) (NameTaken/AlreadyClaimed) — so the whole repo layer surfaces one error enum as the module doc promises, and the Db(String) duplication disappears.
verifierNote:
Verified against the source. The quoted enum at crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/auth/repo/orgs.rs:33-41 exists exactly as cited, and mod.rs:5-6 does state "Errors collapse into the [RepoError] enum so higher layers don't have to reason about the underlying db client's error type." I grep'd all 12 repo submodules: the other 10 (users, sessions, pats, tokens, oauth_pkce, provider_links, cli_codes, jwt_blocklist, local_credentials, bootstrap_tokens) use super::RepoError; orgs.rs is the lone module defining its own OrgsRepoError, and it even duplicates the Db(String) variant. So the inconsistency / broken-contract claim is factually correct.

Severity is correctly low (cosmetic architecture smell, no correctness or security impact). I'd actually weaken the reviewer's "callers must now know about two unrelated error enums" framing: in production, NO caller discriminates NameTaken vs AlreadyClaimed. upsert_claimed (the only producer of those two variants) is invoked solely from #[cfg(test)] code (orgs.rs tests + auth_ui/pages/orgs.rs tests). The one production cross-module caller, service.rs:375, calls find_by_name and immediately stringifies via .map_err(|e| AuthError::Internal(e.to_string())); auth_ui/pages/orgs.rs:23 calls list_for_user with .unwrap_or_default(), discarding the error. The "Plan C Cluster 2" HTTP 409 mapping the doc comments cite as justification does not yet exist anywhere in the codebase. So the distinct-variant rationale is currently aspirational and unrealized — which actually strengthens the finding (the parallel enum buys nothing today while breaking the module contract and duplicating Db). The recommendation (fold into a RepoError::Conflict(kind) so the layer surfaces one enum and drops the duplicate Db) is the right root-cause fix. Note this is a pre-existing shape issue, not a regression from the #249 error-handling refactor.

title: provider_links doc comment falsely claims find_by_provider_ref 'remains on raw SQL'
description: The list_for_user doc block claims 'The auth-internal lookup (find_by_provider_ref) remains on raw SQL because it's called only by auth itself' and contrasts typed-vs-raw. This is stale: find_by_provider_ref (lines 107-131) and upsert (63-103) are entirely on db::list_all / db::create / db::update_by_filters — there is no raw SQL anywhere in this module's production code. The comment describes an implementation that no longer exists and would mislead a future reader (or auditor running the exec_raw scan) into thinking a raw-SQL violation lives here.
recommendation: Delete or rewrite the typed-vs-raw paragraph to reflect that the whole module is on db-client builders. Keep only the still-true note about cross-block read access if relevant.

title: Composite-key upsert hand-rolled as list-then-branch — missing builder, with a TOCTOU window
description: provider_links::upsert implements an upsert over the composite key (provider, provider_ref) as a manual list_all -> if empty create else update_by_filters. The db client already exposes db::upsert (wafer-core clients/database.rs:318) but only for a single-field key, so it can't serve a two-column natural key — the repo comment 'db::* has no two-key upsert primitive' is accurate. Per the project rule ('If a builder is missing, add it to wafer-sql-utils rather than working around it'), the root-cause fix is a composite-key upsert builder, not a per-repo read-modify-write. The hand-rolled version also has a check-then-act race (two concurrent first-time OAuth logins for the same provider_ref both see existing.is_empty() and both create), though the migration's PK/unique index makes the second create fail rather than duplicate.
recommendation: Add a composite-key upsert to the db client / wafer-sql-utils (e.g. upsert_by_filters(collection, conflict_fields, data) dispatching to INSERT ... ON CONFLICT(provider, provider_ref) DO UPDATE) and have provider_links::upsert call it. This removes the read-modify-write, closes the TOCTOU window, and matches the orgs.rs upsert_claimed pre-check pattern which has the same shape.
verifierNote:
Confirmed real, low severity. The quoted code exists verbatim at provider_links.rs:63-103: a hand-rolled upsert (db::list_all -> if is_empty() db::create else db::update_by_filters) over the natural key (provider, provider_ref). It IS a genuine architecture-smell under the project's "use a builder, fix at root cause" rule, because an atomic builder-backed path already exists and is already used in-repo.

Key correction to the reviewer's framing: the claim that a composite-key upsert builder is MISSING from wafer-sql-utils is wrong. wafer_sql_utils::upsert::build_upsert (wafer-sql-utils/src/upsert.rs:16) already takes conflict_columns: &[&str] (a slice) and emits INSERT ... ON CONFLICT(<cols>) DO UPDATE. The single-field limitation is only in the convenience wrapper db::upsert (database.rs:318). The correct fix needs NO new builder: provider_links::upsert should call build_upsert(TABLE, data, &["provider","provider_ref"], &["user_id","provider_login","access_token","linked_at"], Backend) and dispatch via db::execute(ctx, &stmt) — exactly the pattern already in products/repo/subscriptions.rs:18-56 (upsert_platform uses build_upsert with &["user_id"] + db::execute). db::execute is WRAP-authorized and documented (database.rs:216-222) as the preferred non-raw write path. So the repo's inline comment ("db::* has no two-key upsert primitive") is misleading — true for the db::upsert wrapper, false for the build_upsert+db::execute path.

TOCTOU is real but benign: migration 001_auth_schema.sqlite.sql:37 has UNIQUE(provider, provider_ref) (the synthetic id is the PK), so two concurrent first-time logins both passing is_empty() => the second INSERT fails on the UNIQUE constraint rather than duplicating — a rare availability hiccup (one concurrent login errors instead of idempotently refreshing), not data corruption. Hence low, not higher.

Two stale docstrings noted (not violations themselves): (1) lines 3-6 claim the upsert "relies on ON CONFLICT against that PK" — false, it hand-rolls and the PK is id, not the composite key; (2) lines 136-142 claim find_by_provider_ref "remains on raw SQL" — false, the non-test code is fully typed (db::list_all/list_sorted/create/update_by_filters); the only exec_raw calls are in #[cfg(test)] fixtures (lines 171/237), an allowed exception. No raw-SQL convention violation here.

title: orgs.upsert_claimed does two SELECT pre-checks before INSERT (TOCTOU + extra round-trips)
description: upsert_claimed runs db::count (verified_via/verified_ref/is_reserved) then find_by_name then db::create then find_by_name again — 4 DB round-trips per claim, with a check-then-act race between the pre-checks and the INSERT. The function comment justifies the pre-checks because 'the wafer-run/database surface doesn't currently let us inspect the constraint name that fired'. That is the actual root cause: there is no way to map a unique-constraint violation back to NameTaken vs AlreadyClaimed, so the repo emulates it with pre-queries. Under concurrency two callers can both pass the pre-checks and one INSERT then fails with an unmapped RepoError::Db instead of the intended NameTaken/AlreadyClaimed.
recommendation: Surface the violated constraint from the db client (e.g. a structured ConstraintViolation { name } in WaferError) so the repo can do a single INSERT and map the constraint name to NameTaken/AlreadyClaimed deterministically — eliminating the pre-check round-trips and the race. Also avoid the trailing find_by_name re-read by returning the created Record's data directly from db::create.

title: find_by_name uses list_all on a UNIQUE column instead of get_by_field
description: orgs::find_by_name looks up a single row on the UNIQUE `name` column via db::list_all (which fetches up to 10,000 rows and takes .into_iter().next()), where the rest of the codebase uses db::get_by_field for unique single-row lookups (users::find_by_email, local_credentials::find_by_user_id, sessions::find_record_by_hash all use get_by_field). list_all also sets a 10k limit and skip_count; using it for a known-unique key is a needlessly broad query and an inconsistent pattern. tokens::find_by_token and pats::find_by_token_hash share the same smell (list_all + .next() on what should be a unique token_hash).
recommendation: Use db::get_by_field(ctx, TABLE, "name", json!(name)) with the NOT_FOUND -> Ok(None) pattern already used elsewhere, for orgs::find_by_name and likewise for tokens::find_by_token / pats::find_by_token_hash. Reserves list_all for genuinely multi-row queries and makes single-row lookups consistent.

title: JSON-API mutation handlers bypass audit logging that the SSR handlers perform
description: Destructive/sensitive admin operations reachable via the JSON API (DELETE/POST /b/admin/api/iam/roles, /user-roles, and the user + settings API handlers) never call logs::audit_log, while the equivalent SSR/htmx handlers in admin/pages/{users,blocks,variables}.rs do. An admin who deletes a role, assigns/removes a role, deletes a user, or changes a config var through the API leaves no audit_logs row, so the audit trail is incomplete and dependent on which UI surface was used. This is an auth/accountability gap on the most security-relevant block.
recommendation: Move audit_log into the leaf mutation handlers (iam/users/settings/custom_tables) so every path that mutates is logged regardless of whether it came from the SSR or JSON API surface, or have the dispatcher in mod.rs::handle emit a structured audit record for every create/update/delete action. Centralizing it removes the SSR-vs-API divergence.
verifierNote: Verified and real. In crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/admin/iam.rs the mutation handlers handle_create_role (66), handle_update_role (91), handle_delete_role (138), handle_create_permission (174), handle_delete_permission (198), handle_assign_role (235) and handle_remove_role (286) all return ok_json directly after db::create/update/delete with no logs::audit_log call. The same is true for the JSON-API settings handlers in admin/settings.rs (handle_set:300, handle_create:354, handle_delete:395) and the users JSON-API handlers in admin/users.rs (handle_update:133→db::update:169, handle_delete:179→db::soft_delete:204). By contrast the SSR/htmx leaf handlers DO log: pages/users.rs:342/364/386 (user disable/enable/delete), :427 (role.create), :454 (role.delete); pages/variables.rs:485/622 (variable.create/update); pages/blocks.rs:258. The dispatcher admin/mod.rs::handle (line 220-258) just routes AdminRoute::IamApi → iam::handle / SettingsApi → settings::handle / UsersApi → users::handle and emits no centralized audit record, so there is no shared logging layer the JSON path goes through. The API endpoints are genuinely registered and reachable (route.rs AdminRoute::IamApi/SettingsApi/UsersApi; mod.rs BlockEndpoint entries at 173-176), gated at AuthLevel::Admin. audit_log itself (logs.rs:123) is fire-and-forget. So the audit trail is incomplete and surface-dependent exactly as described — a genuine accountability gap on the security-relevant admin block. I downgraded high→medium: every reachable path still requires admin auth and nothing unauthorized occurs; the impact is logging/forensic completeness (missing audit_logs rows for API-driven role/user/var mutations), not a confidentiality/integrity/privilege breach. The recommendation to push audit_log into the leaf mutation handlers (or have the dispatcher emit a structured record per create/update/delete) is the correct root-cause fix and removes the SSR-vs-API divergence.

title: validate_url_value localhost-prefix check is exploitable for SSRF (http://localhost.attacker.com)
description: is_localhost is computed by prefix match `value.starts_with("http://localhost")`, which also matches hostnames like `http://localhost.attacker.com/` or `http://localhostfoo/`. is_localhost is then used at line 185 to SUPPRESS the private-IP block, so an attacker-controlled domain whose label merely begins with `localhost` is treated as the dev exception and can also resolve to a private IP without being rejected. The host is never bounded to exactly `localhost` (or `localhost:port`).
recommendation: Parse the authority first, then check the host equals exactly `localhost` (optionally with a `:port` suffix) rather than a raw prefix on the whole URL. Reuse the already-extracted `host` variable for the localhost decision instead of `value.starts_with`.
verifierNote:
Code confirmed at crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/admin/settings.rs:159-187. The core defect the reviewer names is real: `is_localhost = value.starts_with("http://localhost")` is an unbounded prefix that also matches attacker-controlled hosts like `http://localhost.attacker.com`, `http://localhostfoo`, etc. The doc comment (lines 142-145) intends ONLY `http://localhost` as the dev exception, so the recommended fix (extract the authority host first, then require host == "localhost" or "localhost:port", reusing the already-extracted `host` var) is the correct root-cause fix.

However the reviewer's claimed IMPACT is overstated/incorrect. The private-IP block at line 178 fires only when `host.parse::<std::net::IpAddr>()` succeeds — i.e. only for LITERAL IP-address hosts — and this validator does NO DNS resolution. I verified empirically: for `http://localhost.attacker.com/` the parsed host is `localhost.attacker.com` which does not parse as an IpAddr, so line-185's `is_blocked && !is_localhost` suppression is never reached for any hostname. Thus a `localhost*`-prefixed DNS name that "resolves to a private IP" is NOT what gets through line 185; in fact no hostname is ever checked against private IPs here at all. The line-185 `is_localhost` suppression is effectively dead for the described attack, since to reach it the literal text after `http://` must begin with `localhost`, which can never itself be an IP literal.

The genuine exposure is the PROTOCOL gate at line 160: arbitrary `http://localhost<anything>` attacker domains bypass HTTPS enforcement and are accepted as plaintext-HTTP config URL values (e.g. logo/redirect/webhook URLs). That is a real input-validation weakness but: (1) it is an HTTPS-enforcement bypass, not a private-IP SSRF; and (2) the only callers are authenticated admin handle_set/handle_create (lines 328, 375), so it requires admin privilege. Net: real bug, fix as recommended, but downgrade medium→low because the SSRF-to-private-IP scenario is not reachable and exploitation is admin-gated.

title: WRAP-grant create/delete swallow DB errors and give the admin no feedback
description: handle_create_wrap_grant and handle_delete_wrap_grant discard the Result of db::create/db::delete with `let _ = ...` and unconditionally re-render the permissions page. A failed grant write (e.g. table-missing, constraint, WRAP denial) silently reports success to the admin, who believes the access-control rule was created/removed when it was not — a dangerous failure mode for a security-policy surface. The sibling JSON-API handlers (iam.rs, settings.rs) correctly map errors to err_internal.
recommendation: Inspect the Result; on Err, render the permissions page with an inline error fragment (or return err_internal). At minimum tracing::warn! the failure as audit_log does, rather than dropping it entirely.
verifierNote: Confirmed real. crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/admin/mod.rs:401 `let _ = db::create(ctx, WRAP_GRANTS_TABLE, data).await;` and :412 `let _ = db::delete(ctx, WRAP_GRANTS_TABLE, grant_id).await;` discard a genuinely fallible Result — db::create returns Result<Record, WaferError> and db::delete returns Result<(), WaferError> (wafer-core/src/clients/database.rs:109,137). Both handlers then unconditionally re-render permissions_page (mod.rs:404,414), so a failed write is indistinguishable from success in the htmx swap (no toast, no err_internal, no inline error). The sibling-handler claim is correct and stronger than the reviewer stated: the closest analog lives in the SAME pages/ tree — handle_create_role/handle_delete_role (pages/users.rs:424-425, 451-452) are also SSR maud+htmx handlers mutating an access-control table (roles) and they DO `if let Err(e) = ...{ return err_internal("Failed", e.message) }` plus call audit_log; the WRAP-grant handlers do neither. WRAP grants ARE a real security-policy surface (suppers_ai__admin__wrap_grants rows become runtime ResourceGrants via storage.rs update_wrap_grants). I downgraded medium->low because: (1) the table is created at Init via migrations so table-missing is not a realistic steady-state failure; (2) the actual WRAP enforcement is unaffected — only the admin's UI feedback is wrong; (3) a failed create fails closed (no grant = denied). The genuinely concerning case is a failed delete silently leaving a grant in place. Note: the `let _ = db::create` in pipeline.rs:213 is best-effort request-log telemetry, a legitimately different case, so this is not a blanket-accepted pattern. Recommendation stands: inspect the Result and return err_internal / render an inline error fragment, matching the role handlers; at minimum tracing::warn!.

title: Role assignment has a check-then-create TOCTOU with no backing unique constraint
description: handle_assign_role lists existing (user_id, role) rows, returns 409 if any exist, then creates. The two steps are not atomic and USER_ROLES_TABLE declares only `.index(&["user_id"])` (mod.rs:78-83), not a unique index on (user_id, role). Two concurrent assign requests can both pass the emptiness check and create duplicate role rows, which then surface as duplicated entries in users.rs role-enrichment and could confuse role-based checks.
recommendation: Add a UNIQUE(user_id, role) constraint to USER_ROLES_TABLE in the admin migration + CollectionSchema and rely on the constraint (translate the unique-violation error to err_conflict), instead of the racy pre-check.
verifierNote:
Mechanics verified and accurate. iam.rs:247-283 does a non-atomic check-then-create: db::list_all on (user_id, role) → return err_conflict if non-empty → db::create. The backing schema declares only a non-unique index: CollectionSchema USER_ROLES_TABLE has .index(&["user_id"]) (mod.rs:78-83) and migration 001 (both 001_admin_schema.sqlite.sql:53-63 and the postgres variant) creates only suppers_ai__admin__user_roles_user_id_idx — no UNIQUE(user_id, role). So two concurrent assign-role requests can both pass the emptiness check and insert duplicate rows. The admin block is a Singleton but that does not serialize concurrent async requests, so the TOCTOU window is genuine. This also deviates from the codebase's clear pattern of DB-enforced uniqueness (roles/permissions/variables/block_settings all use field_unique / UNIQUE indexes), and the recommended fix is feasible: CollectionSchema::unique_index(&["user_id","role"]) exists (wafer-block types.rs:951) plus a CREATE UNIQUE INDEX migration, then translate the violation to err_conflict.

Severity downgraded medium→low because the reviewer overstated impact. The actual role-based authorization path, auth::Auth::get_user_roles (auth/mod.rs:84-116, the source for crypto.rs's auth.user_roles meta and auth_ui/api/me.rs), DEDUPLICATES roles (line 109: `if !roles.iter().any(|r| r == role)`). Duplicate user_roles rows therefore do NOT 'confuse role-based checks' — that claim is wrong; no privilege escalation or auth bypass. The only real consequence is cosmetic: admin/users.rs:68-79 and admin/pages/users.rs:201-206 collect into non-deduped Vec<String>, so a duplicate role would render twice in the admin UI. Combined with the operation being admin-only (requires two near-simultaneous admin actions), realistic likelihood and blast radius are small. Real bug, low severity.

title: User-ID extraction is inconsistent across users.rs handlers (only delete bounds the path segment)
description: handle_delete carefully takes only the first '/'-bounded segment after /admin/users/ (lines 186-190 with an explanatory comment), but handle_get (line 92) and handle_update (line 137) use a bare strip_prefix that keeps any trailing segments, so /admin/users/u123/foo yields id `u123/foo` for get/update but `u123` for delete. The three handlers should agree; the divergence is a latent bug and a copy-paste smell.
recommendation: Extract a single `fn user_id_from_path(path) -> &str` (segment-bounded, mirroring custom_tables::extract_table_name) and use it in get/update/delete so all three behave identically.

title: WRAP-grant resource_type and write are unvalidated free-form strings/ints (primitive obsession + magic values)
description: handle_create_wrap_grant accepts `resource_type` as an arbitrary form string and stores it verbatim, and encodes `write` as a hand-rolled `if write {1} else {0}`. resource_type is semantically the ResourceType enum (Db/Storage/Network/Crypto) that the runtime matches on elsewhere; storing an arbitrary/typo'd string produces a grant row that silently never matches any resource. There is no allow-list check against the known ResourceType variants.
recommendation: Validate resource_type against the wafer_run ResourceType variants (reject unknown values with the permissions page error fragment) and parse into the canonical enum before persisting, so admins cannot create dead grants from typos.

title: WRAP-grant rows are created without created_at/updated_at stamping (inconsistent with other handlers)
description: Every other create handler in scope calls helpers::stamp_created / now_rfc3339 (iam.rs:84, settings.rs:387, logs.rs:135), but the WRAP-grant create builds its data map directly and relies on the backend to synthesize timestamps. This is an inconsistent pattern across sibling handlers and leaves grant audit metadata to backend defaults.
recommendation: Call helpers::stamp_created(&mut data) before db::create so grant rows carry the same timestamp metadata as roles/permissions/variables.

title: Duplicated RecordList-from-list_all construction in iam.rs
description: handle_list_permissions and handle_list_user_roles both wrap db::list_all results into a RecordList with the identical `{ records, total_count: len, page: 1, page_size: total_count }` shape. The pattern is copy-pasted and ad-hoc (page_size = total row count is meaningless paging metadata).
recommendation: Add a small helper (e.g. RecordList::unpaged(records) in the db client or a local fn) and use it in both handlers; or have these endpoints return the bare Vec<Record> the way other list endpoints do. Removes the duplicated, semantically-odd page metadata.

title: wafer_info handle_flows returns a hand-maintained static flow list that drifts from reality
description: handle_blocks was already fixed to read ctx.registered_blocks() (per its own comment about a list that 'drifted from reality every time a block was added'), but handle_flows still returns a hardcoded JSON array of flow ids/summaries, and handle_info returns a hardcoded version '1.0.0' / feature list. These are exactly the stale hand-maintained lists the block claims to have moved away from, and they hardcode domain-specific block/flow names in block code.
recommendation: Derive the flow list from the runtime (registered flows / endpoints) the same way handle_blocks now does, and source the version from a build constant (env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION")) rather than a hardcoded literal. If the runtime cannot enumerate flows yet, that capability belongs in wafer-run, not a static literal here.
verifierNote: Verified real. In crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/admin/wafer_info.rs: handle_flows (lines 40-58) returns a hardcoded JSON array of 13 flow ids/summaries with the comment "In production, query Wafer runtime."; handle_info (lines 60-68) hardcodes "version":"1.0.0" and a static feature list. Meanwhile handle_blocks (lines 17-37) was already converted to ctx.registered_blocks() with an explicit comment (lines 18-20) that it WAS a "hand-maintained static list that drifted from reality every time a block was added or renamed" — the exact smell handle_flows still exhibits. The reviewer's hedge ("if the runtime cannot enumerate flows yet, that belongs in wafer-run") is moot: the capability already exists. wafer-block/src/context.rs:102 defines Context::flow_introspection() -> Option<&dyn FlowIntrospection>; wafer-run/src/context.rs:565 implements FlowIntrospection for RuntimeContext (flow_infos_json/flow_defs_json off the live startup snapshot); and wafer-block-inspector/src/lib.rs:174-178 already serves its /flows endpoint via intro.flow_infos_json(). The fix is a near-mechanical mirror of handle_blocks — handle is wired live (admin/mod.rs:243 AdminRoute::WaferApi => wafer_info::handle(ctx,&msg)) and already passes ctx to handle_blocks; handle_flows just declines to take it. The version literal should come from a build constant (env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION")) per the recommendation. Genuine architecture-smell plus hardcoded domain-specific block/flow names (workspace CLAUDE.md "no hardcoded domain-specific values in blocks"). Severity low is correct: this is an admin info-read endpoint with no correctness or security impact; the harm is silent drift, not breakage. One precision note: handle_blocks here uses registered_blocks(), not flow_introspection — flows would use the FlowIntrospection sibling, but the live-enumeration precedent and capability are both confirmed in-tree.

title: extract_record_id uses a magic offset and is unbounded, unlike extract_table_name
description: extract_record_id slices with the magic literal 9 ('/records/'.len()) and returns everything after the last '/records/' including any further '/' segments, whereas extract_table_name bounds to one segment. The magic 9 is brittle (silently wrong if the literal changes) and the unbounded tail is inconsistent with the table-name extractor.
recommendation: Use a named const for the separator and strip_prefix on the located position (e.g. `path[idx..].strip_prefix("/records/")`) instead of `+ 9`, and bound the result to a single segment for consistency with extract_table_name.

title: mod.rs::handle re-derives the normalized resource via brittle re-stripping with a comment admitting the duplication
description: The API path normalization is applied once at the top (lines 217-219) and then re-applied verbatim inside the StorageDelegate arm (lines 250-251), with a comment explaining it 'mirrors' the original behavior by re-deriving from path_owned because msg.path() is now mutated. Carrying both the captured path_owned and the mutated msg, and re-running the same strip_prefix in one arm, is a fragile coupling that invites the exact 'set_meta mutates msg.path()' bug the route.rs test comment warns about.
recommendation: Compute the normalized resource string once (it is already done at the top) and pass it explicitly into the delegate arms rather than recomputing from path_owned, eliminating the duplicated strip_prefix and the implicit dependency on mutation order.

title: Stored XSS: request-log method/path spliced unescaped into onclick JS string
description: maud (0.26) escapes only & < > " in attribute values — NOT the single quote. `method` and `path` come from request_logs rows, which are populated from arbitrary inbound HTTP requests (an unauthenticated attacker can craft a request to any path). They are interpolated into a single-quoted JS string inside an HTML `onclick` attribute. A path containing a single quote (e.g. `/x');alert(document.cookie);//`) breaks out of the JS string literal and executes in the admin's authenticated session when the admin opens the Network page. Same untrusted values also flow into `detail_url` which is then spliced into the same onclick.
recommendation: Do not build JS event handlers by string-splicing untrusted data. Move the identifiers to `data-*` attributes (maud escapes them, and `'` inside a double-quoted attribute is harmless) and read them in a delegated JS handler, or JSON-encode + JS-escape the values. URL-encode `method`/`path` when building `detail_url`. Audit the other onclick/href splices in this scope (e.g. storage.rs row toggles use only DB ids, which are safer, but apply the same rule).
verifierNote:
Confirmed genuine stored XSS. Verified every claim against the actual code and dependency source.

1) maud 0.26.0 is the version in Cargo.lock. Its escape_to_string (.cargo/.../maud-0.26.0/src/escape.rs) escapes ONLY & < > " — single quote ' is NOT escaped. Verified directly.
2) maud emits attribute values double-quoted (maud_macros generate.rs: build.push_str("=\"") ... "\""). So onclick="toggleDetail('...','...')" — a ' inside survives unescaped and breaks the JS string literal; HTML " escaping does not help.
3) method/path are attacker-controlled: pipeline.rs:111-112 sets method=msg.action(), path=msg.path() straight from the inbound request, then pipeline.rs:197-213 writes every non-/static//-/health request into REQUEST_LOGS (suppers_ai__admin__request_logs) AFTER routing, regardless of auth/status (a 404/401 still gets logged). So an UNAUTHENTICATED attacker can plant arbitrary path values.
4) network.rs:169-172: row_id = format!("inbound-{}-{}", method, path.replace('/',"_")) and detail_url = format!(".../inbound?method={method}&path={path}"), both spliced into onclick={"toggleDetail('" (row_id) "','" (detail_url) "')"}. network_inbound_tab is the DEFAULT tab (lines 16-19), so simply opening /b/admin/settings/network triggers render.

Simulated the render with payload path /x');alert(document.cookie);// — produces onclick that, after HTML-attribute decode, executes ;alert(document.cookie); in the admin session. Note the path.replace('/',"_") in row_id only neutralizes slashes, not quotes/parens, so the row_id vector alone still breaks out; detail_url carries path verbatim regardless. (Minor correction to the reviewer's exploit string only — does not weaken the finding.)

No mitigating CSP: only CSP in the repo is solobase-web/src/lib.rs which sets script-src 'unsafe-inline' (permits inline handlers); the native admin server has none.

Severity high is correct: unauthenticated-plant → code execution in an authenticated admin's session (admin can manage users, run the SQL explorer, alter auth config). Recommendation (data-* attributes + delegated handler, or JSON/JS-escape, plus URL-encoding method/path in detail_url) is the right root-cause fix. Same pattern should be audited elsewhere; storage.rs row toggles use DB ids which are lower-risk but warrant the same treatment.

title: Duplicated config-sensitivity rule diverges from canonical ConfigVar convention
description: The 'cannot empty a secret' rule is hardcoded here as a key-suffix check (`_SECRET`/`_KEY`), but the canonical convention lives in `ConfigVar::can_be_empty()` / `ConfigVar::is_sensitive()` (defined as `input_type == Password` in wafer-block types.rs:793-812). These two definitions can disagree: a var declared sensitive via `input_type=Password` whose key does not end in `_SECRET`/`_KEY` is emptiable through this handler, silently bypassing the canonical protection. This is exactly the 'no magic code / implicit mapping; same rule everywhere' rule the project forbids — the sensitivity convention now has two independent sources of truth.
recommendation: Resolve the var's ConfigVar (or fall back to the stored `sensitive` int when unowned) and call `can_be_empty()` / `is_sensitive()` instead of re-deriving the suffix rule. The DB row already carries `sensitive`; use it. This keeps the sensitivity definition single-sourced in wafer-block.
verifierNote: Confirmed real. variables.rs:589-595 exists exactly as quoted: the empty-guard is keyed off `var_key.ends_with("_SECRET")||ends_with("_KEY")`, while the canonical sensitivity rule is ConfigVar::is_sensitive()/can_be_empty() = `input_type == Password` (wafer-block types.rs:793-812, verified). The divergence is exploitable: SOLOBASE_SHARED__AUTH__BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_PASSWORD and __BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_TOKEN are declared `.input_type(Password)` (auth/config.rs:90-103) but their keys end in _PASSWORD/_TOKEN, so this handler will NOT block emptying them, bypassing the canonical protection. The handler already fetches the DB `record` (line 598) which carries a `sensitive` int column derived from `input_type==Password` (settings.rs:440/494) and uses it everywhere else in this very file (lines 118,175,238,335,421,517,537) — it just ignores it for the empty-check, so the reviewer's recommended fix (use the stored `sensitive`/resolve ConfigVar) is correct and the data is already in hand. Two clarifications to the finding: (1) The same divergence is duplicated a SECOND time in the JSON API at settings.rs:317-323 (handle_set), so the suffix rule actually has multiple non-canonical copies, not just this one. (2) The reviewer frames `input_type==Password` as the sole canonical convention, but solobase/CLAUDE.md ALSO blesses the `_SECRET`/`_KEY` suffix as a sensitivity convention, and settings.rs:118-120 (`is_sensitive_key`, SEC-060) deliberately unions flag||suffix for masking — so the codebase intentionally treats suffix as one signal. That doesn't refute the finding; it reinforces the 'multiple independent sources of truth' complaint and shows the empty-guard uses only the weaker half. Severity stays medium: it's a documented no-magic/single-source convention violation with a demonstrable bypass, but the leaky vars are `.optional()` and emptying them degrades a feature rather than breaking live auth, so it is not high-severity.

title: Tab-bar scaffolding duplicated verbatim across seven page handlers
description: The `div .tabs { a .tab .(if active==X {"active"} else {""}) href=... hx-get=... hx-target="#content" hx-push-url="true" { ... } }` pattern is hand-rolled near-identically in users.rs (36-58), logs.rs (37-51), storage.rs (37-44), blocks.rs (100-125), network.rs (29-36), variables.rs (26-39), and the settings sub-tabs in permissions.rs (29-54). Each copy repeats the active-class ternary, the hx attributes, and the icon+label. There is no shared `tabs()` UI helper (confirmed: ui/ has none). Adding/altering tab behavior means editing seven sites, and they already drift (some include hx-push-url, some attach extra targets).
recommendation: Add a `components::tabs(items: &[TabItem])` helper (label, href, icon, active, optional hx-target) in ui/components and call it from all seven handlers. The settings `form_page` already takes a structured `tabs: Vec<(label, href, active)>`; extend/reuse that shape so the SSR tab bar is single-sourced.
verifierNote: Duplication confirmed: the tab-bar pattern is hand-rolled in all 7 cited files (users.rs:38, logs.rs:38, storage.rs:37, blocks.rs:100, network.rs:29, variables.rs:26, permissions.rs:29) — plus an 8th the reviewer missed in blocks/files/pages_admin.rs:33. Each copy repeats the active-class ternary + hx-get/hx-target=#content/hx-push-url attrs + icon+label. HOWEVER the reviewer's key claim is FALSE: they wrote "There is no shared tabs() UI helper (confirmed: ui/ has none)" and recommend adding components::tabs(). A shared helper ALREADY EXISTS — ui::components::tab_navigation(tabs: &[Tab], active_id: &str) at ui/components.rs:24, with a Tab struct {id,label,href,icon}. It has ZERO call sites (dead code). The real fix is to wire the handlers onto the existing helper, not create a new one. Adoption was likely skipped because the helper passes icons by string name via sidebar::nav_icon(&str) wrapped in span.nav-icon, whereas every handler calls typed icons::users() directly without that wrapper, and per-tab hx-target/single-tab cases vary slightly. Genuine duplication/maintainability smell under the "fix at root cause / no code smells" rule, but it is pure SSR markup with no correctness, security, or async/SQL implications, so medium overstates it — downgraded to low. The recommendation should be corrected to "reuse/repair the existing tab_navigation helper" rather than "add a tabs() helper."

title: Page-handler boilerplate (load config/user + admin_page wrap) copy-pasted across handlers
description: Every top-level page handler repeats the same opening (`let config = SiteConfig::load(ctx).await; let user = UserInfo::from_message(msg); let tab = msg.query("tab"); let active_tab = match tab {...};`), a near-identical `refresh_action` button (logs.rs 30-35, storage.rs 29-34, network.rs 22-27), and the same `list_page(PageHeader{title:"",subtitle:None,primary_action:None}, None, body, None)` + `admin_page(..., Topbar{ crumbs: crumb(X), primary_action, subtitle, show_palette:true }, ...)` tail. The `PageHeader { title:"", subtitle:None, primary_action:None }` empty placeholder is passed in essentially every call, suggesting the abstraction boundary is off.
recommendation: Extract a `components::refresh_button(href)` helper and consider a small `tabbed_page(title, path, topbar, tabs, body)` wrapper that folds the SiteConfig/UserInfo load + list_page + admin_page tail. Drop the always-empty PageHeader from list_page calls or give list_page a no-header variant.

title: N+1 row-count queries when listing database tables
description: `load_tables` lists all tables, then issues one `SELECT COUNT(*)` per table sequentially inside the loop (`.await` per iteration). On a DB with N tables this is N+1 round-trips, fully serialized — costly on D1/Cloudflare where each query is a network hop, and it runs on every Database page load. This is the same class of amplification the dashboard was explicitly refactored to avoid.
recommendation: Issue the per-table COUNT queries concurrently (collect futures + `futures::future::join_all`) instead of awaiting one at a time, or add a single batched count builder to wafer-sql-utils (e.g. a UNION ALL / `sqlite_stat`-style aggregate) so one round-trip returns all counts. The admin DB explorer is an allowed raw-SQL site, so the issue here is purely the serial N+1, not the use of query_raw.
verifierNote: Confirmed real. crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/admin/pages/database.rs:59-86 `load_tables` runs build_list_tables then loops over every table issuing build_table_row_count + db::query_raw(...).await one at a time — a genuine serialized N+1 (N COUNT queries + 1 list per page render). Code matches the quote exactly. The same anti-pattern is ALSO present in the sibling JSON API at crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/admin/database.rs:45-76 (handle_tables), which the reviewer did not mention; a proper fix should cover both. The reviewer's claims hold up: (a) raw query_raw here is an ALLOWED exception (admin DB explorer page) and the reviewer correctly scopes the issue to the serial N+1, not the raw SQL; (b) the 'same class the dashboard was refactored to avoid' is accurate — commit 9a37b99 'perf(widgets): db::count for 9 dashboard widgets' explicitly closed D1 read-amplification follow-up work. `futures = \"0.3\"` is already a solobase-core dependency, so join_all is available with no new dep. Severity downgraded medium→low: this is an admin-only, auth-gated page rendered on demand by an operator with a typically modest table count (dozens), not a user-facing or steady-state billable hot path like the dashboard/registry traffic that justified the original amplification work; it is a latency/efficiency smell, not a correctness or security bug. Note for the fix: db::count() exists but issues one query per call, so it does not by itself cut round-trips — the root-cause fix is either concurrent join_all of the per-table counts or (preferred per convention) adding a single batched-count builder to wafer-sql-utils introspect that returns all counts in one round-trip.

title: Dashboard chart queries run sequentially after the join! optimization
description: The handler carefully runs the six tile/panel queries concurrently via `futures::join!` (and a comment brags about eliminating D1 amplification), but then issues the three 30-day chart aggregations strictly sequentially with separate `.await`s. These three are independent and could join the same concurrency batch — currently they add three serial round-trips on every dashboard load.
recommendation: Fold the three `daily_counts_30d` futures into the existing `futures::join!` (or a second join!) so all dashboard queries are concurrent, matching the stated intent of the tile-query refactor above.

title: registered_blocks() fetched twice in storage_rules_tab
description: `storage_rules_tab` calls `ctx.registered_blocks()` twice (once as `blocks`, once as `registered`) and derives two slices from them. `registered_blocks()` returns an owned `Vec<BlockInfo>` (clone of every block's metadata incl. endpoints/collections), so this duplicates a non-trivial allocation per render for no reason.
recommendation: Call `ctx.registered_blocks()` once and derive both `block_names` and `storage_blocks` from the single Vec.

title: Stringly-typed rule/grant/permission fields parsed ad-hoc at every render site
description: WRAP grants, storage rules, and network rules are read as raw `record.data.get(field).and_then(|v| v.as_str()).unwrap_or(default)` everywhere — in permissions.rs (all four sections), storage.rs (storage_rules_tab), network.rs (network_rules_tab). The boolean `write` field is decoded with the fragile triple-form `v.as_i64().unwrap_or(0)!=0 || v.as_str()==Some("1")` in two places (permissions.rs:187 and 476-479), the literal rule_type/access/scope strings ('block','allow','readwrite','global') are re-matched at each site, and human-label mapping is partly centralized (human_resource_type) but partly re-inlined (the badge_class match at 627-634). These rows have stable shapes (WRAP_GRANTS/STORAGE_RULES/NETWORK_RULES schemas in mod.rs) and should be typed.
recommendation: Introduce small typed structs (WrapGrant, StorageRule, NetworkRule) with a `from_record` constructor that does the field extraction + bool coercion once, and an enum for resource_type/access/rule_type. The render sites then read typed fields. This removes the duplicated coercion and the divergent default values (e.g. network rule_type defaults to 'block' in one place and 'allow' framing elsewhere).
verifierNote:
Verified against the actual code. The quoted decode exists at permissions.rs:187 and :476-479 verbatim, and the same raw `record.data.get(field).and_then(as_str).unwrap_or(default)` pattern repeats across all four sections of permission_rows() (461-598), storage.rs:172+ (storage_rules_tab), and network.rs:305+ (network_rules_tab). The schemas ARE stable: mod.rs:118-123 declares WRAP_GRANTS with explicit fields incl. `field_default("write","int","0")`, and STORAGE_RULES/NETWORK_RULES are similarly fixed-shape (mod.rs:25-29). So the reviewer's core point — fixed-shape rows decoded ad-hoc at every render site, badge_class re-inlined at 627-634 while only human_resource_type is centralized, and divergent defaults (storage rule_type defaults to "allow" at line 519 vs network's "block" at line 564) — is accurate. A `from_record` typed struct (WrapGrant/StorageRule/NetworkRule) + enums would be the clean root-cause fix and matches solobase's "no magic/implicit mapping" rule.

Two corrections that lower the severity from the claimed medium to low: (1) The triple-form `v.as_i64().unwrap_or(0)!=0 || v.as_str()==Some("1")` is NOT a live fragility — the ONLY writer (mod.rs:398) stores `write` as `serde_json::json!(if write {1} else {0})`, always a JSON integer, so the `as_str()=="1"` branch is provably dead defensive cruft, not a real representation divergence. (2) These are admin-only (AuthLevel::Admin) SSR display handlers; the divergent default strings only affect the human-readable sentence shown when a field is absent — they do not drive WRAP enforcement (the enforcement engine reads these rows elsewhere). So there is no correctness or security impact; the row never round-trips through a wrong type. Net: a genuine maintainability/code-smell finding worth a typed refactor, but display-only and harmless today, hence low rather than medium.

title: Empty-string sentinel for current_user_id is primitive-obsession with a hidden contract
description: `single_user_row(record, roles, current_uid: &str)` uses `current_uid == ""` to mean 'this is a single-row fragment update, suppress the (you) affordance'. The contract is documented only in a doc-comment and enforced by passing a literal `""` from `user_row_fragment` (line 326). An empty string is an in-band sentinel for a genuinely optional value; a caller could pass an empty id by accident and silently change behavior.
recommendation: Take `current_uid: Option<&str>` (or a dedicated `RowContext`) so the 'no self affordance' case is explicit rather than an empty-string sentinel.

title: Block-name <-> identifier translation logic scattered as inline string munging
description: The mapping between a block name (`org/block`), its DB/config prefix (`org__block__` / uppercased), and its URL encoding is computed inline with `replace('/', "__").replace('-', "_")` here, while blocks.rs computes `name.replace('/', "--")` for URL segments and database.rs reverses `org__block` back to `org-block/block` in `group_label`. Three different inline transforms encode the same naming convention in three files; if the convention changes (or a block name contains an unexpected char) they drift independently. The project rule is 'no magic/implicit mapping — same value/format everywhere'.
recommendation: Centralize the block-name encoding/decoding (URL segment form, DB-ident/prefix form, config-prefix form) into named helpers on the block-name type or a shared module, and call them from permissions.rs/blocks.rs/database.rs instead of re-implementing the replaces inline.
verifierNote: Confirmed real. permissions.rs:238 `format!("{}__", b.name.replace('/', "__").replace('-', "_"))` is a verbatim inline reimplementation of the EXISTING public, tested helper `wafer_block::wrap::resource_prefix()` (wafer-run/crates/wafer-block/src/wrap.rs:63), whose unit test (wrap.rs:557) asserts `resource_prefix("suppers-ai/auth") == "suppers_ai__auth__"` — exactly the value permissions.rs computes. solobase-core already depends on wafer-block (Cargo.toml:118), so the helper is directly importable; the inline code bypasses it. The config_prefix uppercase variant (permissions.rs:239) likewise duplicates the SCREAMING_SNAKE convention encoded in runtime.rs:731 `block_name_to_var_prefix` (private) and solobase-cloudflare/config_source.rs:69 `screaming_block`. blocks.rs:22 `encode_block_name` (`/`->`--`) is the URL-segment form (distinct, no shared helper), and database.rs:201 `group_label` reverses `org__block` overlapping with `wafer_block::wrap::resource_owner` (wrap.rs:41). So the reviewer's count of three inline transforms encoding one convention is accurate — and slightly UNDERSTATED, since a ready-made public `resource_prefix` already exists and is being reimplemented rather than merely scattered. This is a genuine 'no magic/implicit mapping — same value/format everywhere' + DRY/root-cause violation. Severity stays LOW: this is admin-UI JS-form-hint generation, not a security boundary (actual WRAP enforcement already uses the canonical helper), and the values match by convention so there is no current functional bug — it is a drift/maintainability risk only. Recommendation is sound: call `wafer_block::wrap::resource_prefix` from permissions.rs, and add/export named helpers for the SCREAMING_SNAKE and URL-segment forms so blocks.rs/database.rs/config_source.rs share one source of truth.

title: Email settings save swallows config::set errors and reports success unconditionally
description: `handle_save_email_settings` writes each field with `let _ = config::set(...).await`, discarding the Result, then always returns `{"message":"Email settings saved"}`. If a write fails (e.g. WRAP denial, DB error) the admin sees a success toast while the value was not persisted. Given the recent #249 push to replace string-coerced errors with explicit handling, silently dropping a fallible write here is the opposite pattern.
recommendation: Propagate the first `config::set` error and return `err_internal`/a failure JSON so the toast reflects reality. At minimum, collect failures and report which keys failed.
verifierNote: Verified at crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/admin/pages/email.rs:124-129. The quoted code exists exactly: the loop does `let _ = config::set(ctx, field.key, value).await;` and the handler unconditionally returns `{"message":"Email settings saved"}`. config::set returns Result<(), WaferError> (wafer-core/src/clients/config.rs:33), so a WRAP-deny/DB failure is discarded and the admin gets a success toast for an unpersisted value. The frontend JS (lines 103-107) keys the toast type off `d.error`, so the response shape is designed to carry an error the handler never sets. So the bug is real and the low severity is appropriate (admin-only, no security boundary crossed — WRAP still denies; nothing unsafe persists). Two corrections to the reviewer's framing: (1) This is NOT a #249-related regression. #249 removed RuntimeError string-coercion in error variants; there is no string-coerced error here, and #249's direction does not cover UI-toast accuracy. (2) The implied 'right pattern' is overstated. I checked all 5 analogous settings-save handlers: email.rs, userportal/mod.rs:779, and auth_ui/pages/settings.rs:224 all use `let _ = config::set(...)`; products/pages.rs:671 and legalpages/pages.rs:884 use `if let Err(e) { tracing::warn!(...) }` but STILL return `{"message":"Settings saved"}` with no error field — so even the 'careful' handlers show a success toast on failure. The genuinely correct fix (collect failures, return `{"error": ...}`) is implemented by none of them. This is a codebase-wide consistent UX gap, not a localized outlier, so singling out only this file slightly overstates it; a proper fix should cover all five.

title: Cubic-feeling per-config-key grant lookup loop still walks two synthesized resource patterns per key
description: The comment claims the old cubic blocks×grants×config_keys loop was replaced by an O(1) map, but the template still allocates `[ck.key.clone(), format!("{}*", ck.key)]` for every config key on every render and loops over both, plus the inner `@for (grantee, write)`. The `format!("{}*", ck.key)` encodes the WRAP wildcard-pattern convention inline (a value+suffix mapping) and allocates a String per key per render. It works but re-introduces per-key allocation in the supposedly-optimized path and hardcodes the `*` pattern convention.
recommendation: When building `grants_by_resource`, also index by the stripped (non-wildcard) form so the lookup is a single `get(&ck.key)` without per-render `clone()`+`format!`. Centralize the `{key}*` wildcard convention in a helper rather than inlining the `format!`.

title: Broken indentation in custom_blocks_list signals copy-paste / merge damage
description: The `@else` branch of `custom_blocks_list` is indented two levels short of its surrounding block (the `div .table-container` and its closing braces sit at column 16 while the enclosing `@if`/`@else` is much deeper), and the function's trailing braces are visibly misaligned. This is cosmetic but indicates the block was edited by hand without a formatter pass; maud blocks are hard to read when indentation lies about nesting.
recommendation: Re-indent the `@else` body to match its nesting depth. Confirm `cargo +nightly fmt` is run (maud macro bodies aren't auto-formatted, so this needs manual cleanup).

title: custom-blocks install/upload/delete handlers are TODO stubs wired to live admin routes
description: `handle_custom_block_install`, `handle_custom_block_upload`, and `handle_custom_block_delete` ignore their inputs (`_ctx`, `_input`, `_block_name`) and return canned 'local deployment' HTML with `TODO(cloud)` markers. The Custom tab UI (custom_tab_content) presents working-looking install/upload forms and an enabled toggle/delete on the custom-blocks list, but submitting them does nothing real — the delete handler even returns an empty list regardless of what was deleted. This is dead/placeholder behavior exposed through real, admin-gated routes (registered in route.rs + mod.rs endpoints), which is misleading to operators.
recommendation: Either implement the cloud path or make the UI honestly reflect the no-op (disable the forms / show an explanatory state) so admins aren't presented with controls that silently do nothing. If kept as stubs, track them as a single owned follow-up rather than three independent TODOs behind live routes.

title: WRAP grant create/delete handlers skip audit logging that every other mutation performs
description: Every other admin mutation in this scope writes an audit_log entry (user.disable/enable/delete, role.create/delete, variable.create/update, block.enable/disable). `handle_create_wrap_grant` and `handle_delete_wrap_grant` — which change security-relevant cross-block access — perform `db::create`/`db::delete` with `let _ = ...` (also swallowing the error) and emit no audit_log. Granting one block read/write into another block's data is arguably the most security-sensitive admin action and is the one with no audit trail.
recommendation: Add `logs::audit_log(ctx, &admin_id, "grant.create"/"grant.delete", resource, &ip)` to both handlers (these live in mod.rs but operate on the permissions pages in scope), and stop discarding the db::create/delete Result so a failed grant write surfaces.

title: Checkout claim not reverted when Stripe response fails to parse — purchase stuck forever in checkout_started
description: handle_checkout transitions the purchase pending -> checkout_started via claim_for_checkout (line 85). Every later failure reverts the claim back to pending EXCEPT the JSON-parse failure of the Stripe response: this branch returns an error without calling revert_checkout_claim. Because the response passed the `status_code >= 400` gate, a truncated/garbled 2xx body (or any deserialization hiccup) permanently parks the purchase in `checkout_started`, which claim_for_checkout will never re-claim (it only matches `pending`). The user can never retry checkout for that purchase.
recommendation: Call repo::purchases::revert_checkout_claim(ctx, &body.purchase_id).await before returning on the parse-failure branch (and likewise if session_id/url come back empty), mirroring the revert already done on the >=400 path. Better: wrap everything after the claim in a helper that reverts on any Err so future post-claim failure paths can't leak this state.
verifierNote: Confirmed real. In crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/products/stripe.rs, handle_checkout claims the purchase pending -> checkout_started via claim_for_checkout (line 85). claim_for_checkout (repo/purchases.rs:121-149) ONLY matches rows where status="pending", and the only writer that reverts checkout_started -> pending is revert_checkout_claim (repo/purchases.rs:153-181), called exclusively on the >=400 path (stripe.rs:173). The JSON-parse-failure branch at lines 191-194 returns err_internal_no_cause (verified: just logs + returns an Internal error, no state revert) WITHOUT calling revert_checkout_claim. So a 2xx Stripe response with a body that fails to deserialize permanently parks the purchase in checkout_started, and the checkout endpoint can never re-claim it (rows==0 -> err_bad_request at line 89-91). I grepped the whole products block: no cron/sweeper/admin path resets checkout_started -> pending, so recovery requires manual DB intervention.
