The reviewer's secondary note is also correct: if a 2xx body parses but lacks id/url (lines 196-197), the handler still leaves the purchase in checkout_started (empty provider_session_id, empty checkout_url) with the same dead-end. Downgrading severity high->medium: the bug is genuine and the stuck state is unrecoverable, but the trigger is a narrow edge case — a 2xx response from Stripe that fails to parse, or one missing id/url — not a routinely-hit path; blast radius is per-purchase, not systemic. Recommended fix is sound and matches the existing >=400 revert pattern; the 'wrap post-claim work to revert on any Err' variant aligns with the repo's root-cause convention.

title: stripe.rs writes provider_session_id, a column declared in neither CollectionSchema nor the migration
description: After a successful Stripe session creation, the purchase is updated with `provider_session_id`. That column does not exist in the purchases CollectionSchema (mod.rs:98-112) nor in 001_products_schema.sqlite.sql / .postgres.sql (the purchases table there has stripe_payment_intent_id, provider_payment_intent_id, approved_at, etc. but no session-id column). On Postgres this write will error; on SQLite/D1 with lazy column synthesis it may be silently dropped or lazily added off-schema. Either way the Stripe session id is not reliably persisted, so there is no durable link from a purchase to its Stripe checkout session.
recommendation: Add `provider_session_id` to the migration (a new migration 003 ALTER, since 001 is hash-gated and already applied) and to the CollectionSchema, or stop writing it. Decide on one canonical session-id column and use it consistently.
verifierNote:
CONFIRMED REAL, but the reviewer's per-backend runtime claims are backwards. The write exists exactly as quoted (stripe.rs:205-208) and `provider_session_id` appears ONLY at stripe.rs:206 in the entire repo — never in the PURCHASES_TABLE CollectionSchema (mod.rs:98-112) nor in 001_products_schema.{sqlite,postgres}.sql (which carry stripe_payment_intent_id, provider_payment_intent_id, approved_at, but no session-id column). It is also never read back anywhere; the session_id is returned to the client directly in the response (stripe.rs:218), so the persisted column is dead write-only data with no consumer.

Corrections to the reviewer: (1) Postgres does NOT error — its update_async (wafer-block-postgres/src/service.rs:125) calls ensure_columns_from_data and would lazily ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN ... TEXT, off-schema. (2) On SQLite/D1 — the ACTUAL production backend per the migration comment 'Solobase deploys SQLite/D1 today' — the value is NOT silently dropped or lazily added: the SQLite update() (wafer-block-sqlite/src/service.rs:441-500) does NOT call ensure_columns_from_data (only create() does, line 396), so `UPDATE ... SET provider_session_id = ?` against a table without that column returns a 'no such column' error.

Impact: stripe.rs:213-215 swallows that error as a tracing::warn!, so checkout still succeeds (user gets the checkout URL). But the whole upd map is one UPDATE, so the SQLite/D1 failure ALSO drops the legitimate `provider = 'stripe'` and `updated_at` writes in the same statement — every real Stripe checkout leaves `provider` stuck at its default 'manual'. That's genuine latent data corruption, though muted: purchase completion is keyed off provider_payment_intent_id in the webhook, not `provider`, so payments still complete. Real off-schema-write bug worth fixing per the no-magic / root-cause conventions (add a migration 003 ALTER + schema field, or stop writing the column and the provider/updated_at writes survive). Medium is reasonable given the silent per-checkout provider corruption, despite it not breaking checkout or payment.

title: Two unrelated price columns (price vs base_price): pages display `price`, all pricing/purchase logic reads `base_price`
description: The products table declares both `price` and `base_price` (mod.rs:62-63). The admin Manage and user My-Products tables render `price` (pages.rs:147, 427), but every pricing and purchase calculation reads ONLY `base_price` (pricing.rs:46, purchase.rs:111/119/126). There is no normalization between the two anywhere in the codebase. Result: a product priced for sale via `base_price` shows blank/0 in the admin grid, and a product whose UI sets `price` will be charged 0 at purchase (caught by validate_price, so the buyer just sees 'Invalid price'). This is the 'no magic/implicit mapping — a value means the same thing everywhere' rule being violated by having two columns for one concept with split read/write sites.
recommendation: Collapse to a single price column. Either make pages display `base_price` (the column the engine actually uses) and drop `price`, or make the engine read `price` and drop `base_price`. Update CollectionSchema + migrations accordingly.
verifierNote: Confirmed in the actual code. PRODUCTS_TABLE declares BOTH columns: `price` float default "0" (mod.rs:62) and `base_price` float default "0" (mod.rs:63). The two display grids render `price` — pages.rs:147 (`record.str_field("price")`, admin Manage) and pages.rs:427 (`r.str_field("price")`, user My-Products). Every pricing/purchase calculation reads ONLY `base_price`: pricing.rs:46 (direct-price branch when no template), and purchase.rs:111/118/125 (all three fallback branches in handle_create). I grepped the whole products module and confirmed there is NO normalization between the two anywhere — `price` appears only at the two display sites, `base_price` only at the logic sites + tests. The write path is a generic passthrough (crud::crud_create/crud_update and the user handler's `db::create(...data)` at handlers.rs:597) — neither column is defaulted/derived, so whatever field name a form submits is stored unreconciled. Tellingly, EVERY test (purchase_tests.rs, handler_tests.rs) seeds `base_price`, never `price`, so the engine-canonical field is `base_price` while the rendered grids show `price` — meaning a correctly-priced product shows blank in both admin and my-products grids. This is a genuine 'two columns for one concept with split read/write sites and no mapping' violation of the no-magic/implicit-mapping rule. Severity correction on the reviewer's framing: the charge side is NOT a security/money hole — validate_price (pricing.rs:125-137, rejects <=0 and <MIN_PRICE=0.01) plus the total_cents guards (purchase.rs:155-166) reject a 0-priced product with 'Invalid price' rather than charging 0; the reviewer correctly acknowledged this. So the real impact is broken display + confusing purchase rejection + a dual-source-of-truth maintainability smell, which lands solidly at medium (low end), not a high-severity charge bypass. Recommendation to collapse to one column (and update CollectionSchema + the display sites) is sound.

title: Rollback on line-item failure deletes the purchase but orphans already-inserted line items
description: handle_create inserts the purchase header, then loops inserting line items with no surrounding transaction. If line item N fails, it deletes the purchase header — but line items 1..N-1 were already inserted and are NOT deleted, and the line_items table has no ON DELETE CASCADE (migration 001 line 112-126). The comment only reasons about the header ('a dangling pending purchase'), missing the orphaned children. These orphaned line_items rows accumulate silently and reference a non-existent purchase_id.
recommendation: Delete the purchase's line items as part of rollback (repo::purchases already lists them; add a delete-by-purchase_id repo fn) before/with deleting the header, or perform the whole header+items write inside a DB transaction so partial failure rolls back atomically.
verifierNote: Verified and accurate. In purchase.rs handle_create (lines 199-253), line items are inserted one-per-loop-iteration with no enclosing transaction. On the first add_line_item failure, the rollback calls repo::purchases::delete(ctx, &purchase.id) which (purchases.rs:38-40) deletes only the PURCHASES_TABLE header row by primary key. Already-inserted sibling line items (iterations 1..N-1) are never deleted. Confirmed both migrations define line_items with `purchase_id TEXT NOT NULL` and NO `FOREIGN KEY ... ON DELETE CASCADE` (sqlite 001 lines 113-126; postgres 001 lines 108-121), so there is no DB-level cascade to clean them up. No delete-by-purchase_id repo fn exists; list_line_items (purchases.rs:52) exists and could back one, as the reviewer suggests. Multi-item purchases are normal (line_items_data is a Vec built per cart product, lines 142-149), so the partial-failure case is reachable. The inline comment (lines 223-227) reasons only about the dangling header and is blind to orphaned children, exactly as claimed. This is a real root-cause data-integrity defect (CLAUDE.md: fix at root cause, no half-fixes); the proper fix is a single atomic header+items write or a delete-by-purchase_id cascade in the rollback. Severity adjusted down from medium to low: it only triggers on a transient mid-loop DB write failure, and the resulting orphaned line_items are inert — the parent purchase is either deleted or marked `failed`, the rows are keyed by a purchase_id that no longer resolves to a usable purchase, and no user-facing read (list_user/list_admin/line_item lookups) surfaces them or produces wrong billing. So it is silent storage cruft, not a correctness/security exposure. Note one minor overstatement in the reviewer's evidence comment ('never touches sibling line items') — correct; the marked-failed branch also leaves siblings, so the leak occurs on both rollback sub-paths, not only when delete succeeds.

title: Pricing formula evaluator silently ignores trailing tokens after a valid sub-expression
description: evaluate_formula tokenizes, parses one expression, and returns without verifying that all tokens were consumed. A malformed formula such as `"base_price 5"`, `"2 3"`, or `"(1+2))"` parses the first valid sub-expression and silently discards the rest, returning a partial result instead of an error. For a money-computing engine this means a typo'd template formula can produce a wrong-but-plausible price that passes validate_price and creates a purchase at the wrong total.
recommendation: After parse_expression, return Err if `pos != tokens.len()` (e.g. 'Unexpected trailing tokens in formula'). Add a test for `"2 3"` and `"1+2)"`.
verifierNote: Confirmed real. In crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/products/pricing.rs:143-148, evaluate_formula calls parse_expression once and returns Ok(result) with no check that pos == tokens.len(). The recursive-descent parser (parse_expression/parse_term/parse_factor, lines 228-317) stops at the first token it can't continue with, so trailing tokens are silently dropped. I hand-traced the reviewer's examples: "base_price 5" -> parses base_price, leaves Number(5) (pos=1, len=2); "2 3" -> returns 2.0, drops 3; "(1+2))" -> parse_factor consumes the inner expr + first RParen (pos=5, len=6), drops the extra RParen. All three return Ok of a partial value instead of an error. Blast radius is real and money-relevant: purchase.rs:102 uses evaluate_formula to compute the purchase total and its own comment ("Surface formula errors instead of falling back to 0.0 - a broken template would otherwise sneak past validate_price below and create a purchase with the wrong total") states a fail-closed intent that this gap defeats. validate_price (lines 125-138) only rejects non-finite/<=0/<0.01, so a plausible partial price passes it. The test file (tests/pricing_tests.rs) covers missing-closing-paren and bad-char but has no trailing-token test, so this is uncaught. The recommendation is correct. Note the fix should return Err(String) to match the existing Result<f64,String> evaluator contract (consumed via err_bad_request) - that stringly-typed shape is the established local pattern here, not the RuntimeError path touched by #249, so it is not a separate convention issue. Severity held at medium rather than higher because the formula text is admin-authored template data, not attacker-controlled end-user input (variables are numeric); it masks authoring typos rather than enabling an exploit.

title: Admin manage-products LIKE search is not wildcard-escaped, unlike the JSON handlers
description: handlers.rs introduced escape_like() and wraps every LIKE search through it (handlers.rs:261, 492) so user input like '100% off' or 'a_b' doesn't act as SQL LIKE wildcards. The SSR pages search path builds the LIKE pattern with raw interpolation and bypasses escape_like entirely, so the admin Manage page (and any future page search) has the exact behavior handlers.rs deliberately fixed. Inconsistent handling of the same concern across two files in the same block.
recommendation: Move escape_like into a shared helper (it is currently private to handlers.rs) and use it for the pages.rs search filter too; or expose the escaped-LIKE construction as a single function both call sites reuse.

title: Seven near-identical ownership-check blocks duplicated across user-owned CRUD handlers
description: handle_user_get_product, handle_user_update_product, handle_user_delete_product, handle_user_get_group, handle_user_update_group, handle_user_delete_group, and handle_user_group_products each repeat the same pattern: extract user_id, strip path prefix, validate id non-empty, db::get the row, compare created_by/user_id to user_id returning err_not_found on mismatch, with the same NotFound/internal error arms. This ~15-line block is copy-pasted ~7 times, differing only in table, ownership-field name, and path prefix.
recommendation: Extract a helper like `async fn load_owned(ctx, table, id, owner_field, user_id, label) -> Result<Record, OutputStream>` (or extend crud.rs with an owner-scoped variant) and call it from all seven handlers.

title: Dead/duplicated money columns: amount_cents always equals total_cents and is never read; stripe_payment_intent_id never written
description: purchase.rs writes both total_cents and amount_cents to the identical value, and a grep shows amount_cents is never read anywhere in the block (or schema-consumers). Separately, CollectionSchema/migration declare stripe_payment_intent_id but the code only ever writes/reads provider_payment_intent_id (repo/purchases.rs:96,226). So the purchases table carries two redundant cents columns (one dead) and a dead payment-intent column alongside the real one — primitive/duplicate-column obsession that invites future drift about which column is authoritative.
recommendation: Drop amount_cents (or, if it was meant to track post-refund residual, actually populate/read it). Drop stripe_payment_intent_id in favor of the single provider_payment_intent_id that the code uses, updating CollectionSchema + migration.

title: calculate-price endpoint is unauthenticated, ignores product status, and echoes the pricing formula back to callers
description: handle_calculate (reached via the public /b/products/calculate-price route in handlers.rs:212, with no auth gate and no BlockEndpoint auth declaration) fetches any product by id regardless of status (draft/inactive included) and returns the raw price_formula string and variables_used in the response. This lets any unauthenticated caller enumerate draft product ids and reverse-engineer internal pricing formulas. The catalog endpoint, by contrast, is careful to only expose status=active products.
recommendation: Gate calculate-price to active products (return not-found for non-active, like handle_get_product_public), and stop echoing the raw `formula` to clients (return only the computed numbers). If formula echo is needed for an admin tool, expose that via the admin route instead.

title: Admin product delete is a hard delete despite a soft-delete (deleted_at) design
description: The products table has a deleted_at column and the SSR pages filter on `deleted_at IS NULL` (pages.rs:92-96, 390-394), implying a soft-delete model. But handle_delete_product routes to crud::crud_delete, which performs a hard DELETE (crud.rs:145). So admin deletes physically remove rows (orphaning any line_items / purchase references and the deleted_at filtering becomes pointless), while the surrounding code is written as if rows are soft-deleted. The catalog list (handle_catalog) also never filters deleted_at, so a soft-deleted-but-still-active product would remain visible — inconsistent across the three read paths.
recommendation: Pick one model: either implement a soft-delete handler that sets deleted_at (and have catalog/get filter it out) or drop the deleted_at column and the IS-NULL filters and accept hard delete consistently.
verifierNote: Verified against the actual code. handle_delete_product (handlers.rs:312-321) calls crud::crud_delete, which calls db::delete (crud.rs:145). I traced db::delete to wafer_block::db → the postgres delete_async, which uses wafer_sql_utils::query::build_delete_by_id — a real physical DELETE (returns NotFound on 0 rows_affected). So admin delete is genuinely a hard delete. The products table really has a `deleted_at TEXT` column (migrations/001_products_schema.{sqlite,postgres}.sql line 38/35). manage_products (pages.rs:92-96) and my_products (pages.rs:390-394) both filter deleted_at IS NULL, but handle_catalog (handlers.rs:411-435) and handle_get_product_public filter ONLY on status="active" and never touch deleted_at — confirming the three-read-path inconsistency the reviewer described. No code anywhere ever WRITES deleted_at, so the soft-delete model is half-built: the column + two filters exist but nothing populates it, and the only delete path hard-deletes. The line_items table does have product_id (TEXT NOT NULL) so hard-deleting a product logically orphans line items — but note these are plain columns, NOT declared FK REFERENCES constraints, so it is a logical orphan, not a DB-enforced one (reviewer slightly overstated by implying referential integrity). Minor: reviewer's 'pages.rs:390-394' is the my_products user path, not separately labeled, but the content matches. This is a legitimate architecture-smell / dead-code inconsistency, not a correctness or security bug; nothing actively breaks today since deleted_at is never set, so 'low' is the correct severity (not understated). Not an allowed exception under any convention.

title: Monetary totals accumulated in f64 across line items before converting to cents
description: Per-line totals (unit_price * quantity) and the running total_amount are all f64, and each line's total_price is persisted as a float; only the grand total is converted to cents and rounded once at the end. Accumulating money in binary floating point invites representation drift (classic 0.1+0.2). The single end rounding mitigates the common case, but the stored per-line total_price floats and the f64 sum are still a latent rounding-correctness smell for an order-of-money path.
recommendation: Compute and accumulate in integer cents per line (round unit_price to cents first, multiply by quantity as i64, sum as i64) rather than summing floats and converting once. Store line total_price in cents too for consistency with total_cents.

title: Float-equality conditions use f64::EPSILON, which is wrong for non-near-zero comparisons
description: evaluate_condition compares the '==' / '!=' operators with `(a - b).abs() < f64::EPSILON`. f64::EPSILON (~2.2e-16) is the gap near 1.0, so for any field value of meaningful magnitude (e.g. quantity 10, price 100) the tolerance is far too tight and effectively becomes exact bit-equality — meaning '==' rarely matches and '!=' almost always does. For integer-valued pricing variables it happens to work, but the intent (approximate equality) is not what the code does.
recommendation: Either compare with a relative/scaled tolerance, or—since pricing variables are conceptually exact—document that these compare exactly and use plain `==`/`!=`. Add tests covering non-integer condition values.

title: Repo hardcodes Backend::Sqlite while the block's own migration layer is backend-aware
description: Every sql-utils builder call in repo/purchases.rs and repo/subscriptions.rs passes Backend::Sqlite literally (10+ sites), yet the block ships postgres migration files and migrations/mod.rs explicitly selects the backend from SOLOBASE_SHARED__DATABASE__BACKEND. The backend choice changes placeholder syntax (?N vs $N) and upsert/ON-CONFLICT shape, so on a Postgres deployment the migrations would build correct Postgres DDL but these repo statements would emit SQLite-only SQL and fail. This matches the codebase-wide sibling pattern (rate_limit.rs, admin/*, vector/* all hardcode Sqlite), so it is a systemic concern rather than a products-only mistake, but products is the block that most visibly contradicts itself (backend-aware migrations + backend-locked repo).
recommendation: Thread the backend (read once from SOLOBASE_SHARED__DATABASE__BACKEND, the same key the migration layer uses) into the repo functions instead of hardcoding Sqlite — ideally via a shared helper so all blocks resolve backend consistently. At minimum, document the SQLite-only assumption to match the postgres migration's 'validate before enabling Postgres' note.
verifierNote:
Verified real. purchases.rs hardcodes Backend::Sqlite at 7 sites (subscriptions.rs at 8 more = 15 in the products block), and the mechanism is accurate. The Backend enum genuinely changes dialect: render_update/render_select dispatch to sea_query's SqliteQueryBuilder vs PostgresQueryBuilder (lib.rs:90-106), differing in placeholder syntax (? vs $N, per the enum docs at lib.rs:78-85). A client-built Statement carries pre-rendered SQL (Statement.sql) that db::execute/db::query (clients/database.rs:223,246) ship verbatim to the backend service. So on a Postgres deployment the migrations would emit correct Postgres DDL (migrations/mod.rs reads SOLOBASE_SHARED__DATABASE__BACKEND via migration_helper.rs:65) while these repo statements would still emit SQLite-dialect SQL and fail — the self-contradiction the reviewer describes is genuine.

Important asymmetry the finding doesn't state but that bounds the severity: the high-level db helpers used elsewhere in the SAME file (db::get/create/update/count/sum at lines 18,26,48,79,275,etc.) are backend-portable — dialect is resolved SERVER-side from each backend's `const BACKEND` (interfaces/database/exec.rs:26,89). Only the client-built-Statement path (build_update_where/build_select_columns) freezes the dialect. So the bug is confined to the few atomic-CAS/projection helpers that build Statements client-side, not the whole repo.

Severity stays low (reviewer's rating is correct, not understated): this is a dormant/latent issue, not a live bug. Solobase deploys SQLite/D1 only (per project memory), and the products postgres migration file's own header says 'Solobase deploys SQLite/D1 today; ... Validate before enabling Postgres for the products block.' So Postgres is explicitly an un-validated, not-yet-enabled path. It's a codebase-wide systemic pattern (45 Backend::Sqlite sites across 14 files), correctly flagged as architecture-smell. Note there is currently no client-side API to discover the active backend over the db service, so the recommended fix (thread SOLOBASE_SHARED__DATABASE__BACKEND into the repo, as migration_helper already does) is the feasible path. No raw-SQL/sync-bridge/error-string convention is violated here.

title: subscriptions table is created only by migration, never declared in CollectionSchema
description: The block declares CollectionSchema entries for products, groups, types, pricing, purchases, line_items, group/product_templates, and variables, but NOT for the subscriptions table — even though subscriptions is a first-class table this block reads/writes heavily (repo/subscriptions.rs) and the migration creates it. The CollectionSchema list is therefore an incomplete picture of the block's data model (it also omits the provider_payment_intent_id/approved_at/provider_session_id purchase columns and all subscription/addon columns), so any tooling that relies on CollectionSchema (WRAP table-name validation, admin schema views) sees a partial model.
recommendation: Add a CollectionSchema for the subscriptions table (and reconcile the purchases CollectionSchema with the migration columns) so the declared schema matches what the migration creates and the code uses. If CollectionSchema is intentionally advisory-only now that migrations own DDL, document that and stop maintaining a partial list.
verifierNote:
Factually correct on the divergence, but the reviewer overstated the consequence. CONFIRMED: products/mod.rs (lines 57-134) declares CollectionSchema for products/groups/types/pricing/purchases/line_items/group+product_templates/variables but NOT subscriptions, even though subscriptions is a first-class table (repo/subscriptions.rs, const SUBSCRIPTIONS_TABLE) created by migrations/001_products_schema.sqlite.sql lines 163-181 with addon_* columns. The purchases CollectionSchema also omits provider_payment_intent_id and approved_at columns that the migration creates (lines 96-97). (Minor reviewer error: the migration has no provider_session_id column on purchases, so that part of the quoted evidence is off; the rest holds.) The migration's own comments even acknowledge the subscriptions table "was previously absent from CollectionSchema."

WHERE THE REVIEWER IS WRONG: (1) WRAP table-name validation does NOT consult CollectionSchema. WRAP enforces the {org}__{block}__{name} prefix on the actual table name, and access grants use the separate BlockCapabilities.collections grant set (wafer-block/src/capabilities.rs) — a different field entirely from BlockInfo.collections (Vec<CollectionSchema>). I found no WRAP/runtime consumer of BlockInfo.collections in wafer-run. (2) CollectionSchema no longer drives DDL: solobase-core/src/migrations.rs generate_initial_schema is test-only; production blocks own DDL via migrations/*.sql applied through migrations::apply(ctx) at lifecycle(Init) (products/mod.rs:280). The llm block already dropped its .collections(...) declaration with the comment 'schema is no longer materialised implicitly' (llm/mod.rs:359-363).

GENUINE (small) IMPACT: The only live consumers of block.collections are admin UI: admin/pages/blocks.rs:443 (block-detail display) and admin/pages/permissions.rs:244,283 (the specific-resource dropdown in the WRAP grant form). So the subscriptions table is invisible in those admin views and cannot be selected as a narrow specific-grant target — admins must use the wildcard prefix grant or type it manually. That is a convenience/consistency gap, not a security or correctness defect (wildcard grants cover it; enforcement is by prefix). Real low-severity architecture-smell; the reviewer's recommendation (complete the list, or explicitly mark it advisory-only as llm already did) is sound.

title: key_var is never resolved into api_key — every secret-referencing provider fails Unauthorized at chat time
description: schema.rs / config.rs document that secrets live in suppers_ai__admin__variables and providers reference them via `key_var`, with the feature block expected to resolve `key_var` -> plaintext `api_key` before calling `ProviderLlmService::configure`. No code does this. `reload_provider_service` (the ONLY place that populates the shared provider service used by chat dispatch) calls `row_to_config`, which always sets `api_key: None`, then hands those configs straight to `configure()`. `resolve_key` in providers/mod.rs is a self-described no-op that just returns `cfg.api_key` (always None). The result: OpenAI/Anthropic encoders return `EncodeError::MissingApiKey` -> `LlmError::Unauthorized` for every provider whose key is stored the documented way. `config::get(ctx, key)` exists and is async — it is exactly what should be called per-provider here.
recommendation: In `reload_provider_service` (and the duplicated `reload_service` in migrations/legacy_providers.rs), after decoding each enabled config, if `cfg.key_var` is Some, resolve it via `config::get(ctx, key_var)` and set `cfg.api_key`. Then delete the placeholder `resolve_key` no-op or make `provider_snapshot` resolve at request time. Add an integration test that configures an OpenAI provider with only `key_var` set and asserts the encoded request carries the Authorization header.
verifierNote: Verified end-to-end; the finding holds. (1) schema.rs:6-12 documents the contract: secrets live in suppers_ai__admin__variables, referenced via key_var, and "the feature block resolves key_var -> plaintext api_key at runtime before calling ProviderLlmService::configure." (2) row_to_config (schema.rs:122-131) hardcodes api_key: None — tests at schema.rs:220-223 even assert this. (3) resolve_key (providers/mod.rs:227-232) is a self-described no-op that returns cfg.api_key.clone(); its own doc comment says resolution is "currently a no-op placeholder." (4) Both reload paths call configure(configs) with no resolution: reload_provider_service (routes.rs:412-436) and the duplicated legacy reload_service (migrations/legacy_providers.rs:232-251). No with_api_key or config::get(key_var) call exists anywhere in non-test code in the block (grep confirms key_var is only ever read from request bodies and stored back). (5) Consequence confirmed: openai.rs:45 and anthropic.rs:42 return EncodeError::MissingApiKey when resolved_api_key is None, which maps to LlmError::Unauthorized (mod.rs:403, 410). Only OpenAiCompatible tolerates a missing key. So every OpenAI/Anthropic provider — including all auto-migrated legacy ones (legacy_providers.rs:61-74 derives a key_var for them) — fails chat with Unauthorized. discover_models (mod.rs:143) has the same defect. (6) The recommended fix is viable: wafer_core::clients::config::get exists, is async on native, returns Result<String, WaferError> (wafer-run/crates/wafer-core/src/clients/config.rs:20), and the reload functions are already async with a ctx in hand. Severity high is correct: it is a complete, never-implemented correctness break for the documented secret path, gated only behind admins who happen to set an inline api_key (which itself never survives a DB roundtrip since config_to_row drops it). Recommend resolving key_var->api_key in both reload paths and removing/repurposing the resolve_key placeholder.

title: SSE streaming chat handler silently drops assistant-message persistence
description: `handle_chat_stream` forwards chunks to the client but never persists the assistant reply to the messages block (the buffered `handle_chat` does, at line 255). The thread_id, msg, and ctx are explicitly discarded with `let _ =` and an acknowledged TODO. Consequence: any conversation conducted via the streaming endpoint loses all assistant turns from history, so the next request's `messages_list` returns only user turns and the model sees a broken transcript. This is a real data-loss/correctness regression masked as a 'simplification'.
recommendation: Accumulate assistant text inside the producer (it already iterates every chunk) and call `messages_create` after the stream ends. `ctx.clone_arc()` yields an owned `Arc<dyn Context>` that can move into the producer closure (the same pattern other streaming blocks use), and the auth fields needed by `messages_create` can be snapshotted from `msg` before the spawn. This removes the TODO and makes streaming history-consistent with the buffered path.
verifierNote: Confirmed real and accurately described. crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/llm/routes.rs:295-353: handle_chat_stream explicitly discards thread_id/msg/ctx (`let _ =` at lines 310-312) with a TODO(llm-phase-b-task-14) and skips assistant-message persistence; the producer loop (325-348) forwards chunks and closes with `data: [DONE]` but never writes the reply. The buffered handle_chat persists at routes.rs:255 via messages_create. grep confirms routes.rs:255 is the ONLY assistant-persistence site in the whole llm block — no compensating write exists, so the doc comment claiming clients can "fetch the assistant message via the normal messages endpoint" is false (nothing wrote it). Mechanism verified: the shared dispatch_chat persists the USER message (routes.rs:148) and builds the model transcript from messages_list/history_to_messages (150-152, 83-101). So after any streaming exchange, the thread holds only user turns; a subsequent request feeds the model consecutive user messages with no assistant turns — a broken transcript and permanent loss of assistant history. This is a live, reachable path: mod.rs:490-491 wires POST /b/llm/api/chat/stream to handle_chat_stream. The recommendation is sound and feasible: Context::clone_arc() exists (used in storage.rs:320, auth/service.rs:230) and yields an owned Arc<dyn Context> that can move into the from_producer closure; messages_create only needs owned String snapshots of msg's auth meta (auth.user_id/user_email/user_roles, mod.rs:92-103), which can be captured before the spawn. The original commit (06d9cf5, Phase B 14) deferred this citing 'from_producer's spawn boundary can't currently capture &dyn Context' — but clone_arc (wafer-run #46) removed that blocker, so the stated reason is stale. Minor severity nuance: blast radius is limited to clients using the streaming endpoint (the buffered /chat path is correct), and the loss is conversation-quality/history degradation, not a security or auth defect — but it is a clear correctness regression on a live endpoint with no workaround, so high stands at the conservative end.

title: Renaming a provider via PATCH orphans the old name in the in-memory service until next reload of all rows
description: `update_provider` allows changing `name`, which is the backend_id key in both the DB (UNIQUE) and the in-memory HashMap keyed by name. After update it calls `reload_provider_service`, which clears and rebuilds from the full DB scan, so the in-memory map is consistent. However any per-thread setting that stored the old `provider_block`/backend name (settings table) or any in-flight default still pointing at the old name will now resolve to 'unknown backend'. There is no migration of `suppers_ai__llm__settings.provider_block` on rename, so per-thread overrides silently break. Lower severity because reload keeps the service map itself correct.
recommendation: Either disallow renaming once a provider is referenced by thread settings, or cascade the rename to `suppers_ai__llm__settings.provider_block`. At minimum document that `name` is an identity key and renaming breaks existing per-thread overrides.
verifierNote: Confirmed real. `name` is the provider identity key: ProviderConfig.name is documented as "Display name + backend_id key. Must be unique. Used as the ChatRequest::backend_id" (providers/config.rs:51-52); the in-memory map is keyed by name (providers/mod.rs:117 `inner.providers.insert(name, p)`) and chat_stream looks up by req.backend_id, returning `LlmError::InvalidRequest("unknown backend: {id}")` on miss (providers/mod.rs:241-245). update_provider does allow renaming (routes.rs:564-566 `cfg.name = n`). The settings table stores `provider_block` as the provider NAME verbatim with no cascade (mod.rs:294-295, 309), and resolve_provider returns that stored name unchanged (mod.rs:164-173). So after a rename, existing per-thread overrides resolve to a name no longer in the rebuilt map → "unknown backend". The default-provider config var (DEFAULT_PROVIDER_VAR, mod.rs:184) and the settings page (pages.rs:476-491, displays pb verbatim with no validation) have the same un-cascaded reference, slightly broadening the blast radius beyond just thread settings. Correction to the reviewer's wording: the title ("orphans the old name in the in-memory service until next reload of all rows") is imprecise — reload_provider_service (routes.rs:412-435) clears+rebuilds the map immediately and correctly, so the map is consistent right away; the actual bug is the reverse — stale name references in the DB settings table point at a provider that no longer exists in the correctly-rebuilt map. The body description and recommendation capture this correctly. Severity stays low: admin-gated mutation, recoverable (re-pick provider), no data loss, no security impact, pre-production env. Not a convention violation (no raw SQL / block_on / hardcoded-value issue); it's a missing-cascade design gap.

title: Two near-identical SSE producer bodies duplicated across chat-stream and load-model handlers
description:
`handle_chat_stream` (314-353) and `load_model` (726-757) contain the same SSE emission loop: send the text/event-stream meta, loop over a typed stream, JSON-encode each item into a `data: ...

` frame, emit `event: error
data: {}

` on decode/stream error, and finish with `data: [DONE]

`. The frame-building, error-frame literal, and DONE sentinel are copy-pasted byte-for-byte. This is exactly the kind of shared transport that sse.rs already centralizes on the decode side but not on the encode side.
recommendation: Extract a generic `sse_forward<T: Serialize, S: Stream<Item = Result<T, _>>>(stream) -> OutputStream` (or a helper that takes the sink) that does meta-header + per-item JSON framing + error frame + DONE. Both chat-stream and load-model call it. Keeps the SSE wire encoding in one place, mirroring sse.rs on the decode side.
verifierNote:
Confirmed by reading routes.rs. handle_chat_stream (314-353) and load_model (726-757) contain near-identical SSE producer bodies: same send_meta(META_RESP_CONTENT_TYPE, "text/event-stream"), same `event: error
data: {}

` error frame (twice each — once for stream error, once for serde encode error), same `data: ` + json + `

` framing with Vec::with_capacity(json.len() + 8), and same terminal `data: [DONE]

`. The only differences are the bound variable name (chunk vs progress) and the typed item, which are irrelevant to the framing. The reviewer's "mirror sse.rs on the decode side" claim is also accurate: crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/llm/providers/sse.rs centralizes the SSE *parse/decode* transport (SseFrameStream) but there is no encode-side counterpart. The proposed `sse_forward<T: Serialize, S: Stream>` helper is the right root-cause fix and has no async/lifetime obstacle (both closures already `move` the stream and only touch `sink`). However the claimed "medium" severity is overstated — this is pure maintainability duplication of ~25 lines of wire-encoding plumbing with no correctness or security impact; the only downside is the two error-frame/DONE literals could drift independently. Real finding, but low severity.

title: SSE error frames carry an empty body, discarding the actual error from the client
recommendation: Capture the error in the `else`/`Err` arm (it is currently let-else discarded), `tracing::warn!` it, and serialize a small `{"error": "..."}` (or a code) into the SSE error frame so clients can react. The buffered path already surfaces the message via `err_internal`; the streaming path should not be strictly worse.

title: reload_service duplicated between routes.rs and migrations/legacy_providers.rs
description: `reload_service` here is a copy of `routes::reload_provider_service` (list_all -> row_to_config -> filter enabled -> configure), explicitly justified as 'intentionally duplicated to keep the migration self-contained'. Both functions also share the same missing key_var resolution (see the high-severity finding), so the bug must be fixed in two places. Self-containment is not a strong reason to fork data-layer logic that must stay in lockstep.
recommendation: Make `reload_provider_service` take `&ProviderLlmService` (it already takes `&LlmBlock` only to reach `block.provider_svc`) or expose a free function `reload(ctx, svc)` in routes/schema, and call it from the migration. One implementation, one place to fix key_var resolution.
verifierNote:
Verified real. legacy_providers.rs:232-251 reload_service is a genuine fork of routes.rs:412-436 reload_provider_service: both do db::list_all(PROVIDERS_TABLE) -> schema::row_to_config -> filter cfg.enabled -> provider_svc.configure(configs). The only differences are the parameter (the migration takes &ProviderLlmService; reload_provider_service takes &LlmBlock but uses block ONLY at line 434 to reach block.provider_svc) and error handling (log-and-return vs Result). The docstring (lines 230-231) explicitly admits the fork ('intentionally duplicated to keep the migration self-contained'), which is the 'no code smells/no quick fixes' convention violation. Both reload paths also share the same non-resolution of key_var (row_to_config sets api_key:None and leaves key_var; resolve_key in providers/mod.rs:227 is a documented no-op placeholder) — so any future key_var-resolution change would indeed need parallel edits, supporting the reviewer's lockstep concern. The reviewer's recommendation is viable: reload_provider_service can take &ProviderLlmService and be called from both sites (the lifecycle handler has self.provider_svc).

Downgraded medium->low on impact: at the SOLE call site (mod.rs:560-572) the Init lifecycle handler UNCONDITIONALLY calls routes::reload_provider_service(self, ctx) on line 570 immediately after migrate_legacy_providers returns. The migration's internal reload_service call (line 175) is therefore functionally redundant — the routes-path reload re-runs the same logic on the very next line regardless of whether migration ran. So this is a maintainability/cleanliness issue (a forked, largely dead-weight duplicate helper), not a correctness or security risk. The cleanest root-cause fix is arguably to delete reload_service / the line-175 call entirely (the caller already reloads), or to share one implementation. Confidence high: duplication, single call sites, and the redundant double-reload all directly confirmed in source.

title: Every provider CRUD write triggers a full-table scan reload of the provider service
description: `discover_models` calls `reload_provider_service` twice — once before discovery (818) and once after the row update (834) — each doing a `db::list_all(PROVIDERS_TABLE)` full scan plus per-row decode. create/update/delete each also do one full reload. For discover specifically, two full scans bracket a single row mutation. Provider cardinality is small so this is not urgent, but the double reload in discover is gratuitous: the pre-reload exists only so `discover_models` can find the provider by name in the in-memory service, which could instead be configured from the single row already in hand.
recommendation: Drop the pre-discover full reload: the handler already has `cfg` from `db::get`; resolve its key and push just that one provider into the service (or add a `configure_one`), or have `discover_models` accept a `&ProviderConfig` directly instead of looking up by name. Keep the single post-write reload.

title: Buffered chat truncation cap is checked off-by-a-chunk and the appended chunk can exceed the cap
description: The buffered handler checks `content.len() + s.len() > MAX_BUFFERED_RESPONSE_BYTES` and on overflow skips the whole chunk and sets `truncated`. So the persisted/returned content can be up to one full chunk short of the cap, but the bigger issue is the semantics: the first oversized chunk is dropped entirely (not partially appended up to the cap), and the cap is a soft byte budget on `String` length rather than a hard guarantee. It is internally consistent but the comment claims it 'caps' memory while a single very large chunk before the cap is reached is still appended in full.
recommendation: If a hard cap matters, append the byte-prefix that fits (respecting a char boundary) and then mark truncated; otherwise document that the cap is approximate. Minor — only relevant for pathological streams.

title: discover_models stringifies its error with {e:?} producing debug-formatted error text in the API response
description: `block.provider_svc.discover_models(&cfg.name)` returns `Result<_, LlmError>`. The error arm formats it as `format!("{e:?}")` (Debug) and passes that to `err_internal`, leaking Rust enum debug syntax (e.g. `Network("...")`) into the HTTP error cause. Everywhere else in this file LlmError is surfaced via `e.message` / Display. This is the kind of stringly/Debug error handling the #249 refactor aimed to remove.
recommendation: Use the Display form of LlmError (it derives a user-facing message via thiserror in sibling code) — `err_internal("discover_models failed", e)` if LlmError: Display, or map to a clean string. Avoid `{:?}` in user-facing error bodies.

title: ProviderProtocol::parse error messages hardcode the variant token list, duplicating the enum
description: Two handlers build the identical 'expected `open_ai`, `anthropic`, or `open_ai_compatible`' message inline (491-493 and 571-574). The valid-token list is a manual duplicate of the `ProviderProtocol` variants and their `as_str()` values; adding a fourth protocol requires editing the enum plus both message literals, with no compiler help. This is exactly the magic/implicit-duplication the conventions warn against.
recommendation: Add `ProviderProtocol::VARIANTS: &[&str]` (or a `known_tokens()` helper) in config.rs derived from the variants, and build the message from it once. Both call sites and any future variant stay in sync automatically.

title: handle_post_config does a redundant second get_by_field after get_thread_setting already loaded the row
description: The per-thread update path calls `get_thread_setting` (which does `db::get_by_field` and returns the data map) and then, inside the `Some` branch, immediately issues a SECOND `db::get_by_field` for the same thread_id solely to obtain `record.id`. That is two identical queries for one update, plus a race window the code itself acknowledges ('Thread setting vanished between read and update'). `get_thread_setting` discards the record id it already had.
recommendation: Have `get_thread_setting` return the full `Record` (id + data) instead of just `data`, or inline a single `get_by_field` here and reuse `record.id`. Eliminates the duplicate query and the read-then-read race.

title: resolve_provider builds provider_block via an or_else that always returns an empty String, then re-checks emptiness
description: `provider_block` is computed with a trailing `.unwrap_or_else(|| String::new())` whose only job is to produce an empty string that is then re-tested at line 187 (`if provider_block.is_empty()`). The empty-string sentinel + later re-check is a convoluted way to express 'fall back to default'. The `unwrap_or_default()` for model is cleaner; provider should mirror it. The comment 'Will be filled below from config' admits the closure is a placeholder.
recommendation: Compute an `Option<String>` for the resolved override (thread override, then req_provider) and finish with `override.filter(|s| !s.is_empty()).unwrap_or(default_provider)`. Drops the placeholder closure and the empty-string round-trip.

title: default-target internal endpoint relies on caller_id presence for access control with no positive auth
description: The `/b/llm/api/internal/default-target` route is gated solely by `ctx.caller_id().is_none()` -> 404, on the documented assumption that the shared pipeline strips caller_id for external HTTP. This couples a security boundary to a pipeline-stripping invariant that lives in another crate; if that stripping ever regresses, this leaks the configured default provider/model to any authenticated user (the endpoint is checked before the user_id guard, and returns config values). It is also the only endpoint using this pattern, so it is easy to overlook when the pipeline contract changes.
recommendation: Document this as a hard dependency on the pipeline stripping caller_id for external requests, and add a runtime assertion/test that exercises the external path returns 404. Longer term, prefer an explicit internal-only dispatch capability over relying on caller_id absence as the auth signal.
verifierNote:
Verified against actual code. The quoted dispatch exists at crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/llm/mod.rs:460-464 (comment 456-459) and means what the reviewer says: the /b/llm/api/internal/default-target route is gated solely by `ctx.caller_id().is_none() -> err_not_found`, and this check precedes the `user_id.is_empty()` guard (line 468). handle_default_target (lines 231-244) returns the configured default provider block + model id.

The reviewer's central assumption is CORRECT and the invariant is currently robust, not fragile in practice: RuntimeContext.caller_id is Option<String>; the single top-level constructor make_context (wafer-run/crates/wafer-run/src/runtime.rs:449) hardcodes `caller_id: None` for ALL top-level entrypoints (HTTP listener, lifecycle, flow executor, and run_block at runner.rs:109). The only site that sets it to Some(...) is call_block (wafer-run/crates/wafer-run/src/context.rs:377), where it is `self.node_id` — the calling block's resolved name, never derived from the Message or any HTTP header (Message has no `caller` field). So an external HTTP request structurally cannot present a non-None caller_id; the legitimate consumer is vector/ingestion.rs:185 via ctx.call_block("suppers-ai/llm", ...). I confirmed this is the only endpoint in solobase-core using caller_id-gated dispatch.

This is correctly classified as architecture-smell / low: it is NOT a present bug — the reviewer explicitly frames it as a hazard 'if stripping ever regresses' (defense-in-depth + a cross-crate invariant that lives in another crate). Two corrections to the framing: (1) the exposed data is non-secret block config (the provider BLOCK NAME like suppers-ai/provider-llm and a model id, no API keys/credentials — those live in provider records), so even a hypothetical regression would be a low-impact info disclosure, not credential leak; (2) the runtime invariant is genuinely solid today (single make_context constructor, no request-controlled path to caller_id), so the real residual gap is purely the missing regression test. I confirmed NO test currently exercises the external-path 404 (no test references default-target/handle_default_target/with_caller for this route). No project convention is violated (no raw SQL, no block_on, no string-coerced errors — uses err_not_found/ok_json helpers). Severity stays low; the actionable item is the recommended regression test plus documenting the cross-crate dependency.

title: history_to_messages silently coerces unknown roles and missing content to empty user turns
description: `role_from_str` maps any unknown role (including 'tool') to `User`, and `history_to_messages` defaults missing content to "" while keeping the turn. Combined, a stored tool message or a role-less-but-content-bearing entry becomes an empty or mis-roled user turn fed to the model. The buffered/streaming paths persist assistant text fine, but stored tool results (if any block ever writes them) would be silently corrupted into user turns rather than dropped. The behavior is tested and intentional, but the silent coercion of 'tool' -> 'user' is a latent correctness hazard if tool-calling history is ever persisted through the messages block.
recommendation: Either drop entries with unrecognized roles (filter_map already returns Option) instead of coercing, or explicitly handle 'tool' with its tool_call_id once tool history is persisted. At minimum, document that this block does not round-trip tool turns.

title: Model-picker composite "{backend_id}:{model_id}" value is never decoded on the remote chat path — wrong model id sent to backend
description: render_model_picker emits each remote option with value="{backend_id}:{model_id}" (a single composite string). On submit, llm-chat.js reads picker.value and — for anything NOT prefixed `local:` — passes it verbatim as body.model (handleRemoteChat, llm-chat.js:218 + :285). dispatch_chat (routes.rs:137-145, 168-170) then puts that string directly into ChatRequest.model WITHOUT splitting off the `backend_id:` prefix; backend_id is resolved independently from provider_block. So selecting a concrete remote model from the picker sends model id "openai:gpt-4o" (composite) to the backend instead of "gpt-4o". The composite is decoded NOWHERE on the remote path (verified: the only `:` split anywhere is the JS `local:` sentinel slice(6)). The picker doc-comment claims this 'stays compatible with the single `model` field', but the field's consumer treats backend and model as separate values.
recommendation: Stop conflating two identifiers into one delimited string. Either (a) emit the bare model_id as the option value and carry backend_id separately (e.g. a data-backend-id attribute the JS reads and the chat body sends as a distinct `provider`/`backend_id` field), or (b) if a single field must be kept, split the composite back into backend_id + model_id in dispatch_chat before building ChatRequest. Whatever shape is chosen, define the encode/decode in ONE shared place so producer and consumer can't drift — this is exactly the 'no magic/implicit mapping; same representation everywhere' rule.
verifierNote: Confirmed end-to-end. render_model_picker (pages.rs:596-599) emits value="{backend_id}:{model_id}" for every real option; backend_id is always non-empty because providers seed cached_models via ModelInfo::new(&p.name, id, id) (providers/mod.rs:113), so the value is always the composite, never a bare model_id. In llm-chat.js, handleChatSubmit reads picker.value (line 205-206); only values prefixed "local:" are special-cased (line 209, slice(6) at 215) — everything else goes to handleRemoteChat (line 218), which sets body.model = composite verbatim (line 284-285). Server-side, dispatch_chat (routes.rs:137-156) deserializes body.model and passes it to resolve_provider, which returns it verbatim as final_model (mod.rs:175-199 — no ':' split), then puts it directly into ChatRequest.model (routes.rs:168-170). backend_id is resolved independently from provider_block via resolve_backend_id (routes.rs:161), NOT from the composite. Providers send model: &req.model verbatim to the upstream HTTP API (openai.rs:109, anthropic.rs:96). I grep-verified the only ':' decode anywhere is the JS local: sentinel; nothing splits the composite on the remote path. So picking a concrete remote model sends "openai:gpt-4o" to OpenAI instead of "gpt-4o", and the upstream rejects it as an unknown model. This is both a correctness bug and a clear violation of the project's 'no magic/implicit mapping; same representation everywhere' rule — the doc-comment's claim that the composite 'stays compatible with the single model field' is false since the field's consumer treats backend and model separately. The reviewer's recommendation (carry backend_id as a distinct data-attribute/field, or define encode/decode in one shared place) is sound. Downgraded from high to medium: real and user-facing, but blast radius is limited to the explicit model-picker path (the value="" 'Default (remote)' option is unaffected since body.model isn't set when empty), there is no security/data-loss aspect, and the failure mode is a clean upstream 'unknown model' error rather than silent wrong behavior.

title: models_page does a redundant serialize→buffer→re-decode roundtrip instead of calling the typed client (inconsistent with pages.rs)
description: models_page calls routes::list_models (which calls wafer_core::clients::llm::list_models, then re-serializes the typed Vec<ModelInfo> into a JSON envelope via ok_json and wraps it in an OutputStream), then collect_buffered()s that stream and serde_json::from_slice's it back into an untyped serde_json::Value, finally as_array()ing it. pages.rs::load_models (pages.rs:557-569) does the right thing — it calls wafer_core::clients::llm::list_models(ctx) directly and works with typed ModelInfo. So two handlers in the same block fetch the identical data two different ways, and the ui.rs path pays for an extra encode + buffer-allocation + decode and discards all type information (everything downstream becomes stringly-typed JSON access in model_row).
recommendation: Call wafer_core::clients::llm::list_models(ctx) directly (as pages.rs already does) and render model_row from the typed ModelInfo instead of serde_json::Value. This removes the encode/buffer/decode roundtrip and lets model_row use real fields (.backend_id, .model_id, .capabilities.streaming) instead of stringly-typed .get(...).and_then(as_str/as_bool).unwrap_or(...).
verifierNote: Confirmed real against the code. ui.rs:373-389 (models_page) calls routes::list_models, which at routes.rs:670-679 calls the typed llm_client::list_models(ctx) (= wafer_core::clients::llm::list_models, returning Vec<ModelInfo>) and re-serializes it via ok_json(json!({"models": models})) wrapped in an OutputStream. models_page then collect_buffered()s that stream, serde_json::from_slice's the bytes back into an untyped serde_json::Value, and .get("models").as_array()s it (lines 380-389). model_row (453-547) then does stringly-typed .get().and_then(as_str/as_bool).unwrap_or() access throughout. So the redundant typed->serialize->buffer->decode->untyped roundtrip is genuine, and ModelInfo is a real struct (wafer-block/src/wire/llm.rs:252) with backend_id/model_id/display_name/capabilities, so the recommendation to call the client directly and render from ModelInfo is feasible. Two corrections to the reviewer: (1) pages.rs::load_models (557-569) is NOT a fully-typed exemplar — it calls the typed client but immediately serde_json::to_value's each ModelInfo back into serde_json::Value (line 567), and render_model_picker is also stringly-typed; the real difference is only the extra stream encode/buffer/decode hop, which load_models avoids. (2) Severity is overstated as medium: this is an admin-only page-render path gated by is_admin, invoked once per page load (not a hot path), with no correctness or security impact — purely an efficiency/type-safety smell. Genuine root-cause-fix item under project conventions, but low severity.

title: DEFAULT_PROVIDER_VAR / DEFAULT_MODEL_VAR / DEFAULT_PROVIDER duplicated in pages.rs, shadowing the canonical mod.rs constants
description: pages.rs re-declares the same three config constants that already exist in mod.rs (mod.rs:44-46), where DEFAULT_PROVIDER is even exported as `pub(super)` specifically for sibling-module reuse. Having the SUPPERS_AI__LLM__DEFAULT_PROVIDER / SUPPERS_AI__LLM__DEFAULT_MODEL var names and the suppers-ai/provider-llm default literal copy-pasted into two files means a rename in one place silently diverges from the other — and the settings page would then display/read a different var name than the chat dispatch actually uses. This is the 'same value, same place / no magic duplication' rule.
recommendation: Delete the three pages.rs copies and import the canonical ones from the parent module (`use super::{DEFAULT_PROVIDER_VAR, DEFAULT_MODEL_VAR, DEFAULT_PROVIDER};`). Make the two var-name consts `pub(super)` in mod.rs alongside the already-exported DEFAULT_PROVIDER so the single source of truth is shared.
verifierNote: Confirmed by reading both files. pages.rs:28-30 declares `DEFAULT_PROVIDER_VAR = "SUPPERS_AI__LLM__DEFAULT_PROVIDER"`, `DEFAULT_MODEL_VAR = "SUPPERS_AI__LLM__DEFAULT_MODEL"`, `DEFAULT_PROVIDER = "suppers-ai/provider-llm"` — byte-identical copies of mod.rs:44-46. The duplication is real and self-inflicted: mod.rs already exports `DEFAULT_PROVIDER` as `pub(super)` and routes.rs:29 already imports it from the parent (`use super::{LlmBlock, DEFAULT_PROVIDER}`), so the single-source-of-truth/sibling-import pattern is established and the recommended fix (import all three from `super`, promoting the two var consts to `pub(super)`) is sound and idiomatic. The values are also load-bearing in two distinct paths: mod.rs's ConfigVar declarations (mod.rs:124-131, the block's declared config_keys) and chat dispatch (mod.rs:184/232/248) reference the mod.rs copies, while the settings page reads/displays via the pages.rs copies (pages.rs:165,402-403,434,448) — a rename in one file would silently desync the UI from the declared ConfigVar and dispatch. This matches the project's 'no magic duplication / single source of truth' rule, so it is a genuine finding. Severity downgraded from medium to low: it is pure duplication within one block module with currently-identical values, so nothing is broken at runtime; the reviewer's 'settings page reads a different var than dispatch' claim is a hypothetical future-divergence risk, not a present defect. Not an allowed exception (these are domain-specific config var names + a {org}/{block} provider literal, exactly the kind of value the convention says must live in one place).

title: render_messages_pane takes an `_entries` parameter it never uses (dead parameter + dead wiring)
description: render_messages_pane's first parameter is `_entries: &[db::Record]` and the body never references it — messages are rendered entirely client-side from the JSON bootstrap carrier. render_page_body still threads `entries` into this call (pages.rs:78) purely to satisfy the signature, alongside separately building messages_json from the same entries (pages.rs:65-75). The argument and its plumbing are dead and misleading: a reader expects the pane to render entries server-side.
recommendation: Drop the unused parameter — make it `fn render_messages_pane(thread_id: Option<&str>)` and update the single caller. If server-side pre-render of entries was ever intended, implement it; otherwise remove the dead plumbing so the client-side-only contract is explicit.

title: render_model_picker carries a dead legacy `id` fallback for a field that no longer exists on ModelInfo
description: render_model_picker accepts `model_id` OR a legacy `id` key. The only production caller (load_models, pages.rs:565-568) serializes a typed wafer_core ModelInfo, which has exactly one field `model_id` (service.rs:441) — no `id` field and no #[serde(alias)]. So the `.or_else(|| m.get("id"))` branch can never fire in production; it only exists to satisfy a 'pre-Task-16 shape' test fixture. Carrying untyped-JSON back-compat for a typed source that can't produce the legacy shape is dead defensive code.
recommendation: Render the picker from typed ModelInfo (same as the models_page fix) so model_id is a real field — then the legacy-`id` branch and its dedicated test disappear. If the function must stay JSON-shaped for now, drop the `.or_else(|| m.get("id"))` and the legacy-id test case, since no live producer emits `id`.

title: Only external CDN dependency in the SSR layer — marked@14 loaded from jsdelivr while all other JS is self-hosted via assets module
description: render_page_body emits <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/marked@14/marked.min.js">. This is the ONLY external CDN reference in solobase-core's SSR (verified repo-wide); every other asset — htmx, the llm-chat module, the files browser, toast, CSS, fonts — is self-hosted and content-hash-versioned through crate::ui::assets (htmx_js_url(), llm_chat_js_url(), etc.). The CDN bypasses that convention and introduces an external availability/supply-chain dependency and a CSP-tightening blocker. The comment acknowledges it as deferred, but it remains a live inconsistency in the reviewed file.
recommendation: Vendor marked.js into the assets module the same way llm-chat.js / htmx are handled (a `marked_js()` + `marked_js_url()` pair with content-hash versioning) and reference it via the asset URL. Removes the external runtime dependency and keeps the self-hosted-assets convention uniform.
verifierNote: Verified real. crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/llm/pages.rs:90-92 emits `script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/marked@14/marked.min.js" {}` with a comment marking it deferred. Repo-wide grep confirms this is the ONLY external CDN/script-src in solobase-core SSR; the other https hits are OAuth API endpoints (auth_ui/oauth/spec.rs googleapis token/userinfo), not asset loads. The reviewer's convention claim holds: every other JS/CSS/font/image is self-hosted and content-hash-versioned through crate::ui::assets (htmx_js_url, llm_chat_js_url, files_browser_js_url via short_hash/OnceLock) and served by the system block via the /b/static/{name}-{hash}.ext dispatch table in blocks/system.rs:63-104 with immutable cache headers. So the recommended fix (add marked.min.js to assets/, an include_str!, a marked_js()/marked_js_url() pair, plus one table entry) is exactly the established pattern. Not an allowed exception (the project exceptions concern raw SQL and block_on, not CDN scripts). Kept at medium rather than down-graded to low because, beyond the availability angle (which llm-chat.js already degrades gracefully on via `typeof marked !== 'undefined'`), the script tag carries no SRI integrity attribute and pins only a major version (marked@14), so it executes unverified third-party JS in an authenticated admin/chat context — a genuine supply-chain/CSP-tightening concern. Not a correctness bug and not stringly-typed-error-related; purely the flagged architecture/supply-chain smell.

title: Defence-in-depth admin re-check is applied inconsistently across the block's UI handlers
description: ui.rs::providers_page and ui.rs::models_page both re-check `if !helpers::is_admin(msg) { return forbidden }` internally (ui.rs:86-88, 362-364) with a doc-comment calling it 'defence in depth ... for the case where a caller reaches this function directly'. But the two sibling page handlers in pages.rs (settings_page at pages.rs:397, which mod.rs declares UiRoute::admin, and page at pages.rs:113) do NOT carry the same in-handler guard — they rely solely on the dispatcher in mod.rs:473-477. So the stated defence-in-depth contract is half-applied within the same block: an equally 'admin' settings page is unguarded at the function level while providers/models are guarded. Either the in-handler guard is the contract (then settings_page must have it too) or it isn't (then it's redundant on providers/models).
recommendation: Pick one policy and apply it uniformly to all admin-gated LLM page handlers. If defence-in-depth is the rule, add the same is_admin guard to pages.rs::settings_page; if the dispatcher guard in mod.rs is considered sufficient, drop the redundant in-handler checks from providers_page/models_page and document the single enforcement point.

title: model_row builds status/load/unload URLs by raw string interpolation of provider-supplied ids without URL-encoding
description: model_row constructs /b/llm/api/models/{backend_id}/{model_id}/status (and /load, /unload) via plain format! over ids that come from provider 'discover-models' responses. A helpers::url_path_encode exists precisely for 'constructing <a href> URLs from caller-supplied data' (helpers.rs:116-127, noting maud does NOT URL-encode). The extract_model_path round-trip (routes.rs:645-664) documents a hard assumption that ids contain no `/`, which holds for the remote backends rendered here, so the practical blast radius is small — but the ids are still external data interpolated into a path with no encoding, so any future backend whose model_id contains `/`, `?`, `#`, or a space silently produces a wrong or broken request URL.
recommendation: Wrap backend_id/model_id in helpers::url_path_encode when building these URLs (and decode correspondingly in extract_model_path), or document/enforce the no-`/` id contract at the data boundary. Encoding is the safer root-cause fix and matches how other handlers build href URLs from external keys.
verifierNote: Confirmed. ui.rs:485-487 builds status/load/unload URLs via raw `format!("/b/llm/api/models/{backend_id}/{model_id}/...")` with no URL-encoding. backend_id/model_id come from provider-supplied JSON (`model.get("backend_id")`/`get("model_id")` from the aggregated list_models service response) — external data. `url_path_encode` does exist and its doc explicitly says to use it "when constructing <a href> URLs from caller-supplied data ... maud's HTML escaping does NOT URL-encode"; it's already used this way in files/pages_user.rs and products/stripe.rs. The round-trip handler extract_model_path (routes.rs:645-664) documents the unenforced assumption that ids contain no `/` and relies on splitn(3,'/'), so the no-`/` contract is real and unguarded. CORRECTIONS to the reviewer: (1) the helper lives at crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/helpers.rs:121, not an llm-local helpers.rs:116-127 (no such file exists) — substance unchanged. (2) Not a security issue: these are admin-only authenticated endpoints and the ids round-trip back to the same server (htmx hx-get/hx-post), not injected into a trusted external context — so no XSS/SSRF. It is a latent correctness/robustness gap, low severity today because every currently-rendered remote backend's model ids (e.g. `gpt-4`, `claude-3-5-sonnet`) contain none of `/ ? # space`. Worth noting: a proper fix requires symmetric encode (ui.rs) + decode (extract_model_path), since simply percent-encoding the href without decoding on the receiving side would break the round-trip — the reviewer correctly flagged this coupling. Legitimate finding under the project's root-cause/no-code-smell convention, but genuinely low impact.

title: Files block has no repo/ data layer
description: Sibling blocks use a repo/ module; files has none, scattering queries across pages_user.rs (1659-line god-file), storage.rs, cloud.rs, quota.rs and duplicating query construction.
recommendation: Add files/repo modules owning TABLE consts and db calls. Related low-sev: mod.rs:119-246 hand-rolled router duplicates endpoints + storage.rs extractors; load_quota_info pages_user.rs:825-845 sums via list_all not db::sum; VIEWS_TABLE orphan const mod.rs:14-17; sweep_stale_pending runs per-upload storage.rs:390.
verifierNote:
VERIFIED REAL but severity inflated to "high"; downgraded to medium (pure architecture/consistency smell, no correctness/security risk).

CORE CLAIM HOLDS: Sibling blocks auth (crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/auth/repo/, 14 submodules) and products (.../products/repo/) have a repo/ data layer. files/ has none. The convention is explicitly documented in products/repo/mod.rs: the repo submodule "owns its table name(s) (the canonical repo-module-owns-its-TABLE convention) and is the sole place that issues db::* / wafer_sql_utils statements against those tables." files violates this: ~58 db/sql-utils call sites are scattered across pages_user.rs (1659-line god-file, 23 sites), storage.rs (14), cloud.rs (7), pages_admin.rs (8), quota.rs (4), share.rs (2) — the queries live in the SSR-page and API-handler layers, not a data layer.

DUPLICATION CLAIM HOLDS: the created_by ownership filter is genuinely re-implemented: user_owns_bucket (pages_user.rs:465-489), is_bucket_access_denied (storage.rs:81-108) — both `name==bucket AND created_by==user_id` against BUCKETS_TABLE — plus the `created_by==user_id` list filters in list_buckets_for_user (pages_user.rs:128) and handle_list_buckets (storage.rs:182). load_quota_info (pages_user.rs:825-845) re-sums via list_all in-process while quota.rs:44-60 already has get_user_usage using db::sum/db::count — a real, fixable duplication.

BUT SEVERAL SECONDARY CLAIMS ARE WRONG / OVERSTATED:
- NO raw SQL anywhere in files/ (grep exec_raw/query_raw = empty). All queries use db::* helpers + wafer_sql_utils builders correctly. So the most severe convention (raw SQL ban) is NOT violated. No sync bridges, no string-coerced errors either. This is organization-only, no runtime risk → not "high".
- "VIEWS_TABLE orphan const mod.rs:14-17" is FALSE: mod.rs:14-17 carries an explicit comment documenting why it lives there (no dedicated owner module; only schema decl + single insert site storage.rs:348/record_view). quota.rs and share.rs DO own their TABLE consts, so the table-naming convention is mostly followed.
- "mod.rs hand-rolled router duplicates endpoints" is essentially INVALID: every solobase block (products, auth_ui, legalpages, llm, vector, messages, userportal) dispatches via the same hand-rolled path-prefix matching in handle(). BlockEndpoint declarations are OpenAPI metadata, not a router; not files-specific.
- "sweep_stale_pending runs per-upload storage.rs:390" (actually :394) is by-design: quota.rs:100-109 documents it as intentional best-effort self-healing (single indexed delete_by_filters) to avoid a cron. Not a smell.

Net: a legitimate medium refactor (add files/repo/ owning BUCKETS/OBJECTS/quota queries, dedupe the ownership filter and the in-process sum). Not high — block is correct and convention-compliant on the load-bearing rules (SQL utils, errors, async).

title: Admin/quota lookups do not coerce SQLite TEXT integers, rendering 0
description: RecordExt i64_field is plain as_i64; pages_admin and quota.rs use it for size/access_count/max fields while SQLite returns them as strings, so they collapse to 0/default though user pages coerce correctly.
recommendation: Make i64_field fall back to as_str parse and use it everywhere. The fallback closure is also copy-pasted ~6x in pages_user; quota defaults are re-spelled vs QuotaConfig::default (quota.rs:27-37).
verifierNote: Confirmed real. RecordExt::i64_field (crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/helpers.rs:44-46) is `self.data.get(key).and_then(|v| v.as_i64()).unwrap_or(0)` with NO string fallback; serde_json's as_i64() returns None for Value::String, so a TEXT-encoded integer collapses to 0. pages_admin.rs uses it at line 448 (access_count) and 554-556 (max_storage_bytes/max_file_size_bytes/max_files_per_bucket); quota.rs:get_user_quota uses plain as_i64().unwrap_or(default) for the three byte/count fields (lines 23-37) and i64_field for reset_period_days (line 38), again with no string fallback. The decisive proof the DB actually returns these columns as strings is the developer's OWN user-facing code: pages_user.rs applies the explicit fallback `v.as_i64().or_else(|| v.as_str().and_then(|s| s.parse().ok()))` in four spots for the SAME logical fields — size (525, 836), access_count (783), max_storage_bytes (814). So the admin/quota paths are an inconsistency, not deliberate design. Correcting the reviewer's framing on impact/severity: admin Quotas/Shares tables are read-only display (cosmetic 0/blank rendering, humanized via format_bytes). For get_user_quota the byte fields degrade to QuotaConfig defaults (1GB/100MB/10k), not 0, so check_quota would silently ignore an admin override and apply the default cap rather than producing a 0-byte lockout — wrong-but-bounded, no data loss or security bypass. Hence medium, not high. The recommendation is correct and matches project conventions: make i64_field fall back to as_str().parse() and use it consistently (kills the ~6x copy-pasted closure in pages_user), and reuse QuotaConfig::default (models.rs:11-20) constants instead of re-spelling 1_073_741_824/104_857_600/10_000 in quota.rs and pages_user.rs:817-822.

title: Download serves user content_type inline without nosniff/attachment; stored-XSS
description: Upload stores caller content_type verbatim; download serves it with no Content-Disposition or nosniff, so an uploaded HTML/SVG renders inline in the authenticated origin (stored XSS). Share path share.rs:171 uses inline disposition.
recommendation: Add nosniff and Content-Disposition attachment on downloads and share-direct. Also: cloud.rs:76-81 handle_create_share omits the SEC-064 backslash check is_valid_storage_key enforces; call the shared validators.
verifierNote: Partially real but the finding materially misstates the facts and overstates severity. CONFIRMED: storage.rs:381-388 stores the caller-supplied content_type verbatim (no allowlist), and both the download (storage.rs:353 `ResponseBuilder::new().body(data, &info.content_type)`) and share (share.rs:171-180, `Content-Disposition: inline`) paths serve it back with no `attachment` disposition. REFUTED — the central "no nosniff" premise is false. `wafer-run/security-headers` is the FIRST step of the top-level `site-main` flow (crates/solobase-core/src/flows/site_main.rs:15) that ALL requests traverse, and it sets `resp.header.X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, `X-Frame-Options: DENY`, and a restrictive CSP (`default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; ...; frame-ancestors 'none'`) — see wafer-block-security-headers/src/lib.rs:89,99-111 and DEFAULT_CSP at lib.rs:20. The flow executor (wafer-run/.../waferflow/executor.rs:198-201, 301-307) merges these via `Message::set_meta` (types.rs:1184, upsert-by-key), so the headers set by the middleware survive onto the files block's terminal response (the inner `ctx.call_block` response meta is added to, not replacing, the outer message's `resp.*`). Net effect: downloads/shares DO carry nosniff + CSP. nosniff blocks the MIME-sniffing variant (octet-stream sniffed to HTML). A residual stored-XSS vector remains for an explicitly-uploaded `text/html`/`image/svg+xml` served inline, because the global CSP allows `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'` — inline scripts in an uploaded HTML doc would execute in-origin. So defense-in-depth (Content-Disposition: attachment + per-download nosniff, or a content_type allowlist) is still worthwhile, but this is not the unmitigated high-severity stored-XSS the finding describes; given the global nosniff+CSP it is low. SECONDARY claim CONFIRMED and real: cloud.rs handle_create_share (lines 76-81) inline-validates `..`, leading `/`, and `\0` but NOT backslash, whereas the shared is_valid_storage_key (storage.rs:117-123) rejects `\\` per SEC-064 — a genuine but minor validator inconsistency; calling the shared validator would close it.

title: Block reaches around its own runtime backend to count rows in the backend's private _meta table
description: map_index_row computes `{storage_name}_meta` and calls `db::count` directly against it to get the per-index vector count. That `_meta` table is a private storage detail owned by the `wafer-run/vector` runtime backend (declared in wafer-sql-utils::vector::VectorIndexSchema, created/dropped/queried only by that backend). The proper, backend-agnostic way to count an index is `vclient::count`, which the JSON `stats()` route (pages.rs:427) already uses via the VECTOR_COUNT service op. So the block has two different counting paths: the UI/service path bypasses the backend and hardcodes its `_meta` table-name convention, while the API path goes through the service. This couples the feature block to the runtime backend's storage layout — if the backend ever renames `_meta` or changes how rows map to vectors (e.g. soft-deletes, the `rowid` autoinc scheme), the UI count silently diverges from the real count, and the `audit-allow` pragma on line 141 is papering over exactly this boundary crossing.
recommendation: Route the count through `wafer_core::clients::vector::count(ctx, &storage_name)` like `stats()` does, so the UI and API agree and the block stops depending on the backend's `_meta` naming. map_index_row would become async-over-vclient instead of async-over-db; the `audit-allow` pragma and the table-name convention duplication then disappear.
verifierNote: Verified and upheld as a real architecture-smell. In crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/vector/service.rs:140-142, map_index_row hardcodes `{storage_name}_meta` and calls db::count directly to get the per-index vector count, guarded by an audit-allow pragma (line 141) that labels the table "own-block".
