That label is wrong: the `_meta` table is owned by the vector runtime backend's schema, defined in wafer-run/crates/wafer-sql-utils/src/vector.rs (struct VectorIndexSchema, `meta_table: format!("{ident}_meta")` at line 47), and created/queried/dropped only by wafer-block-sqlite's vector service (wafer-run/crates/wafer-block-sqlite/src/vector.rs: create_index via build_create_vec_and_meta, count() via build_count_meta at line 379-389). The solobase block dispatches every other vector op (create_index/upsert/delete/query/count) across the block boundary via vclient (wafer-core::clients::vector, alias `wafer-run/vector`), and the JSON stats() route at pages.rs:427 already uses vclient::count. So the reviewer is correct that two counting paths exist (UI/service path reaching around the backend vs API path going through the service op) and can diverge: the backend's count() also checks index_exists and uses the canonical schema-builder name, neither of which the block's db::count path does. The reviewer's recommendation (route map_index_row through vclient::count) is sound and trivial. Minor corrections to the finding: (1) the proper count goes through wafer_core::clients::vector::count, not a literal `wafer_core::clients::vector::count(ctx, &storage_name)` against a "wafer-run/vector backend" that owns VectorIndexSchema — the schema lives in wafer-sql-utils and the impl in wafer-block-sqlite, behind the `wafer-run/vector` alias; (2) the cited "SqliteVecService" name in doc-comments (pages.rs:248, :595) is stale — no such type exists. Severity downgraded from high to medium: this is db::count (a sql-utils builder), NOT raw SQL, so it does not breach the no-raw-SQL rule; and there is no live correctness bug today (discover/registry paths only surface existing tables, so the count is currently accurate). It is a latent coupling + a mislabeled audit-allow pragma that violates the "no magic/implicit mapping, fix at root cause" convention — worth fixing, but not high. Note: the same `{storage_name}_meta` convention is also duplicated at pages_ui.rs:266 for introspect_columns, which has weaker justification (no vclient introspection op exists).

title: Hand-written json_extract raw SQL in the ingest handler bypasses sql-utils
description: The re-ingest cleanup builds and executes a fully hand-written SQL string (`SELECT id FROM {prefixed}_meta WHERE json_extract(metadata, '$.document_id') = ?1`) via `db::query_raw` inside a normal request handler. This is not one of the allowed raw-SQL exceptions (admin SQL explorer / migration runner / test fixtures). It also (a) interpolates `{prefixed}_meta` — again reaching into the backend-owned `_meta` table — and (b) reads back the chunk ids only to feed them to `vclient::delete`. The metadata shape (`$.document_id`) is written by this same handler (line 783-787) but read by raw SQL, so the JSON layout is an implicit contract split across a typed write and an untyped query. wafer-sql-utils owns the vector schema (VectorIndexSchema) and already has builders for meta-table selects/deletes; a `select id where metadata json_extract` builder belongs there, or the delete-by-document_id should be a backend service op exposed through vclient.
recommendation: Add a builder to wafer-sql-utils::vector (e.g. `VectorIndexSchema::build_select_ids_by_metadata_field`) that emits the json_extract query, or expose a `delete_by_document_id` op on the wafer-run/vector backend and call it via vclient. Either removes the inline raw SQL and the `_meta` table-name assumption from the block.
verifierNote: Confirmed real. pages.rs:697-715 builds a fully hand-written SQL string ("SELECT id FROM {prefixed}_meta WHERE json_extract(metadata, '$.document_id') = ?1") and runs it via db::query_raw inside the normal `ingest` request handler — not the admin SQL explorer, a migration runner, or a test fixture, so it is not an allowed raw-SQL exception under either solobase/CLAUDE.md or workspace/CLAUDE.md. Both sub-claims verified: (a) {prefixed}_meta is a backend-owned, runtime-named table — the block's own migration file (001_vector_schema.sqlite.sql) states the per-index `_meta`/`_fts`/vec0 tables are NOT created here and "their DDL lives in the upstream wafer-run/vector runtime block"; the block here reaches into it directly. (b) The `$.document_id` JSON shape is written by the typed vclient::upsert path (pages.rs:783-787) but read by raw SQL, splitting the JSON layout across a typed write and an untyped query. The recommendation is architecturally sound, not invented: the wire protocol already models `MetadataFilter { equals: BTreeMap<String,Value> }` (wafer-block/src/wire/vector.rs:84) and vclient::query already accepts it, so a metadata-filtered delete op on the backend (or routing this through the typed client) is feasible; the DeleteRequest currently only takes `ids`, which is exactly why the round-trip exists. The author's inline justification ("a dialect-portable builder is neither needed nor possible — the query stays raw") does not satisfy the convention: sql-utils already emits dialect-specific SQL (e.g. introspect::build_table_info with Backend::Sqlite), so a json_extract builder is buildable, and the cleaner fix is the backend op since the block doesn't even own the table. Note this query carries NO audit-allow pragma, unlike the genuinely-blessed own-block count at service.rs:141; and unlike the introspect_columns raw query at service.rs:223 (which at least uses a sql-utils builder for the SQL), this one is 100% hand-built. Not a security/correctness bug — index name is validated to [A-Za-z0-9_] at the route boundary and document_id is a bound param, so no injection — so medium (maintainability/convention debt) is correct, not high.

title: Hand-written sqlite_master existence probe in load_index_metadata
description: The fallback path for pre-registry indexes runs a hand-written `SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name = ?1` via db::query_raw to detect whether the `_fts` table exists. This is raw SQL in a handler, not an allowed exception, and the comment itself admits the gap: 'wafer-sql-utils::introspect doesn't expose a table-exists single-name probe; adding one is out of scope for this PR.' Per CLAUDE.md, a missing builder should be ADDED to wafer-sql-utils, not worked around with raw SQL. There is already `introspect::build_list_tables_like` (used by discover_indexes), so the introspect module is the right home for a `build_table_exists(name, backend)` companion.
recommendation: Add `introspect::build_table_exists(name: &str, backend) -> (String, Vec<Value>)` to wafer-sql-utils and call it here (and reuse it for any other single-table probes in the codebase). Keeps the FTS-presence inference but removes the inline string and makes it backend-portable.
verifierNote: Verified real. The quoted code exists verbatim at crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/vector/pages.rs:603-609 inside load_index_metadata, a normal block handler (not the admin SQL explorer, a migration runner, or test-fixture setup), so it is not an allowed exception. It is a hand-written, parameterized `SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name = ?1` passed through db::query_raw to detect the per-index `_fts` table — exactly the "missing builder worked around with raw SQL" pattern that solobase/CLAUDE.md and workspace/CLAUDE.md forbid. The inline comment itself admits the gap ("introspect doesn't expose a 'table exists' single-name probe; adding one is out of scope for this PR"), which is the deferral the rule explicitly disallows. I confirmed in the pinned wafer-run checkout (crates/wafer-sql-utils/src/introspect.rs) that no build_table_exists exists — only build_list_tables, build_list_tables_like, build_table_info, build_table_row_count — and that the sibling discover_indexes (line 259-260) already routes through introspect::build_list_tables_like, so introspect is the correct home for the proposed builder. The reviewer's recommendation is sound and follows the existing dual-backend pattern (Sqlite: sqlite_master, Postgres: information_schema.tables). One correction strengthening the finding: the comment's "SQLite-only by design, no dialect-portable equivalent" rationale is weak here — a single-table existence check IS trivially portable (unlike the genuinely SQLite-specific json_extract raw query at line 697, which is more defensible). Severity is medium but at the low end: it is a pure convention/portability/maintainability issue with no correctness or injection risk (query is parameterized, table-name goes through ?1 binding), and the block is SQLite-only at runtime today. Not a security or behavioral bug.

title: Same registry column parsed two different ways by two readers
description: map_index_row (service.rs) reads `dimensions` and `keyword_search` with a number-OR-string dual path (`v.as_u64().or_else(|| v.as_str().and_then(parse))`), justified by a comment that 'the SQLite service stores all auto-created columns as TEXT'. But load_index_metadata (pages.rs:587) reads the same `keyword_search` column with `.as_i64()` only, and create_index writes it via a typed upsert::build_upsert Statement as a real i64 (pages.rs:200) into a migration-declared `INTEGER` column. So three sites disagree about the column's runtime type: one writer (typed int), one reader assuming int, one reader defensively accepting both int and string. Either the value really can come back as a string (in which case load_index_metadata has a latent bug and will read keyword_search=false for string '1'), or it can't (in which case map_index_row's string fallback is dead defensive code). The two readers must agree.
recommendation: Determine the actual round-trip type of a migration-declared INTEGER column read via db::list_sorted vs db::query, then unify both readers on one parsing strategy (and a shared helper). If TEXT round-trip is real, fix load_index_metadata to use the same number-or-string path; if it isn't, drop the string fallback in map_index_row. Don't leave two divergent assumptions about one column.
verifierNote:
Confirmed real, but downgraded from medium to low (consistency/code-smell, not a live bug). The quoted code exists verbatim: map_index_row (service.rs:119-135) reads `dimensions`/`keyword_search` with a number-OR-string dual path; load_index_metadata (pages.rs:584-589) reads `keyword_search` with `.as_i64()` only; create_index (pages.rs:192-206) writes `keyword_search` as `serde_json::json!(cfg.keyword_search as i64)` (a JSON number) into the migration-declared `INTEGER NOT NULL` column (migrations/001_vector_schema.sqlite.sql:25). So three sites carry inconsistent assumptions about one column — a genuine violation of the "no magic / keep things explicit, one representation everywhere" convention.

Two corrections to the reviewer's framing: (1) The reviewer suggests the two readers might diverge depending on db::list_sorted vs db::query. They cannot — both return `Record { data: HashMap<String, serde_json::Value> }` from the SAME backend service and SAME row->JSON conversion (wire/database.rs:287/308). The column's runtime JSON type is identical for both; the divergence lives only in the reader code's assumptions. (2) The justifying comment ("the SQLite service stores all auto-created columns as TEXT") is misapplied: the native SQLite service (wafer-block-sqlite/service.rs:64-68) maps a real INTEGER column via `ValueRef::Integer -> serde_json::Value::Number`, and the TEXT-as-string behavior only applies to lazily ALTER-added columns (`build_add_text_column`, service.rs:136/196). Since `dimensions`/`keyword_search` are migration-declared INTEGER and never lazily auto-created, they round-trip as JSON numbers on the native backend — so `.as_i64()` works and the string fallback in map_index_row is dead defensive code there. (D1's WASM-host round-trip wasn't locatable in these repos, leaving a small residual unknown, but both readers would see whatever D1 returns identically.)

Net: no confirmed correctness bug on the native backend (both readers happen to succeed; only the fallback is dead), but the two readers genuinely encode contradictory type assumptions justified by an inaccurate comment. Worth unifying on one parsing helper, which is exactly the reviewer's recommendation. Hence real but low severity.

title: Two parallel index-enumeration mechanisms (sqlite_master scan vs registry list)
description: The block enumerates indexes two different ways for two different surfaces: the JSON API (`list_indexes`, `stats`) scans `sqlite_master` for `{prefix}%_meta` tables via discover_indexes, while the admin UI (`list_index_rows` in service.rs) lists rows from the registry table. The header comment explains the split (sqlite_master so pre-registry indexes still surface), but the result is that the JSON `indexes` list and the UI table can show different sets of indexes (a registry row with a dropped `_meta` table appears in the UI with count 0 but not in the API; a `_meta` table with no registry row appears in the API but not the UI). Existence-of-index has no single source of truth. This also means name-prefixing/stripping logic is duplicated (discover_indexes strips prefix+`_meta`; map_index_row/display_index_name strip prefix).
recommendation: Pick one source of truth for index existence. If pre-registry indexes are a real concern, run a one-time backfill migration into the registry and make the registry authoritative everywhere (UI and API both read it); then discover_indexes and its sqlite_master scan can be deleted. This collapses the two enumeration paths and the duplicated prefix/suffix stripping into one.
verifierNote: Verified against the actual code. The two enumeration paths are real and accurately described: (1) the JSON API surfaces `GET /b/vector/api/indexes` (list_indexes → discover_indexes) and `GET /b/vector/api/stats` (stats → discover_indexes) both scan sqlite_master via introspect::build_list_tables_like then strip TABLE_PREFIX + `_meta` in Rust (pages.rs:255-274); (2) the admin UI (`GET /b/vector/`, render_index_list_fragment) reads the registry table via service::list_index_rows (service.rs:163), mapping rows with map_index_row and stripping only TABLE_PREFIX via display_index_name (service.rs:54). So existence-of-index has two sources and the prefix/suffix stripping is genuinely duplicated — the finding's factual claims hold up. The divergence scenarios the reviewer cites are correct in principle (a registry row whose _meta table was dropped would show in the UI with count 0 but not the API; an orphan _meta table with no registry row would show in the API but not the UI). However I downgrade severity from medium to low: (a) the split is intentional and explicitly documented at pages.rs:247-254 — sqlite_master is scanned so pre-registry indexes still surface, while the registry is the documented source of truth for per-index *metadata* (model, keyword_search), not existence; (b) create_index (pages.rs:178-209) and delete_index (pages.rs:280-306) keep both in sync, and registry-write failure is surfaced as an error rather than swallowed, so steady-state divergence requires a partial-failure or a legacy pre-#200 index; (c) the registry came from a single migration file (001, ported in PR #200) and the project is pre-production with a wipeable prod DB (per memory), so the 'pre-registry index' justification is largely theoretical — which is exactly why the recommended backfill-and-consolidate is reasonable but is a refactor, not a bug fix. No correctness or security impact. Note: discover_indexes uses db::query_raw, but this is the established introspection execution pattern (mirrors service.rs:218 introspect_columns, documented as the only path short of a typed introspect API) — it goes through an introspect::* builder, so it is NOT a separate raw-SQL convention violation, and the reviewer did not claim it was. The finding is a valid, low-severity architecture-smell observation.

title: Stale doc comments claim routes are Unimplemented / pending tasks
description: The handle() comment states 'Task 15 fills in the indexes CRUD routes; remaining routes (upsert, query, ingest, embed, stats, delete-vector) still resolve to Unimplemented in pages::route.' This is false — pages.rs::route (lines 71-84) implements every one of those routes. Likewise pages.rs:16 ('Task 19 implements ingest and embed') and the route() docstring at pages.rs:62-65 ('Anything that does not match a handled route resolves to Unimplemented, which lets the block compile and be registered while the remaining handlers land in future tasks') describe a scaffolding state that no longer exists. Stale comments mislead future readers into thinking the block is half-built.
recommendation: Delete the Task 15 / Task 19 scaffolding references and update route()'s docstring to describe the real behavior (Unimplemented is now only the genuine fallthrough for unknown paths, not a placeholder for pending work).

title: Global index namespace with no per-tenant scoping despite 'for this project' docs
description: The module header says `GET /b/vector/api/indexes` lists indexes 'for this project', and all API endpoints are declared AuthLevel::Authenticated (mod.rs:50-76), but there is no project/org/user scoping anywhere: index names map to a flat global `suppers_ai__vector__{name}` namespace, discover_indexes returns every index in the database, and any authenticated user can create/delete/query/ingest/embed against any index by name. So any logged-in user can read another user's vectors, overwrite their chunks (ingest is keyed only on document_id), or delete their index. The `user_id` is read in mod.rs:93 purely to reject anonymous callers, then never used for authorization. Either the endpoints should be Admin-only (matching the UI), or the index namespace must be scoped by org/user.
recommendation: Decide the tenancy model explicitly: either scope index names by the caller's org/user (prefix with org id and filter discover_indexes/list by it) and enforce ownership on delete/query/ingest, or downgrade the API endpoints to AuthLevel::Admin to match the admin-only UI. Fix the 'for this project' comment to match reality.
verifierNote: Factually accurate. Verified: prefixed_index_name (service.rs:48-50) builds a flat global `suppers_ai__vector__{name}` namespace with no org/user component; discover_indexes (pages.rs:255-274) scans sqlite_master for ALL tables with that prefix, no per-caller filter; every API endpoint is AuthLevel::Authenticated (mod.rs:50-76), which the enum doc defines as "Any logged-in user is allowed" (wafer-block/types.rs:64); user_id is read once in mod.rs:93 solely to reject anonymous callers and is never threaded into create_index/delete_index/upsert/query/ingest(document_id-only key)/embed/delete_single/stats. So any authenticated user can list/read/overwrite/delete any other user's vectors by name. The "for this project" header (pages.rs:5) and the Admin-only UI (mod.rs:44-49) vs Authenticated JSON API mismatch are also real. HOWEVER, severity is overstated as medium: (1) there is NO org/tenant concept anywhere in Message or Context (only user_id exists as a scoping signal — no org accessor in wafer-block/types.rs), so solobase has no tenancy model to scope against at this layer; (2) the sibling `messages` block is identical — AuthLevel::Authenticated, admin-only UI, and it also does NOT scope its data by user_id in its repo/handlers (user_id only read in mod.rs for the anon check). Shared-across-all-authenticated-users data is thus an established codebase pattern, not a vector-specific defect. This is a legitimate design/tenancy observation and a doc-vs-reality (and API-vs-UI auth-level) inconsistency, but not a clear convention violation given the codebase has no per-tenant model. The reviewer's recommendation (either scope by org/user, or downgrade API to Admin to match the UI, and fix the comment) is sound; the cleanest minimal fix is aligning the comment and the API auth level. Downgraded medium→low; confidence medium because cross-user exposure is real in a multi-user deployment but is a pervasive architectural choice rather than an isolated bug.

title: metric config is accepted and forwarded but never persisted in the registry
description: create_index accepts a `metric` (DistanceMetric) on the body, defaults it to Cosine, and passes it to vclient::create_index, but the registry upsert only writes prefixed_name/model/dimensions/keyword_search (and the migration only declares those columns). So the chosen metric is write-only to the runtime backend and is unrecoverable afterward: the detail page can't show it, and load_index_metadata can't return it for the query path (query() never reads or re-supplies a metric, relying on the backend to remember it). This is an asymmetry — three of the four create-time knobs are remembered in the registry and one is silently dropped — and the docstring on REGISTRY_TABLE (service.rs:33-43) lists the metric's siblings as the reason the registry exists ('remember per-index knobs').
recommendation: Either persist `metric` in the registry (add a `metric TEXT` column to the migration and write/read it, surfacing it on the detail page) or, if the backend is the authoritative store for metric, document explicitly that the registry intentionally does not duplicate it and remove the implication from the REGISTRY_TABLE doc that it remembers all per-index knobs.

title: Convoluted, redundant keyword_search checkbox parsing in create_index
description: The keyword_search field parse is a redundant double-check: a `matches!(..., Bool(true) | String(_))` guard ANDed with an `is_some_and` that re-evaluates as_bool() and matches 'on'|'true'|'1'|'yes'. The left side of the && is almost entirely subsumed by the right (a String('off') passes the left matches! but is correctly rejected by the right; a Bool(true) passes both). The whole thing collapses to just the right-hand is_some_and clause. As written it's hard to verify correct and duplicates the string-truthiness logic that likely already exists for other checkbox fields in the codebase.
recommendation: Replace with a single shared helper, e.g. `helpers::truthy_form_value(parsed.get("keyword_search"))`, that handles Bool / 'on'/'true'/'1'/'yes' string forms in one place, and reuse it across the checkbox-parsing handlers rather than re-deriving the logic inline.

title: render_index_list_fragment downgrades WaferError to String
description: render_index_list_fragment returns Result<Markup, String> and stringifies the underlying WaferError via `.map_err(|e| e.to_string())`. The sole caller (create_index, pages.rs:215-217) then wraps that String back through err_internal. After the #249 refactor that replaced RuntimeError string-coercion with explicit WaferError variants, returning String here re-introduces a stringly-typed error edge: the structured WaferError (code, meta) is flattened to a message before it reaches err_internal, so the original ErrorCode is lost.
recommendation: Change the return type to Result<Markup, WaferError> and let the `?` propagate the structured error; the caller already feeds it into err_internal which accepts any Display. Keeps the error typed end-to-end consistent with the #249 direction.

title: Branding settings page hardcodes a parallel copy of shared config-var metadata (already drifted)
description: `portal_settings_keys()` hardcodes name/help/default/input_type for 6 SOLOBASE_SHARED__* variables (APP_NAME, LOGO_URL, LOGO_ICON_URL, AUTH_LOGO_URL, FAVICON_URL, PRIMARY_COLOR). These vars are already the single source of truth in `config_vars.rs::shared_config_vars()`, which carries the same name/description/default/input_type and is what the admin variables page renders from. The CLAUDE.md rule is explicit: 'Shared vars are defined centrally in solobase-core/src/config_vars.rs. No hardcoded lists.' This duplicate has already drifted: FAVICON_URL's default is "" here but `crate::ui::assets::favicon_url()` (a content-hashed URL) in config_vars.rs line 90 — so the branding page shows a wrong/empty default. The descriptions also differ (e.g. 'URL of the favicon for browser tabs.' vs 'Browser tab icon').
recommendation: Delete `portal_settings_keys()` and drive the branding page off `crate::config_vars::shared_config_vars()`, filtering to the branding subset (e.g. by an explicit key allow-list of &str only, reusing each ConfigVar's name/description/default/input_type). The 'color' rendering branch should switch on `InputType::Color` from the central definition rather than a hardcoded "color" string. This removes the drift class entirely.
verifierNote: Verified real. `portal_settings_keys()` (crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/userportal/mod.rs:649-700) hardcodes a parallel 6-entry table of name/help/default/input_type for the SOLOBASE_SHARED__ branding vars (APP_NAME, LOGO_URL, LOGO_ICON_URL, AUTH_LOGO_URL, FAVICON_URL, PRIMARY_COLOR). These are already the single source of truth in config_vars.rs::shared_config_vars() (lines 17-93), which solobase/CLAUDE.md explicitly designates ("Shared vars are defined centrally... No hardcoded lists") and which the admin variables/settings pages render from (admin/pages/variables.rs:162, admin/settings.rs:459). Drift is confirmed in multiple places: (1) FAVICON_URL default is "" here (mod.rs:689) but config_vars.rs:90 uses crate::ui::assets::favicon_url(), which I confirmed returns a content-hashed URL `/b/static/favicon-{hash}.ico` (assets.rs:64-67) and is never empty — so the branding page shows a wrong/empty placeholder default. (2) Descriptions differ (e.g. "URL of the favicon for browser tabs." vs "Browser tab icon"; "Display name shown in headers, emails, and login pages." vs "Display name shown in UI and emails"). (3) input_type is stringly-typed here ("text"/"color", matched via `*input_type == "color"` at line 723) whereas config_vars.rs uses the typed InputType enum; notably the four URL vars are InputType::Url (validated-on-write) centrally but flattened to "text" here, dropping URL validation semantics on this page. Recommendation is valid: InputType::Color exists (wafer-block/src/types.rs:688), and there are exactly two consumers of portal_settings_keys() (render at line 707, save at line 777), both in this file, so driving the branding page off a filtered shared_config_vars() subset is contained. Downgraded from high to medium: genuine convention violation with an actual user-visible wrong-default bug, but blast radius is a single admin-only branding page placeholder, not a security/data-integrity issue.

title: Duplicate admin UI for shared branding vars overlaps the admin block's variables page
description: `admin_settings_page` + `handle_save_settings` implement a second admin surface for editing SOLOBASE_SHARED__* branding vars (writing via `config::set`). The admin block already owns config-var editing: `admin/pages/variables.rs` renders all `shared_config_vars()` for edit (lines 162/237) and `admin/settings.rs` seeds/persists them with URL validation (`validate_url_value`). The userportal copy bypasses that validation (e.g. it accepts any string for the *_URL keys) and duplicates the save flow, so the two surfaces can diverge in behaviour for the same underlying keys. A feature block re-implementing platform settings management is a boundary leak.
recommendation: Either remove the userportal branding page and point its admin_url at the admin block's variables/branding view, or have it delegate to the admin settings handler (so URL validation and seed-hash handling are shared). At minimum the *_URL writes here must go through the same validation as admin/settings.rs::validate_url_value so the two paths can't accept different inputs for the same key.
verifierNote: Confirmed in code. userportal/mod.rs:702-783 implements a second admin-only "Branding Settings" page (admin_settings_page + handle_save_settings) that edits six SOLOBASE_SHARED__* keys (APP_NAME, LOGO_URL, LOGO_ICON_URL, AUTH_LOGO_URL, FAVICON_URL, PRIMARY_COLOR) by calling config::set directly. config_vars.rs:15-138 declares these exact keys as the single-source-of-truth platform shared vars — four (LOGO_URL, LOGO_ICON_URL, AUTH_LOGO_URL, FAVICON_URL) as InputType::Url — and admin/pages/variables.rs:162/237 renders shared_config_vars() for admin editing. The validation divergence is real and verified: admin/settings.rs::handle_set (lines 326-331) routes _URL keys through validate_url_value (blocks SSRF/private-IP, header-injection newlines, enforces HTTPS); config::set (wafer-core clients/config.rs:33) is a plain RPC with NO validation, so the userportal path accepts arbitrary strings for the same _URL keys. portal_settings_keys() even mislabels these as input_type "text", contradicting the canonical InputType::Url. This is a genuine architecture-smell + validation-bypass and violates the project's "single source of truth in config_vars.rs / blocks should NOT re-declare SOLOBASE_SHARED__ vars" and "no magic/implicit mapping" conventions. Two caveats that keep this at medium (not high): (1) both surfaces are admin-gated — userportal checks helpers::is_admin at mod.rs:121 — so this is not an unauthenticated SSRF vector, it requires an authenticated admin; (2) several other feature blocks (products, legalpages, email, auth_ui) also call config::set on shared keys, so "feature block touches shared config" is a broader existing pattern — the specific defect here is the duplicate branding UI re-editing admin-owned _URL keys without the SSRF validation, not config::set usage per se. Recommendation stands: route the userportal _URL writes through the same validate_url_value (or delegate to / remove in favor of the admin variables page) so the two paths cannot accept different inputs for the same key.

title: Settings save reports success even when every config write fails
description: `handle_save_settings` discards each `config::set` result with `let _ = ...` and unconditionally returns `{"message": "Settings saved"}`. If persistence fails (WRAP denial, db error), the admin sees a success toast while nothing was saved. This is error-swallowing of a write path, and it post-dates the #249 push toward explicit error handling.
recommendation: Collect failures and return an error payload (`{"error": ...}`) when any `config::set` fails, e.g. accumulate failed keys and surface them so the JS toast renders the error branch it already supports (`type: d.error ? 'error' : 'success'`).
verifierNote:
Code confirmed at userportal/mod.rs:777-782: the loop does `let _ = config::set(ctx, key, value).await;` and unconditionally returns `{"message": "Settings saved"}`. config::set returns Result<(), WaferError> (wafer-core/src/clients/config.rs:33), and the page JS (line 750) renders type based on `d.error`, so a failed write does surface as a success toast. So the core mechanism is real — error swallowing on a write path.

However, the reviewer's framing is wrong on two material points: (1) The claim it "post-dates the #249 push toward explicit error handling" is false. This `let _ =` was introduced in #56 (2026-05-03), a MONTH before #249 (2026-06-01, commit 6e0f244). And #249 touched only builder.rs — it replaced RuntimeError String-coercion in SolobaseBuilder::build with explicit Config/Wasm variants; it had nothing to do with settings handlers or UI error surfacing. (2) The recommended fix (collect failures and return `{"error": ...}` to the toast) matches NO existing block. The two handlers the reviewer's framing implies are correct — products/pages.rs:671 and legalpages/pages.rs:884 — only `tracing::warn!` on failure and STILL return `{"message":"Settings saved"}` unconditionally; they do not surface to the UI either. Two other handlers (admin/pages/email.rs:126, auth_ui/pages/settings.rs:224) use the same `let _ =` swallow as userportal.

So this is a codebase-wide consistency/observability gap (5 settings handlers, 3 swallow silently, 2 log a warning), not a unique correctness hazard in this file and not a #249-regression. The convention-matching fix is to add a `tracing::warn!` like products/legalpages, not to invent UI error surfacing. Real but low severity (observability/consistency), not medium; config writes to the block's own SOLOBASE_SHARED__* vars rarely fail in practice.

title: Button CRUD handlers pass `e.message`, dropping the structured WaferError context the helper expects
description: `err_internal<E: Display>` logs the underlying error via Display. `handle_update_profile` (line 326-330) deliberately passes the whole `WaferError` and even documents why: 'Pass the full WaferError (code + meta + message) so the helper logs structured info instead of just the rendered string.' But `handle_create_button`, `handle_update_button`, and `handle_delete_button` pass `e.message`, which strips the ErrorCode (WaferError's Display is `{code:?}: {message}`) and all meta. This is exactly the stringly-typed-error regression the #249 refactor targeted, applied inconsistently within the same file.
recommendation: Pass the full error `e` (not `e.message`) to `err_internal` in all three button handlers, matching `handle_update_profile` so the logged record includes the ErrorCode and meta.
verifierNote:
Confirmed in code. userportal/mod.rs lines 510, 628, 638 pass `e.message` to `err_internal`, while `handle_update_profile` (line 329) passes the full `e` with an explicit comment about logging structured info. `err_internal<E: Display>` (helpers.rs:301) consumes the arg ONLY via `tracing::error!(error = %error)` for a server-side log; it never echoes it to the client (client always gets the sanitized "Internal server error (ref: <id>)"). WaferError's Display (core_types.rs:147) is `write!("{:?}: {}", self.code, self.message)`, so passing `e` adds the ErrorCode discriminant (e.g. `NotFound:`) to the log line vs passing the bare message string. So the finding's core claim is real and the recommendation is correct.

Two reviewer overstatements I must correct: (1) The claim that passing `e` preserves `meta` is FALSE — WaferError's Display does not format `meta` at all, so meta is dropped in BOTH cases; only the ErrorCode is the actual delta. (2) The framing as a file-local inconsistency "applied inconsistently within the same file" understates the scope but also overstates the userportal-specificity: `e.message` appears at 16 call sites across 4 files (userportal/mod.rs x3, admin/pages/users.rs x5, admin/pages/variables.rs x2, llm/routes.rs x5), against ~206 sites that correctly pass full `e`. So this is a broader minor inconsistency, not a userportal-only regression.

Real impact is purely server-side log richness (losing the ErrorCode tag in the diagnostic line) — no correctness, security, or client-facing effect, since the client output is identical either way. That is a low-severity convention/observability nit, not the medium the reviewer assigned. Fix is trivial and aligns with the dominant pattern.

title: Declared `info().endpoints(...)` list is stale relative to the actual routes
description: The BlockInfo endpoint list advertises only GET/POST `/admin/buttons` plus the user-facing routes, but the handler also serves GET/POST `/admin/settings`, GET `/admin/buttons/:id/edit`, PUT `/admin/buttons/:id`, DELETE `/admin/buttons/:id`, and `/internal/list-buttons` (lines 137, 217-243). The advertised contract (used for docs/admin discovery) under-reports the real surface — a maintainability/accuracy smell, and the admin_url at line 92 points at /admin/settings which isn't in the endpoints vector.
recommendation: Add the missing endpoints (admin settings GET/POST, button edit/update/delete) to the `.endpoints(...)` list, or derive the list from the routing match so the two can't drift.

title: Likely-dead public /config endpoint with a fragile catch-all route
description: `handle_config` is exposed at `/b/userportal/config` with NO auth level (line 87) and is also the fallback for ANY message whose path does not start with `/b/userportal` (lines 110-112). No in-repo consumer references `/b/userportal/config` (searched solobase, solobase-demo, solobase-site) — it looks like a leftover SPA-bootstrap endpoint from the pre-SSR (Preact) era. The catch-all is also fragile: a cross-block `call_block` with an unexpected path silently returns the public config JSON instead of a not-found error, masking routing bugs.
recommendation: Confirm with an external-frontend grep whether anything still GETs /b/userportal/config; if not, remove `handle_config`, the `/config` route, and the catch-all (replace the non-prefix branch with `err_not_found`). If the endpoint must stay, replace the silent catch-all with an explicit not-found and keep only the explicit `/config` route.

title: Duplicated form-parsing + validation between create and update button handlers
description: `handle_create_button` and `handle_update_button` are near-identical: same label/path/icon/sort_order extraction, same trim, same default 'package', same empty-field validation, same json_map build. The only differences are stamp_created vs stamp_updated and create vs update. Copy-paste of a parsed-form struct invites divergence (e.g. a future validation rule added to one but not the other).
recommendation: Extract a single `parse_button_form(&body) -> Result<ButtonFields, OutputStream>` (or a small struct + validate fn) and call it from both handlers; the handlers then only differ in stamp + create/update.

title: DOM-id safety check applied only in the edit form, not where IDs are first emitted
description: `is_safe_dom_id` (SEC-058) guards the inline-JS interpolation in `handle_edit_button_form`, but `render_buttons_table` interpolates `btn.id` into `id=(format!("edit-modal-{}", btn.id))` and several hx-* attributes without that guard. Maud escapes attribute values so there's no XSS today, and IDs are server-generated UUIDv7, so this is not an active bug — but the defensive check living in only one of the two id-interpolation sites is an inconsistent pattern that a reader can't reason about locally.
recommendation: Make the invariant explicit in one place: either validate the id once at the table-render boundary, or document that IDs are guaranteed UUIDv7 at creation so the per-site defensive checks are unnecessary. Avoid having one path defend and a sibling path silently trust.

title: `handle_list_buttons` reads sort_order via raw map access instead of the RecordExt accessor used elsewhere
description: The cross-block list-buttons action reads label/icon/path via `r.str_field(...)` but reaches into `r.data.get("sort_order")` directly and serializes the raw JSON value (could be Null), while every other read site uses `r.i64_field("sort_order")`. Mixing the typed accessor with raw map access for the same field on the same record is inconsistent and lets a null/absent sort_order leak into the JSON contract as `null` instead of a number.
recommendation: Use `serde_json::json!(r.i64_field("sort_order"))` for consistency with the rest of the block and to guarantee a numeric value in the cross-block contract.

title: /a2a JSON-RPC endpoint is fully unauthenticated despite comments claiming otherwise
description: The pipeline routes POST /a2a into the messages block BEFORE any auth gate (pipeline.rs:106 dispatches before the empty-user_id check in mod.rs:230), and MessagesBlock::handle returns to a2a::handle_a2a at mod.rs:225-227 before that check. The a2a method handlers (handle_send_message, handle_get_task, handle_list_tasks, handle_cancel_task) contain ZERO auth/role checks. So any unauthenticated caller can create contexts+entries (unbounded write via SendMessage), enumerate every task (ListTasks), read any task by id (GetTask), and cancel any task by id (CancelTask). The mod.rs comment 'protocol-public (auth handled by the JSON-RPC method handlers themselves)' (mod.rs:222-224) and the a2a.rs comment 'Auth is enforced by the JSON-RPC method handlers themselves' (mod.rs:201-202) are factually wrong — no handler enforces anything.
recommendation: Either (a) enforce authentication in handle_a2a (check msg.user_id()/role before dispatch, returning a JSON-RPC error if absent), making the comments true; or (b) if A2A is intentionally public, delete the false 'auth enforced by handlers' comments and document the public surface explicitly, and add scoping so ListTasks/GetTask/CancelTask cannot read or mutate tasks the caller doesn't own. The Message struct is currently passed as `_msg` (unused) into handle_a2a, so the auth metadata is available but discarded — wire it in.
verifierNote:
VERIFIED REAL. Every claim in the finding holds against the actual code.

1) Pipeline bypass: pipeline.rs:106-107 intercepts `if msg.path() == "/a2a"` and `return ctx.call_block("suppers-ai/messages", msg, input)` BEFORE the normal `route_to_block` call (line 118). The only route-tier auth enforcement (`check_access` -> `RouteAccess::Authenticated if msg.user_id().is_empty()` in routing.rs:221-231) lives inside `route_to_block`, so `/a2a` never goes through it. JWT/API-key extraction at pipeline.rs:87-100 only POPULATES auth meta if a valid token is present; absence is not rejected. No feature gate guards line 106 either (the "feature-gated in Phase 0b PR-2" text is a future-intent comment, not active code).

2) Block-level bypass: MessagesBlock::handle (mod.rs:225-227) routes `/a2a` to `a2a::handle_a2a` BEFORE the `if user_id.is_empty() { return forbidden }` gate at mod.rs:230. The `/a2a` BlockEndpoint (mod.rs:203-206) has NO `.auth(AuthLevel::...)` whereas every other messages endpoint has `.auth(AuthLevel::Authenticated)`.

3) Handler-level: handle_a2a (a2a.rs:39) takes the Message as `_msg` and discards it. All four method handlers (handle_send_message a2a.rs:133, handle_get_task :199, handle_list_tasks :207, handle_cancel_task :242) contain zero auth/role/ownership checks. The service layer confirms no implicit scoping: list_contexts (service.rs:77) filters only by type/status/sender_id/parent_id and handle_list_tasks passes sender_id:None; get_context/update_context/delete_context operate by raw id with no owner check.

Net effect, exactly as the reviewer states: any unauthenticated caller can POST /a2a to (a) create unbounded contexts+entries via SendMessage, (b) enumerate all task contexts via ListTasks, (c) read any task+full message history by id via GetTask, (d) mutate/cancel any task by id via CancelTask. The mod.rs:222-224 and mod.rs:201-202 comments ("auth handled/enforced by the JSON-RPC method handlers themselves") are factually false — no handler enforces anything. Comment introduced in d795abc 2026-05-18.

Severity high is appropriate: unauthenticated cross-tenant read/enumerate/write/mutate on a production-reachable endpoint with no compensating control. Both remediation options the reviewer gives are valid; option (a) is the minimal correct fix (gate on msg.user_id()/role in handle_a2a, returning a JSON-RPC -32600/auth error). Note that even option (a) alone does not add per-user ownership scoping — an authenticated user could still read/cancel another user's tasks since contexts aren't owner-filtered — so the scoping concern the reviewer raises under option (b) applies regardless of which path is taken if multi-tenant isolation is required.

title: Service functions downgrade WaferError to String, erasing ErrorCode and forcing wrong HTTP status (NotFound/PermissionDenied become 500)
description: The db client returns WaferError (carrying ErrorCode) for every op, but create_context, list_contexts, delete_context, add_entry, and list_entries all collapse it to a String via `format!("Database error: {e}")`. The #249 refactor was specifically to replace RuntimeError string-coercion with explicit variants — this is exactly the stringly-typed error pattern it removed, still present here. Concrete consequence: the REST handlers for these (rest.rs list_contexts/create_context/delete_context/list_entries/add_entry) can only call err_internal (HTTP 500) because the String has no code, so a NotFound or PermissionDenied from WRAP surfaces as 500. Compare with get_context/update_context/get_entry/delete_entry which keep WaferError and correctly map NotFound -> 404 in rest.rs. The result is inconsistent status codes for the same class of failure across sibling endpoints.
recommendation: Make every service function return Result<_, WaferError> (drop the format! map_err). REST handlers then uniformly match on e.code for NotFound/PermissionDenied/etc. and fall through to err_internal only for genuine Internal errors. This also removes the leaky 'Database error: {e}' prefix and the err_internal call that re-wraps an already-formatted String. a2a.rs callers currently do `.map_err(|e| (-32000, e))` on the String returns — switch those to format the WaferError at the a2a boundary instead.
verifierNote: Verified real and grounded in the actual code. service.rs lines 41/80/124/153/199 (create_context, list_contexts, delete_context, add_entry, list_entries) return Result<_, String> via `format!("Database error: {e}")`, discarding the WaferError ErrorCode; sibling functions get_context/update_context (64/111) and get_entry/delete_entry (184/237) keep WaferError. The end-to-end consequence chain is confirmed: db::* returns WaferError carrying ErrorCode (wafer-core clients/database.rs); WRAP denials produce ErrorCode::PermissionDenied (wafer-block/src/wrap.rs check_access); wafer-block-http-listener/src/lib.rs error_code_to_http_status maps NotFound->404, PermissionDenied->403, Internal->500; helpers.rs err_internal hardcodes ErrorCode::Internal->500. In rest.rs the String-returning handlers (lines 62/97/138/160/203) can ONLY call err_internal, so a NotFound/PermissionDenied surfaces as 500, whereas get/update/delete_entry correctly match e.code==NotFound -> err_not_found (404) at lines 108/126/214/226. The asymmetry is within one file and vector/service.rs uniformly returns WaferError, so WaferError-preserving is the established convention; this is a genuine convention/correctness defect. Two corrections to the reviewer's framing: (1) the #249 commit (6e0f244) touched only builder.rs and concerned RuntimeError's removed From<String> impls — it did not introduce or target this file, so this is a pre-existing inconsistency rather than a #249 leftover; the finding nonetheless holds under the project rule to flag remaining stringly-typed error handling. (2) Severity downgraded high->medium: the headline PermissionDenied->500 case is largely a misconfiguration path (a block has WRAP access to its own {org}__{block}__* tables, and the only declared cross-block grants are llm READ), so it is rarely hit per-request; the realistic runtime miss is NotFound->500 on delete_context/add_entry against a non-existent context_id, plus a2a.rs re-formatting an already-formatted String (handle_send_message/handle_list_tasks lines 169/188/229 do .map_err(|e| (-32000, e)) which cannot distinguish task-not-found). Correctness/consistency defect, low security impact. Recommendation to make all service fns return WaferError and let REST/a2a map at the boundary is the correct root-cause fix.

title: delete_context orphans entries on cascade failure but still returns Ok
description: delete_context deletes child entries first via delete_by_filters; if that fails it only logs a warning and proceeds to delete the parent context anyway, returning Ok(()). The entries are now orphaned (their context_id points at a deleted context) and the caller has no signal anything went wrong. Because entries are looked up by context_id, these orphans are effectively unreachable garbage that also skew any future count/aggregate over the entries table.
recommendation: Propagate the cascade-delete error (return Err) instead of swallowing it, so the context is not deleted when its entries cannot be — keeping the operation atomic from the caller's perspective. If best-effort cascade is truly intended, document that contract on the function and return a structured warning rather than silently dropping it.
verifierNote: Confirmed real by reading the file. crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/messages/service.rs:124-138 matches the quoted code exactly: delete_context cascade-deletes ENTRIES_TABLE rows (WHERE context_id=id) FIRST, but on failure only logs `tracing::warn!` and unconditionally proceeds to `db::delete(CONTEXTS_TABLE, id)`, returning Ok(()). There is no DB-level FK ON DELETE CASCADE — verified in migrations/001_messages_schema.sqlite.sql (entries.context_id is a plain TEXT NOT NULL column, no FOREIGN KEY/CASCADE), so the cascade is purely application-level and a failed delete_by_filters genuinely leaves entries behind while the parent context is removed. Entries are only ever queried by context_id (list_entries at service.rs:232 filters on context_id), so the orphans are effectively unreachable garbage, as the reviewer states. The REST caller (rest.rs:136-139) maps Ok(()) → {"deleted": true}, so the caller gets a success signal despite the silent partial failure — the core of the finding holds. Caveats that keep this at medium rather than higher: (1) it only manifests under a transient DB error occurring between the two delete calls (uncommon); (2) the recommended `return Err` does prevent deleting the parent when the cascade fails (good), but neither current nor recommended code is truly atomic since the two deletes aren't wrapped in a transaction; (3) the 'skews count/aggregate' claim is partly speculative — I found no full-table aggregate/count over ENTRIES_TABLE, only context_id-scoped lists. Note the contrast with files/quota.rs:129 sweep_stale_pending, which uses the same `if let Err(e) { warn }` pattern legitimately because it is an explicitly best-effort background sweep returning () — whereas delete_context returns Result and is a user-facing delete, so swallowing the cascade error there is inconsistent and a genuine code smell under the repo's 'fix at root cause / no quick fixes' rule. The String error type is pre-existing throughout this file (create_context/list_contexts also return Result<_, String>), so the #249 stringly-typed concern is not unique to this site; the actionable bug is the swallowed cascade error.

title: build_task_response_with_history silently swallows entry-list errors
description: When assembling task history, a failed list_entries is discarded via `if let Ok(entries) = ...`. On a DB/WRAP error the response is returned with NO messages and NO artifacts keys set, which a client cannot distinguish from a task that genuinely has no history. The error is not even logged. Every other failure path in this file maps to a JSON-RPC error code; this one is silent.
recommendation: Match the Err arm and return a JSON-RPC error (e.g. (-32000, ...)) consistent with the rest of the file, or at minimum tracing::warn! the failure. Don't return a 'successful' task response whose history was silently dropped.
verifierNote: Confirmed real at crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/messages/a2a.rs:355-368. The `if let Ok(entries) = service::list_entries(...)` discards the Err arm. The enclosing fn `build_task_response_with_history` returns `Result<serde_json::Value, (i64, String)>` (the JSON-RPC code/message tuple), and EVERY other failure path in this file propagates via map_err to (-32001)/(-32000) — including the `get_context` call 18 lines above (a2a.rs:337-343). Only this one is silent. `service::list_entries` (service.rs:195-235) genuinely can fail: it returns `Result<RecordList, String>` mapping db::list DB/WRAP errors to "Database error: {e}", so the Err arm is NOT infallible-by-construction. On such a failure the task response is returned Ok with `messages`/`artifacts` keys never set, indistinguishable to a client from a task with genuinely empty history, and nothing is logged. One correction to the reviewer's framing: the underlying `list_entries` itself is still stringly-typed (Result<_, String>), unlike get_context which returns WaferError — so the cleanest root-cause fix is to propagate (matching the existing `.map_err(|e| (-32000, e))?` style used at lines 169/188/229) rather than only adding a tracing::warn!. Severity held at medium: not a security/crash issue, but a silent-failure correctness + observability bug that violates the file's own established error-handling convention and the project's no-code-smells / explicit-error-handling rules (#249 context).

title: entry_card and default-view duplicate a hardcoded color palette with per-kind/per-role branching
description: entry_card hardcodes a 6-way (kind x role) palette of inline hex colors and border styles directly in markup. The same kind/role taxonomy (message/artifact/notification/status; user/agent/system) is independently re-listed in the default-view composer <select> options (pages.rs:392-402) and again as A2A entry mapping in a2a.rs. The styling is duplicated primitive strings with no shared source of truth, so adding a kind or recoloring requires editing several disconnected places, and the hex values bypass the CSS variable system the rest of the shell uses (e.g. var(--text-muted)).
recommendation: Lift the kind/role taxonomy into a single typed enum (or a const table) shared by the card styling, the composer option lists, and the a2a mapping, and move the palette into CSS classes (e.g. .entry--artifact) instead of inline hex so it tracks the design-token system. This kills the triple-listed taxonomy and the inline color magic strings.

title: kind/role/status/type are stringly-typed throughout the block
description: The core domain concepts — context type (conversation/task/notification), entry kind (message/artifact/notification/status), role (user/agent/system), and status (active/submitted/canceled/completed/...) — are passed and compared as bare &str everywhere (service.rs signatures, a2a.rs terminal-state array, pages.rs match arms, mod.rs endpoint schemas). There is no single definition of the valid values; the terminal-state set ["completed","failed","canceled","rejected"] lives only in a2a.rs:257, the kind branches live in pages.rs:61 and a2a.rs:360, and the composer options live in pages.rs. A typo in any one place silently mis-routes (e.g. add_entry with kind="artifcat" just renders as a message). add_entry's positional (kind, role, sender_id, content) &str params are also easy to transpose at call sites.
recommendation: Introduce enums (EntryKind, EntryRole, ContextType, TaskStatus with an is_terminal() method) with serde-backed string representations matching the on-wire values, and thread them through service signatures. This centralizes the valid sets, makes the terminal-state and kind branches exhaustive (compiler-checked), and removes the positional-&str transposition hazard in add_entry/create_context.

title: update_context allows clients to overwrite metadata wholesale via a string-keyed allowlist
description: update_context filters the incoming HashMap against an inline `["status","title","metadata"]` allowlist. The allowlist is a magic string array duplicated against the create_context field set and the endpoint input_schema in mod.rs:151-158, with no shared definition — they can drift. 'metadata' is accepted as an arbitrary opaque object overwrite (no merge, no validation), so a PATCH replaces the entire metadata blob; combined with the A2A path which stores message parts inside metadata, a client PATCH can clobber A2A part history. The rest.rs::update_context handler also passes the raw client HashMap straight through with no shape validation.
recommendation: Replace the ad-hoc allowlist with a typed UpdateContext struct (serde, optional fields) deserialized in rest.rs, so the allowed set is defined once and matches the schema in mod.rs by construction. Decide metadata semantics explicitly (merge vs replace) rather than silently full-replacing, and validate it's an object.

title: page_size/offset are i64 but silently truncated from usize via `as i64`
description: REST handlers compute pagination as usize (msg.pagination_params) then cast `page_size as i64` / `offset as i64` into the params structs, while a2a.rs builds the same params with native i64 literals and a `.min(100)` clamp on page_size. The REST path has no upper clamp on page_size at all — a client passing ?page_size=100000000 is honored verbatim against the DB (potential unbounded read / memory blow-up), whereas the A2A path caps at 100. The two callers of the same service function apply inconsistent limits, and the usize->i64 cast is an unguarded `as` that would wrap on pathological input.
recommendation: Clamp page_size to a shared maximum in one place (ideally in pagination_params or in the service functions, so both REST and A2A get the same cap), and use a fallible conversion (i64::try_from) or have pagination_params return i64 directly to drop the unguarded `as` cast.

title: No repo/ data layer: DB access and identical filter-building duplicated across mod.rs and pages.rs
description: Sibling feature blocks (auth, llm) put all collection access behind a repo/ module that owns the table constant and typed row access. legalpages instead scatters raw db::list / db::list_all / db::get / db::create / db::update / db::delete calls and hand-built ListOptions across both mod.rs and pages.rs. The exact same filter set (doc_type == X AND status == 'published') is rebuilt verbatim in at least three places: handle_admin_publish (mod.rs:283-298), find_current_doc (pages.rs:67-113, twice), and archive_published (pages.rs:894-911). There is no single place that knows how to query this collection, so query shape, sort keys, and field names drift independently.
recommendation: Introduce crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/legalpages/repo.rs (or repo/) following the auth/repo pattern: own `pub const TABLE`, a typed `LegalDoc` row, and functions like `latest_published(ctx, doc_type)`, `latest_draft(ctx, doc_type)`, `archive_published_except(ctx, doc_type, id)`, `next_version(ctx, doc_type)`. Have mod.rs handlers and pages.rs handlers both call the repo so filter/sort shape lives in one place.
verifierNote:
Core facts verified, but the reviewer's framing is partly wrong. TRUE: legalpages has no repo/ module (confirmed: of 10 feature blocks, only auth and products have one). Raw db::list/get/create/update/delete/list_all + hand-built ListOptions are scattered across both mod.rs (12 db calls) and pages.rs (10). The doc_type==X AND status=='published' filter pair is rebuilt verbatim in exactly 3 places — handle_admin_publish (mod.rs:283-298), the published fallback in find_current_doc (pages.rs:94-106), and archive_published (pages.rs:895-911); a 4th near-identical pair is in handle_get_public (mod.rs:71-90, differing only in sort key). The quoted evidence at pages.rs:894 is accurate. Additionally the table name lives in a central `pub(crate) const COLLECTION` (mod.rs:37) re-aliased in pages.rs:24 — that itself violates the documented convention "each repo module owns its own `pub const TABLE`... No central *_COLLECTION constants."

FALSE/overstated: the description claims "Sibling feature blocks (auth, llm) put all collection access behind a repo/ module." That is wrong about llm — llm has NO repo module; it scatters db::list/get/create/list_all/update across mod.rs, pages.rs, routes.rs, and ui.rs, sourcing table constants (TABLE/PROVIDERS_TABLE in schema.rs, SETTINGS_TABLE in mod.rs:42, CONTEXTS_TABLE/ENTRIES_TABLE from messages schema) exactly like legalpages does. So legalpages is NOT an outlier against a uniform convention; the repo/ pattern is followed by only 2 of 10 blocks (auth, products). legalpages matches the majority (8/10) inline pattern, including the cited "exemplar" llm.

The repo/ pattern IS the documented/canonical convention (solobase CLAUDE.md cites auth/repo/users.rs:12 as canonical, and the block-architecture spec lists repo/ as the data layer), so legalpages does diverge from best practice and the verbatim filter duplication + central COLLECTION const are genuine maintainability smells worth a repo.rs refactor. But this is an architecture/maintainability issue with no correctness or security impact, and the reviewer overstated prevalence. No raw SQL, no sync bridge, no stringly-typed error regression here. Downgraded medium -> low.

title: `COLLECTION` constant violates the `pub const TABLE` naming convention
description: solobase/CLAUDE.md mandates: 'each repo module owns its own `pub const TABLE: &str = "{org}__{block}__{name}"` ... No central `*_COLLECTION` constants.' Every other block (auth/repo/*.rs, llm/schema.rs) names it TABLE. legalpages names it COLLECTION and pages.rs re-imports it via `const COLLECTION: &str = super::COLLECTION;` (pages.rs:24). This is the precise `*_COLLECTION` naming the convention calls out, and it also means the table identifier lives in mod.rs rather than the (missing) repo module.
recommendation: Rename to `pub const TABLE` and locate it in the new repo module (per the convention's canonical auth/repo/users.rs:12 example). Update the pages.rs re-import accordingly.
verifierNote: Verified against actual code — every factual claim holds. solobase/CLAUDE.md:10 mandates "each repo module owns its own `pub const TABLE: &str = ...`. No central `*_COLLECTION` constants." legalpages/mod.rs:37 declares `pub(crate) const COLLECTION: &str = "suppers_ai__legalpages__documents";` and pages.rs:24 re-imports it as `const COLLECTION: &str = super::COLLECTION;`. A grep confirms legalpages is the ONLY block using the `COLLECTION` name — all 20+ other table constants (auth/repo/users.rs:12 canonical, llm/schema.rs:20, products, files, userportal, etc.) are named `TABLE`. So this is the precise `*_COLLECTION` deviation the convention calls out. The secondary structural claim is also correct: legalpages has no `repo/` module (only mod.rs, pages.rs, migrations/), so the table id lives in mod.rs. Genuine convention violation but purely cosmetic — the value itself is correctly formatted (`{org}__{block}__{name}`), and there is zero correctness/security/async/SQL impact. Downgrading medium→low: a naming-only nit. The rename portion of the fix is clearly warranted; the "relocate to a new repo module" part exceeds what the convention text strictly requires (it mandates naming, not a forced repo/ split for a single-table block).

title: handle_publish returns 200 OK with an `error` key on bad JSON — the exact anti-pattern handle_save was fixed to avoid
description: handle_save (pages.rs:580-583) carries an explicit comment that returning 200 with an `error` key is wrong because htmx/fetch clients treat 200 as success, and it now returns err_bad_request. handle_publish, three lines of logic away, still does the old thing for the same parse-failure case: returns ok_json with an `error` field (HTTP 200). The save-path fix was not applied to the publish path, so a malformed publish body silently looks like success at the transport layer.
recommendation: Return `err_bad_request(&format!("Invalid request: {e}"))` to mirror handle_save and handle_save_settings, so all three POST handlers branch on HTTP status consistently.
verifierNote: Confirmed real. At crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/legalpages/pages.rs:644-646, handle_publish returns `ok_json(&serde_json::json!({"error": format!("Invalid request: {e}")}))` (HTTP 200 with an `error` key) on a JSON parse failure. The two sibling POST handlers in the same file were fixed to the proper 4xx: handle_save (line 583) returns `err_bad_request(...)` and carries an explicit comment (lines 580-582) noting that the old 200-with-error pattern is wrong because htmx clients treat 200 as success; handle_save_settings (line 879) also uses `err_bad_request`. So handle_publish is the lone outlier. `err_bad_request` is already imported (line 15) and used elsewhere in the file (lines 697, 715), so the one-line fix the reviewer recommends is correct and in scope. The behavioral impact is genuine: a malformed publish body silently returns success at the transport layer. I downgraded severity from medium to low because this is an admin-only handler editing legal pages, the body is generated by the admin's own form (so the malformed-JSON branch is an unlikely edge case), and the impact is purely transport-status consistency — no data corruption, no security boundary, no async/SQL/config convention violation. Still worth fixing for consistency and to honor the very comment the adjacent handler documents.

title: DB write failures during publish return 400 Bad Request and echo the raw error to the client
description: When db::update / db::create fail during publish, the handler returns err_bad_request with `Failed to publish: {e}` interpolated. A database write failure is a server-side condition (500), not a client error (400). More importantly the #249 error refactor centralized internal errors in err_internal specifically so the underlying cause is logged with a correlation id and NEVER echoed to the client (see helpers.rs:295-314). Here the raw DB error `{e}` is leaked in the response body, and the status is wrong. handle_save correctly uses err_internal for the analogous failures (pages.rs:621, 635).
recommendation: Replace both `err_bad_request(&format!("Failed to publish: {e}"))` sites with `err_internal("Failed to publish legal page", e)` to match handle_save, fix the status code, and stop leaking the raw error string.
verifierNote: Confirmed against the actual code. pages.rs:694-718 (handle_publish): both the db::update arm (line 697) and the db::create arm (line 715) return err_bad_request(&format!("Failed to publish: {e}")). err_bad_request (helpers.rs:244-250) maps to ErrorCode::InvalidArgument = HTTP 400 and copies `message` verbatim into the response, so the raw DB error {e} is leaked to the client and the status is wrong for a server-side write failure. This directly contradicts the documented #249 intent: err_internal (helpers.rs:288-314) is explicitly written to NOT echo raw error text — it logs the cause via tracing::error! with a correlation_id and returns only "Internal server error (ref: <id>)" (ErrorCode::Internal = 500), with a doc comment stating "Do not pass raw error text to the client." The sibling handler handle_save handles the identical db::create/db::update failures correctly using err_internal("Failed to save legal-page draft", e) at pages.rs:621 and 635, so this is an internal inconsistency, not an accepted exception. Recommendation is correct: replace both sites with err_internal("Failed to publish legal page", e). Severity kept at medium — it is a genuine post-#249 convention violation (string-coerced internal error leaked) plus a wrong status code on a write path, but the endpoint is admin-authenticated (legal-page publishing), so the leak surface is limited and there is no auth-bypass/injection dimension that would justify high.

title: handle_admin_update passes a raw client HashMap straight into db::update (mass-assignment)
description: The PATCH /api/documents/{id} handler deserializes the body into an untyped `HashMap<String, serde_json::Value>` and, after stamping updated_at, forwards every field to db::update. A caller can therefore set arbitrary columns — status, version, created_by, published_at, even id — bypassing the draft/publish workflow that the rest of the block carefully enforces (e.g. handle_save deliberately creates a new draft when editing a published doc). The endpoint is admin-gated, so severity is bounded, but it is an unvalidated mass-assignment surface that defeats the block's own invariants.
recommendation: Deserialize into a typed struct with an explicit allow-list of mutable fields (title, content, doc_type), the same way CreateDoc is typed at mod.rs:207. Route status/version changes through the publish path only.
verifierNote:
Mechanism is accurately described. handle_admin_update (mod.rs:235-260) deserializes the PATCH body into an untyped HashMap<String, serde_json::Value>, stamps updated_at, and forwards every key to db::update. I confirmed the sqlite service (wafer-block-sqlite/src/service.rs:441-497) builds the SET clause from ALL keys in the data map with no server-side allow-list, so a client CAN set status/version/created_by/published_at and even re-key id (UPDATE ... SET id=? WHERE id=?). Unlike handle_save (pages.rs:588-602), which deliberately spawns a new draft when editing a published doc, the raw PATCH path mutates the live row directly, so the draft/publish convenience workflow is indeed not enforced on this surface.

But the reviewer overstates both scope and severity. (1) This is NOT a legalpages-specific defect: it is the deliberate, codebase-wide pattern. The generic crud.rs::crud_update (lines 102-130) does the identical untyped-HashMap → stamp_updated → db::update passthrough and is used by products/admin-users/custom-tables/etc.; crud.rs even carries an `audit-allow-file` pragma acknowledging the pure pass-through design. handle_admin_update is just an inline instance of that pattern. (2) The endpoint is AuthLevel::Admin (BlockInfo at mod.rs:559), so no privilege boundary is crossed — an admin already has full authority over legal docs via the admin UI, and the JSON API is documented (endpoints_page) as the raw lower-level admin surface. There is no untrusted-input / escalation path here; the draft workflow it 'bypasses' is a UI safety net, not a security control. (3) None of the project conventions (root-cause, no raw SQL, no sync bridges, config naming, error variants) concern typed request bodies / mass-assignment, so this is not a convention violation. It is a legitimate low-severity hardening nit (an admin can clobber a published doc or corrupt the id field via the raw API), worth tightening with a typed allow-list (matching CreateDoc at mod.rs:207-212), but it is admin-only with no security impact — 'medium' is too high.

title: Two parallel CRUD surfaces for the same collection (JSON API vs SSR save/publish)
description: The block exposes the documents collection through two independent write paths with overlapping but subtly different semantics: the JSON API in mod.rs (handle_admin_create/update/publish/delete) and the SSR handlers in pages.rs (handle_save/handle_publish). Both implement create-vs-update branching, version handling, and publish+archive, but differently — e.g. mod.rs handle_admin_create always sets version=1, pages.rs handle_save does too but handle_publish auto-increments; mod.rs handle_admin_publish archives BEFORE re-reading vs pages.rs handle_publish publishes-then-archives (the pages.rs ordering is the safer one, per its own comment at 682-684). This is duplicated business logic that will drift; the safer publish ordering already exists in only one of the two copies.
recommendation: Collapse both onto shared repo functions (see the repo-layer finding) so there is one publish() that does publish-then-archive, one create_draft(), one next_version(). The HTTP handlers should be thin adapters over the repo, not two reimplementations.
verifierNote: Verified against both files. Two independent, both-registered write surfaces for the same collection (mod.rs:583-631 router): JSON API handle_admin_create/update/publish/delete (mod.rs:201-333) at /b/legalpages/api/documents/..., and SSR handle_save/handle_publish (pages.rs:576-731) at /b/legalpages/admin/save|publish. The duplication is concrete: create-vs-update branching exists in both, and the archive-previous-published loop is implemented twice — inline in mod.rs handle_admin_publish (lines 282-307) and as archive_published() in pages.rs (894-921). The publish-ordering divergence the reviewer cites is real and provably material: mod.rs handle_admin_publish archives FIRST (300-307) then publishes (317), so an archive-success-then-publish-failure leaves the doc_type with no published version; pages.rs handle_publish publishes/creates FIRST then archives (722-723), with an explicit comment (682-684) stating the up-front-archive order is unsafe and using except_id to avoid archiving the just-published doc. So the unsafe ordering the comment warns against is exactly what the other copy still does. Version handling also diverges (handle_admin_create + handle_save create-branch hardcode version:1; handle_publish auto-increments via a max-version query 652-680; handle_admin_publish doesn't touch version). This is not an allowed exception (no raw SQL, no test/SQL-explorer carve-out). Notably this block has NO repo/ module — both handlers call db::* directly, contrary to the block-architecture convention that the data layer live in repo/, which is why the recommendation to collapse onto shared repo functions is the correct root-cause fix. Kept at medium rather than upgraded: the unsafe ordering is on the secondary JSON-API path (the admin editor uses the safe SSR path) and only fails in a narrow mid-operation-DB-failure window, but it is a genuine latent correctness bug atop the maintainability/duplication concern.

title: Version-parsing closure (as_i64 or parse from string) duplicated in three handlers
description: The defensive 'version may be stored as int or as string' parse — `v.as_i64().or_else(|| v.as_str().and_then(|s| s.parse().ok())).unwrap_or(1)` — appears verbatim in handle_get_public (mod.rs:126-133), editor_page (pages.rs:141-147), and handle_publish next_version (pages.rs:672-676). That this logic exists at all signals the schema lets version be either a JSON number or string (the SQL says INTEGER, so the string case is purely a serialization quirk). Three copies will drift.
recommendation: Add one `fn version_of(record: &Record) -> i64` (or an `i64_field`-style RecordExt method that also tolerates stringified numbers) and call it everywhere. Ideally fix the upstream serialization so version is always a number and the string branch can be deleted.

title: Version auto-increment is a read-then-write race; publish/archive is not atomic
description: handle_publish computes next_version by listing the highest existing version and adding 1, then writes. Two concurrent publishes of the same doc_type can read the same max and both write the same version. Separately, publish and archive_published are two unsynchronized statements, so an interleaving could briefly leave two published rows of one type, or (with the mod.rs ordering) zero. Admin-only access makes a true race unlikely, but the manual MAX(version)+1 pattern is exactly what an aggregate/upsert builder or a DB-side increment should handle.
recommendation: Compute next_version via an aggregate MAX in sql-utils (aggregate::*) or use db::increment_field_where (the typed increment shipped in SEC-056) rather than read-modify-write in Rust; ideally make publish+archive a single transactional unit in the repo layer.
verifierNote:
Confirmed against pages.rs:651-723. The read-modify-write is real: lines 668-679 list the highest existing version (sort version desc, limit 1) via db::list and add 1 in Rust, so two concurrent publishes of one doc_type could read the same max and write the same version. The non-atomic publish-then-archive is also real: handle_publish does db::create/db::update (lines 685-718) then archive_published (line 723) as separate unsynchronized ops. The reviewer's "mod.rs ordering" aside is accurate — handle_admin_publish in mod.rs (lines 282-317) archives FIRST then publishes, the opposite order, which is the path that could transiently leave zero published rows; handle_publish deliberately publishes first (commented at 682-684) to avoid that, so within the cited function the worst case is two published rows, not zero.

This is a genuine TOCTOU/correctness observation but NOT a project-convention violation: the code uses the typed db:: data layer (db::list/create/update), not raw SQL (exec_raw/query_raw), so the no-raw-SQL rule is not breached; no block_on, no hardcoded values, no stringly-typed-error regression here.

Severity stays low and the recommendation is partly imprecise. All four publish endpoints are AuthLevel::Admin, so a realistic concurrent-publish race is very unlikely. On the fix: wafer-sql-utils has an AggFunc::Max but only via build_grouped_query — there is no db::max(...) facade method, so the aggregate-MAX route requires adding a new data-layer helper. And db::increment_field_where (SEC-056) increments an existing row's field by N; it does NOT compute a cross-row MAX+1, so it does not directly solve next-version selection. True atomicity of publish+archive would need a transaction primitive the data layer does not currently expose. So the finding is real but minor; the suggested remediations are directionally reasonable but not drop-in as stated.

title: Stale/incorrect doc comments: 'Quill rich text editor' and 'doc_id_for_archive'
description: The module doc comment advertises a 'Quill rich text editor' for both editors, but the implementation is a plain Markdown <textarea> (a unit test, editor_page_uses_textarea_not_contenteditable, even asserts there is NO contenteditable). The comment at pages.rs:721 references a variable `doc_id_for_archive` that does not exist anywhere in the file. Both are leftovers from prior implementations that now mislead readers.
recommendation: Update the module docs to describe the Markdown textarea + tabbed preview, and drop the dangling `doc_id_for_archive` reference (just say we pass the freshly-published id to archive_published).

title: extract_doc_id reimplements path parsing the router already does
description: extract_doc_id first tries msg.var("id") (router-extracted) and then falls back to hand-parsing the path with a hardcoded prefix string and split('/'). The hardcoded '/b/legalpages/api/documents/' prefix is repeated from the route table in handle() and will silently break if the route prefix ever changes. The same handle() match arms already string-match these prefixes with starts_with/ends_with, so route knowledge is smeared across the file.
recommendation: If the CF runtime doesn't populate msg.var("id"), fix that at the router/runtime layer so blocks can rely on path vars uniformly, rather than each block re-parsing its own paths. At minimum, derive the prefix from a single const shared with the route table.

title: markdown_to_html / safe_url rendering logic lives in mod.rs instead of pages.rs
description: The block-architecture convention puts SSR/rendering in pages.rs/routes.rs and keeps mod.rs for the block def + service wiring. markdown_to_html, safe_url, render_legal_page, and LegalPageInputs are all pure presentation/rendering helpers but live in mod.rs; pages.rs has to reach back with `super::markdown_to_html` (pages.rs:936). The public-page rendering and the admin-page rendering are thus split across two files for no structural reason.
recommendation: Move markdown_to_html, safe_url, render_legal_page and LegalPageInputs (plus the public-page handler body) into pages.rs (or a rendering submodule) so mod.rs holds only the Block impl, action routing, and the JSON-API/lifecycle wiring.
verifierNote:
All factual claims verified against the actual code. mod.rs holds: markdown_to_html (line 468, pub(super)), safe_url (line 514, private), render_legal_page (line 390), LegalPageInputs (line 376), and the public-page handler handle_get_public (line 56, an impl LegalPagesBlock method that builds maud markup at lines 145-159). pages.rs:936 does indeed reach back with `super::markdown_to_html(markdown)` inside render_preview_fragment. So the public-page SSR rendering (mod.rs) and admin-page SSR rendering (pages.rs: editor_page/endpoints_page/settings_page) are genuinely split across two files, matching the reviewer's description.

This is a real but minor architecture-smell against the soft block-architecture convention (pages.rs = SSR handlers; mod.rs = block def + service impl).
