It is purely organizational: there is NO correctness, security, async-bridge, raw-SQL, hardcoded-value, or error-handling issue. markdown_to_html is actually XSS-safe by construction (pulldown-cmark raw-HTML passthrough disabled + safe_url scheme allow-list), so nothing dangerous is being misplaced. The `pub(super)` cross-module reference is idiomatic Rust, not a hack.

Two caveats that keep this at the low/cosmetic end: (1) the convention is not uniformly enforced — messages/mod.rs also contains rendering-related code, while llm/products/vector keep it out, so there is existing precedent for the exception; (2) the public-page rendering legitimately sits with handle_get_public because that handler is wired through the Block action dispatch (impl LegalPagesBlock), so co-locating its small markup helpers with it is defensible, not arbitrary. Severity 'low' as claimed is appropriate; it could even be argued cosmetic/nit rather than a true smell.

title: Duplicated inline JS toast/fetch boilerplate across editor and settings pages
description: EDITOR_JS (saveDocument) and the settings-page submitSettings function each independently hand-roll the same pattern: disable button, set 'Saving...', fetch JSON, .then(r=>r.json()), dispatch a showToast CustomEvent with `data.error ? 'error' : 'success'`, .catch dispatch error toast, .finally re-enable button. This ~15-line idiom is copy-pasted; any change to the toast contract must be edited in multiple inline string blobs that get no compiler or linter coverage.
recommendation: Factor a shared `postJsonWithToast(url, body, btn)` helper into the app's static JS bundle (referenced by all admin pages) and call it from both editor and settings, instead of embedding two copies as PreEscaped string constants.

title: Inline-styled SSR markup with hardcoded color literals instead of design tokens
description: The editor markup and EDITOR_CSS are riddled with hardcoded hex colors and dimensions (#f1f5f9, #64748b, #e2e8f0, #1e293b, #ef4444, min-height:500px) and inline `style="..."` attributes, while the public page deliberately uses CSS custom-property design tokens (--public-page-bg, --public-page-accent) via the shared template. The admin editor bypasses that token system, so theme changes won't reach it and the same palette values are duplicated from the global stylesheet into a block-local string.
recommendation: Move the editor styles into the shared app CSS bundle using the existing design-token variables, and drop the per-element inline style attributes, so the admin editor inherits theme tokens like the public page does.

title: Bootstrap-token redemption redirects to a non-existent /b/auth/dashboard route (404)
description: After successfully redeeming a bootstrap admin token (creating the admin user and minting a valid session cookie), the handler issues a 302 to `/b/auth/dashboard`. No block registers that path: auth_ui::AuthUiBlock::handle has no `("retrieve", "/auth/dashboard")` arm and falls through to `err_not_found`, and the solobase_core routing table only maps `/b/userportal` / `/b/admin`. The authenticated root redirect (routing.rs root_redirect) sends users to `/b/userportal/`, not `/b/auth/dashboard`. So the freshly-bootstrapped admin lands on a 404 immediately after onboarding — the worst possible first impression for the one-shot admin-setup flow. (The stale comment in solobase-web/solobase.toml repeats the same wrong path, so the mistake is documented, not just incidental.)
recommendation: Redirect to the same place a normal login lands: read `SOLOBASE_SHARED__POST_LOGIN_REDIRECT` (default `/b/admin/`) and validate it with `is_safe_local_redirect` (as login.rs/oauth callback already do), falling back to `/b/admin/` or `/b/userportal/`. Do not hardcode a path that no block serves. Also fix the stale `/b/auth/dashboard` reference in solobase-web/solobase.toml.
verifierNote: Confirmed real. bootstrap.rs:105-109 issues a 302 with `Location: /b/auth/dashboard` exactly as quoted. That path routes to the auth-ui block (routing.rs:106 maps the `/b/auth/` prefix to suppers-ai/auth-ui), but the block's dispatch (mod.rs:312-369) has NO `("retrieve", "/auth/dashboard")` arm — it falls through to `_ => err_not_found("not found")` (mod.rs:369), which returns WaferError{code: NotFound} (helpers.rs:271-277), i.e. a 404. No block serves `/b/auth/dashboard`: the actual dashboards are `/b/userportal/` (userportal mod.rs:128) and `/b/admin/` (admin dashboard); root_redirect (routing.rs:303-308) sends authenticated users to `/b/userportal/`. The bootstrap GET page (pages/bootstrap.rs:50) is a plain no-JS `form method=post` so the browser follows the server 302 straight into the 404 — confirming the bad UX. Note login.rs does NOT suffer this: it returns JSON with Set-Cookie (no server redirect), so it's a JS-driven flow; the bug is specific to the no-JS bootstrap redemption path. The recommendation is sound and matches existing convention in oauth/callback.rs:396-402 (read SOLOBASE_SHARED__POST_LOGIN_REDIRECT default /b/admin/, validate via is_safe_local_redirect). The stale comment in solobase-web/solobase.toml:6 does repeat the same wrong path. Downgraded from high to medium: it's a genuine correctness/UX dead-end but it's a 404 (no data loss, no security exposure — the admin is already authenticated with a valid cookie and can reach /b/admin/ manually), and it only affects the one-shot bootstrap-token redemption path, not the common env-var bootstrap.

title: SETTINGS_KEYS hardcodes config-var metadata that already lives in ConfigVar declarations
description: The admin auth-settings page maintains a 11-row `SETTINGS_KEYS` tuple table that re-declares each key's label, help text, default, sensitivity flag, and input_type. This metadata is already the single source of truth elsewhere: `SOLOBASE_SHARED__*` keys in `config_vars.rs::shared_config_vars()`, and `SUPPERS_AI__AUTH_UI__OAUTH_*` keys in this block's own `BlockInfo::config_keys` (auth_ui/mod.rs). The defaults are duplicated verbatim (e.g. `/b/admin/` for POST_LOGIN_REDIRECT, `false` for ENABLE_OAUTH) and will silently drift when one side is edited. CLAUDE.md explicitly says blocks declare config vars via ConfigVar and that validation/rendering metadata (sensitivity from `_SECRET`/`_KEY` suffix, input_type) must be derived from those declarations, not hardcoded lists. The hand-rolled `(key,label,help,default,bool,&str)` tuple is also primitive-obsession: `bool` for sensitive and `&str` for input_type re-invent what `ConfigVar.input_type` / convention-based sensitivity already model.
recommendation: Drive this page from the declared ConfigVars: pull the relevant subset out of `collect_all_config_vars()` / `shared_config_vars()` + this block's `config_keys()`, grouping by section, and render label/help/default/input_type/sensitivity from each `ConfigVar`. If the framework auth keys (`SUPPERS_AI__AUTH__REQUIRE_VERIFICATION` / `ALLOWED_EMAIL_DOMAINS`) aren't yet declared anywhere (the mod.rs comment notes a wafer-run gap), declare them where they belong rather than letting this page be their de-facto definition. This removes the drift hazard and the hand-rolled tuple type.
verifierNote: Confirmed against the actual code. `SETTINGS_KEYS` (settings.rs lines 18-108) is exactly the 11-row `&[(&str,&str,&str,&str,bool,&str)]` tuple table the reviewer quotes, re-declaring key/label/help/default/is_sensitive/input_type. The duplication is verified: ALLOW_SIGNUP (default "true"), ENABLE_OAUTH ("false"), and POST_LOGIN_REDIRECT ("/b/admin/") are also declared in config_vars.rs::shared_config_vars() with matching name/help/default/input_type; BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_EMAIL/_PASSWORD are declared in auth/config.rs::auth_config_vars() (folded into shared via `vars.extend`); the 4 OAuth keys are declared in this block's own BlockInfo::config_keys (auth_ui/mod.rs lines 125-178) with name/input_type/optional. Defaults are duplicated verbatim, creating a real drift hazard. This directly contradicts an explicit, doubly-stated CLAUDE.md rule (solobase + workspace): "Blocks declare their own config vars via ConfigVar... No hardcoded lists — validation rules derived from conventions (suffix _SECRET/_KEY = sensitive, input_type = UI rendering)." The hand-rolled `bool`/`&str` tuple is primitive-obsession reinventing ConfigVar.input_type and convention-based sensitivity. Reviewer's nuance is accurate: SUPPERS_AI__AUTH__REQUIRE_VERIFICATION and ALLOWED_EMAIL_DOMAINS are NOT declared as ConfigVars anywhere (only read via config::get_default string literals in login/signup/refresh/callback.rs), and mod.rs lines 118-124 documents this as a wafer-run gap — so this page is their de-facto declaration, exactly as claimed; the fix for those two requires declaring them first. One thing to note on severity: there is no active correctness/security bug — current defaults and sensitivity flags happen to match the ConfigVar side, so the impact is latent drift + primitive-obsession, a maintainability finding. Medium is defensible (it violates a hard, explicitly-written convention) but sits at the lower end of medium. (Minor aside, not part of the finding: the page gates signup via SOLOBASE_SHARED__ALLOW_SIGNUP while auth/config.rs declares a separate SOLOBASE_SHARED__AUTH__SIGNUP_ENABLED — two distinct signup-gating keys exist, a possible deeper smell but out of scope here.)

title: Settings save swallows config::set errors and always reports success
description: `handle_post` writes each setting with `let _ = config::set(ctx, key, value).await;`, discarding the `Result<(), WaferError>`. It then unconditionally returns `{"message": "Settings saved"}`. If a write fails (WRAP denial, DB outage, validation rejection) the admin still sees a green 'Saved' toast while the change was silently dropped — an error-swallowing pattern that the recent #249 error-handling refactor was meant to eliminate. Auth settings (allowed domains, require-verification, OAuth secrets) are security-relevant, so a false 'saved' is more than cosmetic.
recommendation: Propagate the first failing `config::set` as `err_internal("Failed to save setting", e)` (including which key failed), or collect failures and return a 500 listing them. Only return the success payload when every write succeeded.
verifierNote:
CONFIRMED. crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/auth_ui/pages/settings.rs:222-228 does exactly `let _ = config::set(ctx, key, value).await;` in a loop over SETTINGS_KEYS, then unconditionally returns `ok_json({"message":"Settings saved"})`. config::set genuinely returns `Result<(), WaferError>` (verified at wafer-run/crates/wafer-core/src/clients/config.rs:33), so the Result is discarded. The client JS (SETTINGS_JS) only shows an error toast when `d.error` is present, so a 200 with this body always renders a green "Saved" — a silent-failure path for security-relevant auth settings (allowed email domains, require-verification, OAuth secrets). Real bug, severity medium is fair.

Two corrections to the reviewer's framing: (1) The recommendation to "return 500 / propagate the first failure" overstates the established convention. The two sibling settings handlers in the SAME repo — products/pages.rs:671 and legalpages/pages.rs:884 — both handle the error by `tracing::warn!(...)` and STILL return success (200). So the in-repo pattern is "log, don't swallow silently," not "return 500." auth_ui/settings.rs is strictly worse than even those because it has zero logging. (Two other call sites — userportal/mod.rs:779, admin/email.rs:126 — also use the bare `let _ =` silent-discard, so this anti-pattern is present in several places, not unique.) (2) The attribution to #249 is loose: #249 (commit 6e0f244) replaced RuntimeError string-coercion with explicit error variants — about internal error TYPE representation, not about UI handlers swallowing config::set Results. The bug stands on its own merits regardless of #249.

The minimal correct fix matching repo convention is at least `if let Err(e) = config::set(...) { tracing::warn!(...); }` (as products/legalpages do); a stronger fix would surface failure to the admin via the existing err_internal helper so the toast turns red. Either way the silent `let _ =` is a real error-swallow on a security-relevant surface.

title: Email-dispatch helper triplicated across signup/verify/forgot_password
description: `send_verification_email` is byte-for-byte identical in api/signup.rs (308-330) and api/verify.rs (207-228), and api/forgot_password.rs (64-86) has `send_reset_email` which differs only in the `template` string and the log message. All three build the same `email.send_template` Message, serialize `{template,to,token}`, call `ctx.call_block("suppers-ai/email", ...)`, and warn-log on failure. Three copies of one concept means a fix to the email-call shape (e.g. adding error surfacing, switching to a typed email client, adding more template vars) must be made in three places.
recommendation: Extract one shared helper, e.g. `auth_ui::email::send_template(ctx, template: &str, to: &str, token: &str)` (or a thin typed wrapper around the suppers-ai/email block), and call it from all three sites. Keeps the call shape and failure handling in one place.
verifierNote: Verified against the actual code. The duplication is real and exactly as described. signup.rs:309-330 `send_verification_email` and verify.rs:207-228 `send_verification_email` are byte-for-byte identical (confirmed via `diff` producing no output). forgot_password.rs:65-86 `send_reset_email` differs ONLY in the function name, the `template` value ("verification" vs "password_reset"), and the warn-log wording — the `Message { kind: "email.send_template" }` construction, `serde_json::to_vec(&req).unwrap_or_default()`, `ctx.call_block("suppers-ai/email", email_msg, InputStream::from_bytes(body_bytes))`, and `if let Err(e) = out.collect_buffered().await { tracing::warn!(...) }` failure handling are identical across all three. The cited line range 308-330 is accurate (308 is the doc comment, 330 the closing brace). There is no existing shared email helper: api/mod.rs is a flat list of leaf handler modules with no `email`/helper module, so these are three genuinely independent private copies. This is a legitimate DRY/maintainability finding consistent with the project's 'fix at root cause / no code smells' convention, and the recommended single shared `send_template(ctx, template, to, token)` helper is the correct fix. Downgrading from medium to low: this is internal, mechanical, best-effort boilerplate with no correctness or security risk — all three already swallow failures with a consistent warn-log, so there is no latent bug, only a 3-site change cost. No convention exception applies (it is not raw SQL, not block_on, not a test helper).

title: Inline auth-form JavaScript ($/showErr/fetch boilerplate) copy-pasted across page handlers
description: Each SSR auth page embeds a near-identical raw-string `<script>`: login.rs (login_script, shared), signup.rs, change_password.rs, and reset_password.rs each redefine `var $=...`, `showErr`, the `fetch(...).then(json)` pattern, the `if(!r.ok||d.error){...}` error extraction `(d.error&&d.error.message)||d.error||d.message`, and button disable/restore logic. settings.rs has yet another copy with toast dispatch. The error-extraction expression in particular is duplicated 5+ times; if the JSON error envelope shape changes (it just did in #249) every copy must be updated by hand, and they already diverge slightly (some read `d.message`, some don't).
recommendation: Hoist the shared bits ($, showErr/showInfo, an `extractError(d)` function, and a `postJson(url, body)` wrapper) into one `pages::auth_form_js()` (sibling to the existing `pw_toggle_js`/`login_script` in pages/mod.rs) and have each page emit it plus only its page-specific submit handler. Centralizes the error-envelope contract on the client side.

title: auth_ui reads user display name from `name` column while writes go to `display_name`
description: GET /b/auth/api/me returns `user.str_field("name")` for the display name, and handle_update writes BOTH `name` and `display_name` (me.rs:50-57) to keep them in sync. Meanwhile login.rs/signup.rs return the typed `user.display_name` field. The users table carries two columns for the same concept (`name` + `display_name`, written together in auth/repo/users.rs:70-71). This dual-column scheme leaks into auth_ui as inconsistent reads (raw `str_field("name")` here vs the typed `display_name` elsewhere) and forces every writer to remember to update both. A read of the wrong column silently returns stale data if the two ever diverge.
recommendation: Read through the typed repo (`users::find_by_id` -> `user.display_name`) here as login/signup do, rather than `str_field("name")`. Longer term, collapse the `name`/`display_name` dual column in the auth repo to one canonical column so handlers can't pick the wrong one (repo-layer change, out of this block's scope but it is the root cause).

title: OAuth provider-configured check duplicated between pages/mod.rs and providers.rs (divergent definitions of 'enabled')
description: `oauth_provider_configured` (pages/mod.rs) deems a provider usable only when CLIENT_ID + CLIENT_SECRET + REDIRECT_URI are all non-empty — matching what start.rs/callback.rs actually need. But oauth/providers.rs (the /b/auth/api/oauth/providers JSON endpoint) reports a provider as `enabled: true` when only CLIENT_ID resolves (`config::get(client_id_key).is_ok()`), ignoring secret and redirect URI. So the API can advertise a provider as enabled that the login page correctly hides and that would fail on click. Two places encode 'is this provider ready' with different rules.
recommendation: Have oauth/providers.rs call the same readiness predicate as the page (factor `oauth_provider_configured` so both the page and the API use it). One definition of 'configured/enabled' prevents the API and UI from disagreeing.

title: Open-redirect / safe-URL validation logic split across redirect.rs and oauth/callback.rs
description: redirect.rs hosts `is_safe_local_redirect` as 'the single canonical check' for redirect safety, but callback.rs defines a second, independent validator `is_safe_frontend_url` (control-char rejection, scheme/host allowlist) right next to where it also calls `is_safe_local_redirect`. Both are security-critical URL validators with overlapping concerns (control chars, scheme-relative tricks) yet live in different files with their own test suites. A future hardening of one (e.g. a newly discovered bypass) can easily miss the other.
recommendation: Move `is_safe_frontend_url` into redirect.rs alongside `is_safe_local_redirect` so the module is the one home for all auth-surface URL-safety checks (the module doc already claims to be exactly that). Keep the tests with it. No behavior change, but the two validators evolve together.

title: Discarded RateLimitOutcome::Allowed headers tracked as a comment instead of being emitted
description: Every rate-limit arm discards `RateLimitOutcome::Allowed(headers)` (the X-RateLimit-* headers), only acting on `Limited`. The block comment acknowledges this and says injecting the headers 'needs a streaming-middleware shape we don't have yet', tracked as a single follow-up. This is a known, documented gap rather than a defect, but it means clients never receive rate-limit budget headers on successful requests, and the five arms repeat the same `let ip = ...; let identity = if ip.is_empty() {"unknown"} else {ip};` boilerplate three times. The catch-all `("update", _)` and `("delete", _)` arms also rate-limit any update/delete path under this block, which is broader than the enumerated endpoints (currently only api-keys/me), so a future route inherits write rate-limiting implicitly.
recommendation: When the streaming-middleware shape lands, thread the Allowed headers onto the eventual OutputStream. Meanwhile, factor the repeated `ip-or-unknown` identity derivation into a small helper to remove the three copies, and consider matching the explicit api-keys/me paths in the write/read arms rather than the broad `("update", _)`/`("delete", _)` wildcards so newly-added routes opt in to rate limiting deliberately.

title: Flagged raw SQL in pages/orgs.rs is a false positive (test-fixture only)
description: The scope notes flagged pages/orgs.rs for raw SQL. The page handler itself is clean — it calls `orgs::list_for_user(ctx, &user_id)` from the typed repo. The only `db::exec_raw` is inside the `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` seed_user helper (lines 84-98), which is the explicitly-allowed 'test-fixture setup' exception. No action needed on the production path; recording so the flag isn't mistaken for a violation. (The repo's own raw SQL lives in auth/repo/orgs.rs, outside this scope.)
recommendation: No change required — this is an allowed test-fixture exception. If desired for consistency, the seed could go through `users::insert`, but raw SQL in test setup is sanctioned by CLAUDE.md.

title: ErrorCode::status_code() is dead code — HTTP status is sourced from the wafer ErrorCode mapping instead
description: The solobase `ErrorCode::status_code()` method has zero non-test callers anywhere in the repo (verified by grep across crates/). The only path that turns an `ErrorCode` into a response is `error_response()`, which calls `solobase_error_code_to_wafer()` and lets the wafer ErrorCode drive the HTTP status. `status_code()` is therefore an unused parallel status table that can silently drift from the wafer mapping (e.g. RateLimitExceeded → 429 here, but maps to wafer ResourceExhausted in solobase_error_code_to_wafer, which the runtime renders as a different status). Dead code that duplicates the source of truth.
recommendation: Either delete `status_code()` (and its test) since the wafer ErrorCode → HTTP mapping is the real source of truth, or — if a solobase-specific status is genuinely needed — wire it through `error_response`/the response middleware so there is exactly one mapping. Do not keep two independent status tables.
verifierNote: Confirmed dead code. `ErrorCode::status_code()` (crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/errors.rs:80-111) has zero non-test callers: grep for `.status_code()` across crates/ shows only the in-file `#[cfg(test)]` asserts (lines 127-160); the lone other hit (solobase-cloudflare/src/network_service.rs:58) is `resp.status_code()` on a worker Fetch Response, an unrelated type. The live error path is `error_response()` (errors.rs:188) -> `solobase_error_code_to_wafer()` (errors.rs:199) -> WaferError, and the HTTP status is derived from the wafer code in wafer-block-http-listener::error_code_to_http_status (lib.rs:148). The solobase `status_code()` table is never consulted; pipeline.rs:135-187 takes status from META_RESP_STATUS meta or hardcodes 500/204/200. The doc comment (lines 77-79) is actively misleading: it claims the method is "Used by JSON-API handlers (and the response middleware)" — it is used by neither. So the finding's core claim (unused parallel status table duplicating the real source of truth) is correct. One correction to the reviewer: their drift example is wrong — ResourceExhausted maps to 429 in error_code_to_http_status (lib.rs:157), so RateLimitExceeded resolves to 429 on BOTH paths, not different statuses. A genuinely-divergent case does exist though: solobase status_code() maps QuotaExceeded/FileTooLarge -> 413, while the live path maps them to wafer ResourceExhausted -> 429; the dead table already disagrees with reality, but harmlessly because nothing reads it. Downgraded medium->low: pure dead code, no runtime correctness or security impact (live status path is unaffected); only cost is maintenance confusion from a stale doc comment. Recommendation stands: delete status_code() + its test, or wire a single mapping if a solobase-specific status is ever needed.

title: error_response embeds the machine code into the human message as `[code]` while also setting a separate wafer ErrorCode — redundant dual representation
description: `error_response` produces `message = "[{code}] {message}"` AND maps the same `ErrorCode` to a wafer `ErrorCode` via `solobase_error_code_to_wafer`. The machine-readable identity now lives in two places (the bracketed prefix in the free-text message and the structured wafer code), and the many-to-one mapping (e.g. InvalidCredentials/NotAuthenticated/InvalidToken/TokenExpired all collapse to Unauthenticated) means the structured wafer code can no longer recover the original solobase code — clients that need the specific code must string-parse the `[...]` prefix. This is the kind of implicit, stringly-typed mapping the project conventions warn against. rate_limited_response in rate_limit.rs:260-275 hand-reproduces this exact `[code] message` + meta construction, confirming the pattern is copy-pasted rather than centralized.
recommendation: Carry the solobase error code as a structured meta entry (e.g. `error.code`) rather than baked into the prose message, so the granular code survives the lossy wafer-code mapping without string parsing. Have rate_limit.rs::rate_limited_response delegate to error_response (passing the Retry-After meta through) instead of re-implementing the `[code]` formatting.
verifierNote: Verified against source — every concrete claim holds. errors.rs:188-196: error_response sets WaferError.code via the lossy solobase_error_code_to_wafer mapping AND bakes the code into the prose as format!("[{}] {}", code.as_str(), message) — a redundant dual representation of the same machine identity. The wafer mapping (errors.rs:200-232) is genuinely many-to-one and lossy: InvalidCredentials/NotAuthenticated/InvalidToken/TokenExpired all collapse to Unauthenticated, EmailAlreadyExists/Conflict→AlreadyExists, QuotaExceeded/FileTooLarge/RateLimitExceeded→ResourceExhausted, and five codes→Internal, so the structured wafer code cannot recover the original solobase code. I grep-confirmed that NOTHING in the tree parses the [...] prefix back out (the only strip_prefix('[') is in admin/settings.rs for an unrelated authority string), so the bracket is purely human-facing display, never re-parsed — meaning a client needing the granular code would have to string-parse it. rate_limit.rs:260-275 (rate_limited_response) does hand-reproduce the identical [code] message + solobase_error_code_to_wafer construction instead of delegating to error_response. The recommendation is idiomatic and available: WaferError.meta is documented in wafer-block/core_types.rs:143 as 'Structured key/value attributes (e.g. resource name, retry hint)' and rate_limit already uses meta for resp.header.Retry-After, so carrying error.code as a meta entry is the established mechanism and would preserve the granular code without string parsing. This does brush the project's 'no implicit mapping / no translation between representations' convention. Severity stays low: it's a maintainability/architecture smell, not a correctness or security bug — the prefix is consistent, no internal code round-trips through it, and these are JSON-API surfaces where clients switch on the coarse wafer code or HTTP status. Not a quick-fix candidate worth blocking on, but the reviewer's analysis is accurate.

title: send_email swallows all failures into a bool with no logging — Mailgun errors and misconfiguration are invisible to operators
description: `send_email` returns `bool` and discards every failure cause. Missing API key/domain returns `false` under a comment claiming it will 'log but don't fail' — but there is no `tracing` call. A non-2xx Mailgun response or a transport error (`Err(_) => false`) is collapsed to `false` with no log of the status code or error. Callers (auth signup/verify/forgot-password) get `{"sent": false}` with HTTP 200 and cannot tell a disabled-email deployment from a broken API key from a Mailgun outage. This defeats diagnosability for a security-critical path (verification / password-reset email delivery).
recommendation: Log the actual failure: `tracing::warn!` on missing config, and `tracing::error!(status = resp.status_code, ...)` / `tracing::error!(error = %e, ...)` on the non-2xx and transport-error branches before returning false (mirror the sanitized-logging discipline of helpers::err_internal). Returning bool to the caller is acceptable for the anti-enumeration flows, but the cause must be observable server-side.
verifierNote: Verified against crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/email.rs lines 335-398. The quoted code exists exactly: send_email returns bool; the missing-config branch (lines 353-356) returns false under a comment "Email not configured — log but don't fail" with NO actual log; the Mailgun call (lines 394-397) collapses both non-2xx (`Ok(resp) => (200..300).contains(&resp.status_code)`) and transport errors (`Err(_) => false`) to false with no logging of status code or error. tracing IS imported and used elsewhere in this same file (line 136, an Init-time warn about ALLOWED_RECIPIENT_PATTERNS), so the "log but don't fail" comment is a genuine lie and the fix is trivially feasible/consistent. Diagnosability confirmed end-to-end: the email.send_template handler returns ok_json(&SendResp{sent}) (helpers.rs:225 = a SUCCESS frame, not an error frame). The auth callers (auth_ui/api/signup.rs:327 send_verification_email and auth_ui/api/forgot_password.rs:83 send_reset_email) only tracing::warn! when collect_buffered() returns Err — i.e. only on dispatch-level failures. A Mailgun outage, bad API key, or missing config all return Ok(sent:false), so the caller's warn never fires and the failure is invisible in server logs. The reviewer's substance is correct on this security-critical path (verification + password-reset delivery). Two minor framing corrections: (a) the auth endpoints fire-and-forget the email and ignore the `sent` field entirely, so it isn't literally the auth route that returns {sent:false} with HTTP 200 — that body is the internal email-block response; the net effect (silent both client- and server-side) is unchanged. (b) Returning bool to clients is itself acceptable for anti-enumeration, so this is an observability/code-smell gap, not a security vulnerability. Aligns with the project's "no code smells / fix at root cause" rule and the #249 sanitized-logging discipline (err_internal). Medium is the right severity — real operability defect with a misleading comment, but no data loss or security breach. Recommendation (tracing::warn! on missing config, tracing::error!(status=...) / (error=%e) on the failure branches) is sound and idiomatic for this file.

title: Hardcoded "Solobase" brand in from-address fallback while app name is otherwise config-driven
description: When `SUPPERS_AI__EMAIL__MAILGUN_FROM` is unset, the from header defaults to the hardcoded literal `"Solobase <noreply@{domain}>"`. Yet the very same handler reads the configurable `SOLOBASE_SHARED__APP_NAME` (default "Solobase") for template subjects/bodies 100 lines below. A white-labelled deployment that sets APP_NAME gets branded templates but a from-address still hardcoded to 'Solobase'. This is a hardcoded domain-specific value where a config var already exists.
recommendation: Derive the fallback display name from `SOLOBASE_SHARED__APP_NAME` (already fetched elsewhere) instead of the literal "Solobase", e.g. `format!("{app_name} <noreply@{domain}>")`, so branding is consistent and config-driven.
verifierNote: Confirmed real. email.rs:344-351 (send_email) builds the from-header fallback as the hardcoded literal `format!("Solobase <noreply@{domain}>")` when SUPPERS_AI__EMAIL__MAILGUN_FROM is unset. The same module already has a config-driven brand name: `SOLOBASE_SHARED__APP_NAME` (declared in config_vars.rs:18, default "Solobase"), fetched at email.rs:234 and used in 9 template subjects/bodies. So a white-label deployment that sets APP_NAME=Acme but leaves MAILGUN_FROM unset gets "Acme" in every subject/body but "Solobase <noreply@...>" in the From header — a genuine hardcoded-brand inconsistency that the project's "no hardcoded domain-specific values; use config" rule targets, since the config var literally already exists for this purpose. Two corrections to the reviewer: (1) app_name is fetched ~110 lines ABOVE the fallback (line 234), not "100 lines below"; (2) app_name is in scope only in handle_send_template, NOT inside send_email, so the recommended fix must thread app_name into send_email (or read it there) rather than a one-token swap — still trivial. Severity is correctly low: no security/correctness impact (emails send fine; recipient-header injection is already guarded by validate_recipient), purely cosmetic brand leakage for white-label deployments. Note the MAILGUN_FROM override exists, so this only bites operators who set APP_NAME but not MAILGUN_FROM.

title: Duplicated validate/allow-list/rate-limit preamble across email.send and email.send_template handlers
description: `handle_send` and `handle_send_template` repeat the identical 3-step guard preamble verbatim: validate_recipient → check_recipient_allowed → check_caller_rate_limit, including the same error-conversion arms. Any future change to the gating sequence must be made in two places and can drift.
recommendation: Extract a single `async fn guard_recipient(limiter, ctx, to) -> Result<(), OutputStream>` that runs the three checks and returns the early-exit OutputStream on failure; call it from both handlers with `?`-style early return.

title: Large inline HTML email templates embedded in match arms make handle_send_template a 120-line function
description: `handle_send_template` inlines four full HTML documents (verification, password_reset, payment_failed, welcome) as raw string literals inside the match, each ~15-20 lines with duplicated inline-style boilerplate. The function mixes URL construction, branding, and presentation, and the duplicated `<div style="font-family:...">` wrapper is copy-pasted four times. This is hard to test, hard to restyle consistently, and bloats the handler.
recommendation: Move each template into a dedicated function (or a small `templates` submodule) returning `(subject, html, text)` given typed inputs, sharing the common HTML wrapper/button helper. handle_send_template then becomes a thin dispatch on `req.template` plus the shared send call.

title: "unknown" caller sentinel skips the storage-path safety check and shares one namespace across all unidentified callers
description: When `ctx.caller_id()` is `None`, caller is set to the literal string `"unknown"`, then the safety validation is explicitly skipped for that value (`if caller != "unknown" && !is_safe_block_name(...)`). Two consequences: (1) every unidentified caller is bucketed into the same `unknown/...` storage namespace, so distinct anonymous callers can read/overwrite each other's objects — an isolation gap; (2) using a magic sentinel string as an identity is exactly the implicit-mapping smell the conventions forbid, and the sentinel could in principle collide with a real block named `unknown`. The path-traversal and namespacing logic all proceed using this sentinel as a trusted prefix.
recommendation: Treat a missing caller_id as a hard error (return PermissionDenied) rather than synthesizing a shared `"unknown"` namespace that bypasses validation — storage ops without an identified caller cannot be safely isolated or WRAP-checked. If anonymous storage is a real requirement, model it with an explicit, validated identity rather than a sentinel string, and still run is_safe_block_name on it.
verifierNote:
Code quoted accurately at storage.rs:228-236: `let caller = ctx.caller_id().unwrap_or("unknown")` then `if caller != "unknown" && !is_safe_block_name(&caller)`. The mechanism the reviewer describes is correct, but the severity is overstated; it's a real low-severity code-quality/defensive-coding issue, not a medium security/isolation bug.

What holds up: the `"unknown"` literal IS a magic sentinel that (a) special-cases out of the is_safe_block_name validation and (b) is then used as a trusted storage path prefix. This squarely violates the project convention "No magic code or implicit mapping layers." It is also genuinely collision-prone: `is_safe_block_name("unknown")` returns true, so a hypothetical real block literally named `unknown` would share the same `unknown/...` namespace as an anonymous caller. Silently synthesizing an identity instead of rejecting is poor hygiene, and the recommendation (return PermissionDenied when caller_id is None) is sound and consistent with how WRAP already treats anonymous callers elsewhere.

What does NOT hold up — the medium-severity isolation framing: `ctx.caller_id()` is `None` ONLY for a top-level dispatch (wafer-run context.rs make_context:449; runner.rs:109). In solobase no HTTP route dispatches directly to `wafer-run/storage` — routing.rs sends `/b/storage/` to `suppers-ai/files`, and all storage access flows through feature blocks via `ctx.call_block`/the `store::` client, where the callee sees `caller_id = Some(\"suppers-ai/files\")` (context.rs:377). So there is no production path delivering DISTINCT anonymous end-users to this handler; \"anonymous caller\" here means \"the runtime dispatched the block directly,\" not \"unauthenticated user,\" so the \"distinct anonymous callers can read/overwrite each other's objects\" scenario isn't reachable. The reviewer also slightly mis-frames WRAP: for own-namespace (non-`@`) ops the block intentionally relies on the call_block-boundary WRAP check, not a per-op check; and that boundary's Storage branch (wrap.rs:304-315) skips the `Some(caller)` self-admit when caller is None and falls through to default-deny (line 347) — so a genuinely anonymous storage op would be denied at the boundary, not silently bucketed. Net: real convention violation + missing hard-deny, but low severity and no demonstrated cross-tenant data exposure.

title: Storage access-log duration computed from wall-clock subtraction can underflow on clock step-back
description: `start = now_millis()` and later `duration_ms = (now_millis() - start) as i64` at three sites. `now_millis()` is wall-clock (chrono::Utc), not monotonic. If the system clock steps backward between `start` and completion (NTP correction), the `u64` subtraction wraps to ~1.8e19 in release (logged as a nonsense duration) and panics in debug builds. The same wall-clock minus pattern repeats at lines 373 and 391.
recommendation: Use a monotonic timer for elapsed-time measurement (std::time::Instant on native; wasm can use performance.now via a monotonic helper) or, at minimum, `now_millis().saturating_sub(start)` to avoid wrap/panic. now_millis (wall-clock) is fine for timestamps but not for durations.
verifierNote: Confirmed real. now_millis() (helpers.rs:20-22) is wall-clock: chrono::Utc::now().timestamp_millis() as u64 — not monotonic. The unguarded u64 subtraction `(now_millis() - start) as i64` exists verbatim at three sites in storage.rs: line 336 (StreamEvent::Complete), 373 (Halt), and 391 (stream-ended fallback), all bracketing one storage op started at `let start = now_millis()` (line 312). On wall-clock step-back (NTP correction, suspend/resume) the second read can be < start: release builds wrap to ~1.8e19 then reinterpret via `as i64` (garbage logged duration); debug builds panic on subtract-overflow. Note the panic site is inside the from_producer async closure, so a debug panic would crash that stream task, slightly worse than the reviewer's 'nonsense number' framing. Decisive corroboration: this codebase ALREADY recognizes and guards this exact hazard in pipeline.rs:189-194, with a comment 'now_millis() reads wall clock — saturating_sub guards against clock skew on suspend/resume from regressing the subtraction, and try_into clamps ... to i64::MAX', using `i64::try_from(now_millis().saturating_sub(start_ms)).unwrap_or(i64::MAX)`. The three storage.rs sites simply omit this established idiom. One correction to the reviewer's recommendation: the project deliberately avoids std::time::Instant on wasm32 (cache.rs:27, database.rs:465 comments note Instant::now() panics on wasm32-unknown-unknown), so the right fix is the existing now_millis().saturating_sub(...) + try_from pattern, not Instant. Severity stays low: the affected value is only the best-effort access-log status string (e.g. 'OK (123ms)'), never the operation result or returned bytes; production (release) builds don't panic, just log a bad number; clock step-back within a single short op window is rare. (Same unguarded pattern also exists at admin/pages/database.rs:469, outside this finding's scope.)

title: StreamEvent forwarding loop duplicated between storage and network; network's copy is unnecessary boilerplate
description: network.rs and storage.rs each hand-roll the same ~8-arm `while let Some(evt) = inner.next().await { match evt { Chunk/Meta/Complete/Error/Drop/Continue/Halt } }` forwarding loop inside `OutputStream::from_producer`. storage.rs adds logging on terminal events, but network.rs is a pure 1:1 passthrough that re-emits every event unchanged — it could simply `return self.inner.handle(ctx, msg, ...).await` directly (which preserves frame boundaries just as well), eliminating the loop entirely. As written, the two loops are a maintenance hazard: a fix to event handling must be mirrored in both.
recommendation: For network.rs, return the inner OutputStream directly (no producer wrapper) since there is no transformation. If a generic relay is wanted, add a shared `forward_stream(inner, on_terminal: impl Fn)` helper in helpers.rs and have storage pass its logging closure; network needs no helper at all.

title: Extra (downstream-registered) routes bypass the feature-flag gate that all built-in routes enforce
description: In route_to_block, the built-in ROUTES loop checks features.is_block_enabled(route.block) before dispatching, but the extra_routes loop only runs check_access and then dispatches — it never consults the FeatureConfig. A downstream block registered via SolobaseBuilder::add_route therefore cannot be disabled via block_settings, unlike every built-in. This is an inconsistent, surprising gap in the gating model and means the admin 'disable block' toggle silently does nothing for project-supplied blocks.
recommendation: Add the same `if !features.is_block_enabled(&route.block_name) { return helpers::err_not_found(...) }` guard to the extra_routes loop so downstream routes obey the same enable/disable semantics as built-ins. Factor the matched-route handling (feature gate -> access gate -> dispatch) into one helper shared by both loops to keep them from drifting.
verifierNote:
Confirmed real by reading the source. In crates/solobase-core/src/routing.rs, route_to_block has two dispatch loops: the built-in ROUTES loop (lines 264-282) calls features.is_block_enabled(route.block) before dispatching, but the extra_routes loop (lines 286-297) only runs check_access then dispatches via ctx.call_block(&route.block_name, ...) — it never consults FeatureConfig. The reviewer's quoted evidence matches the file exactly.

The gating-model gap is genuine end-to-end, not cosmetic: (1) a downstream block is registered via SolobaseBuilder::extra_block -> wafer.register_block (builder.rs:493-494), so it appears in ctx.registered_blocks(); (2) the admin blocks page (admin/pages/blocks.rs:37,166-198) lists every registered block with a working enable/disable toggle keyed purely on block_name (handle_toggle_feature, line 241, has no built-in-only restriction); (3) the toggle writes enabled=false into the generic block_settings table; (4) BlockSettings::is_block_enabled (features.rs) is a HashMap keyed by full block name and would correctly report the downstream block as disabled; (5) pipeline.rs routes solely through route_to_block with no other feature gate. So toggling a downstream block off in the admin UI silently does nothing — it keeps serving traffic. ExtraRoute.block_name is the same identity used as the registered block name and the block_settings key, so the recommended fix (gate the extra loop on features.is_block_enabled(&route.block_name)) is correct.

Severity: keeping medium but on the lower end. It is NOT an auth bypass — extra routes still enforce their RouteAccess tier (check_access, Public/Authenticated/Admin). The harm is a silent failure of an admin operational control (operator believes a feature is disabled when it is still live), scoped only to downstream projects that use add_route (e.g. gizza-ai); built-ins are unaffected. Recommended fix aligns with the repo's root-cause/no-drift convention (factor the matched-route feature-gate -> access-gate -> dispatch into one helper shared by both loops).

title: routes_config() exposes only built-in ROUTES to the inspector; extra routes are invisible
description: routes_config() serializes only the static ROUTES table. The inspector / discovery view it backs therefore omits every downstream route added via SolobaseBuilder::add_route, so the routing document is incomplete for any deployment that uses extra routes. The config is also computed once at build time (builder.rs:540 calls routes_config()) and is decoupled from the extra_routes list passed to the same router, so the two views of 'what routes exist' can disagree.
recommendation: Make routes_config take `&[ExtraRoute]` (or have the router build the config from both ROUTES and its own extra_routes) so the inspector view reflects the full routing table actually in effect.

title: Section-9 config injection fully replaces (not merges) any platform-supplied block_config for the same block
description: Platform block_configs are applied first (loop at 475), then section 9 builds a fresh serde_json::Map per block from the ConfigService and calls wafer.add_block_config(&info.name, Object(obj)). add_block_config is a HashMap insert (registry.rs:101), so for any feature block that both (a) was given a config via .block_config(name, ...) and (b) has at least one declared config_key present in the ConfigService, the section-9 object overwrites the platform-supplied object wholesale rather than merging keys. A caller's explicit per-block config can be silently dropped.
recommendation: Either merge section-9 keys into the existing block_config object instead of replacing it, or document/enforce that ConfigService-derived keys and platform-supplied block_configs are disjoint. A read-modify-write merge in add_block_config (or a builder-side merge before insert) removes the clobber.
verifierNote:
The mechanism is accurately described and the code exists as quoted. Section 5b (builder.rs:475-477) applies platform `.block_config()` entries, then section 9 (builder.rs:506-521) builds a fresh serde_json::Map per feature block and calls `wafer.add_block_config(&info.name, Object(obj))`. `add_block_config` is indeed a plain HashMap::insert (wafer-run registry.rs:101 — `self.registration.block_configs.insert(name.into(), config)`), so it replaces wholesale, not merges. So in principle, a `suppers-ai/*` block that both (a) received a platform `.block_config()` and (b) has a declared config_key present+non-empty in the ConfigService at build time would have its platform object clobbered.

However, the clobber does NOT fire in any actual consumer, so this is a latent footgun, not an active bug:
1. The only solobase-repo platform `.block_config()` caller (solobase-web/src/lib.rs:108) targets `wafer-run/security-headers` — a wafer-run/* middleware block. Section 9 iterates `crate::blocks::all_block_infos()`, which deliberately enumerates ONLY solobase feature blocks and EXCLUDES wafer-run/* framework blocks (blocks/mod.rs:49-55 doc comment explicitly calls this out). So security-headers is never re-inserted by section 9.
2. The one consumer that configures a suppers-ai/* feature block via platform `.block_config()` is gizza-ai (`suppers-ai/llm`, gizza-ai/src/lib.rs:137-143), setting SUPPERS_AI__LLM__DEFAULT_PROVIDER and DEFAULT_MODEL. Those keys are declared config_keys of LlmBlock (llm/mod.rs:422-435) but they are non-auto_generate, non-secret, and are never seeded into gizza's `variables` table (config.rs seed_auto_generated only seeds auto_generate vars; the explicit seeds don't include them). So config_ref.get() returns empty, the `if !obj.is_empty()` guard short-circuits, and add_block_config is never re-called for suppers-ai/llm. On top of that, gizza's EnvConfigService is populated only in Phase 3 (post-build) while section 9 runs during .build(), so it's empty during section 9 regardless.

Net: the reviewer's "can be silently dropped" is theoretically possible for a future consumer that sets a feature-block config key which also lives in the ConfigService, but no current code path triggers it. The merge-vs-replace recommendation is a reasonable robustness hardening but not a fix for an active defect. Downgraded medium→low.

title: JWT secret silently defaults to empty string when unset
description: build() reads the JWT signing secret with config.get(JWT_SECRET_KEY).unwrap_or_default(), so a misconfigured deployment with no SUPPERS_AI__AUTH__JWT_SECRET set gets an empty-string secret and the runtime boots silently. Every Bearer token is then validated against an empty HMAC key. An attacker who knows the deployment uses an empty secret can forge arbitrary JWTs (including admin claims). A required security secret should fail loudly at build, not degrade to a trivially-forgeable key.
recommendation: Treat a missing/empty JWT secret as a hard error in build() (return RuntimeError::Config) for production, or at minimum gate it on SOLOBASE_SHARED__ENVIRONMENT and refuse to start in production with an empty secret. Do not let an empty signing key reach crypto::extract_auth_meta.
verifierNote: REAL, with one correction to the reviewer's claim. The quoted code exists verbatim at builder.rs:313-315 (`config.get(JWT_SECRET_KEY).unwrap_or_default()`), and the secret flows to SolobaseRouterBlock (builder.rs:534) -> handle_request -> crypto::extract_auth_meta, which calls derive_block_jwt_key(jwt_secret, ...) then jwt_verify(token, jwt_secret). An empty secret becomes the HMAC verify key, so forged tokens (incl. admin claims) validate. JWT_SECRET is NOT a declared ConfigVar config_key (confirmed by the explicit comment at auth_ui/mod.rs:117-124: "currently aren't exposed as declared config_keys (wafer-run gap)"), so the WAFER runtime's start()-time required-var validator referenced at builder.rs:499 does NOT catch a missing/empty secret. CORRECTION: the reviewer's "the runtime boots silently" is FALSE for the native binary — cli/server.rs:71-76 explicitly rejects an empty JWT secret before build() and seeds it on fresh DBs (server.rs:339-350). The real exposure is the Cloudflare embedder (the production wafer.run path): solobase-cloudflare/src/lib.rs:236-239 uses the identical `.unwrap_or_default()` with NO empty-check; if the `SUPPERS_AI__AUTH__JWT_SECRET` worker secret is unset, env.secret(key) returns Err, the key never enters cfg_svc_map (lib.rs:202-208), and the runtime boots with an empty HMAC key (crypto_service.rs has no guard either). So the vulnerability is real and fail-open. The reviewer correctly identified builder.rs as the right chokepoint: per CLAUDE.md root-cause guidance, the guard belongs in build() so it protects all embedders uniformly (native already guards independently; Cloudflare does not). Severity stays high (trivially-forgeable admin tokens / auth bypass), gated on operator misconfiguration; native is already safe but Cloudflare is not. Recommendation to return RuntimeError::Config on empty secret in build() is sound.

title: Inspector block_config is hardcoded and order-fragile relative to platform configs
description: The inspector's { allow_anonymous: false } config is set at line 469, immediately before the platform block_configs loop. Because add_block_config is a last-writer-wins insert, a platform that supplies its own wafer-run/inspector config would override this security-relevant default — and the only thing preventing that today is loop ordering, which is implicit and easy to break. The hardcoded literal also lives in builder.rs rather than alongside the inspector block's other config.
recommendation: Move the inspector default after the platform loop (so it is authoritative) or merge rather than replace, and consider sourcing the value from the inspector block's own config defaults instead of a literal in the builder.

title: build() is a ~320-line procedural god-method mixing service wiring, feature gating, fs discovery, and config injection
description: SolobaseBuilder::build is one 300+ line function with 12 numbered, order-dependent phases (validate services, create runtime, register service blocks, build LLM/image/vector routers, cfg-gated wasm32 middleware registration, hardcoded inspector config, platform configs, auth/llm registration, ConfigService bridge, router build, wasm/flow filesystem discovery, flow registration). The phases share mutable `wafer` and have non-obvious ordering constraints (e.g. block_infos must be collected after all registrations; section-9 must run after platform configs). This concentrates a lot of independently-testable logic in one untestable monolith and makes the ordering invariants implicit.
recommendation: Extract the cohesive phases into private helpers taking &mut Wafer (register_service_blocks, build_llm_router, register_middleware_wasm32, inject_feature_block_configs, discover_wasm_and_flows). The build() body then reads as a sequence of named steps, each unit-testable, and the ordering constraints become explicit function boundaries.
verifierNote: Factually accurate. crates/solobase-core/src/builder.rs:291-607 is a single ~316-line build() method with 12+ numbered phases (1,2,3,4,4c,4a-bis,4b,5,5b,6,6b,7,9,10,11,12 — there is no phase 8) all mutating one `let mut wafer`. The implicit ordering constraints the reviewer cites are real and even self-documented: section 10 collects `wafer.block_infos()` with the comment "AFTER all blocks are registered"; section 9 config injection runs after section 5b platform configs; the LLM router (369-382) must be built before register_llm at 6b (490) consumes provider_llm_svc; cfg-gated wasm32 middleware registration (436-467) and `#[cfg(feature = "wasm")]` fs discovery of wasm blocks + flow JSON (545-601) are interleaved with config wiring. The phases do mix concerns (validation, runtime creation, router construction, middleware registration, ConfigService->block_config bridge, fs discovery). So the architecture-smell observation is genuine and the extract-helpers recommendation is reasonable.\n\nBUT severity is overstated at medium. This violates NONE of the enumerated convention rules that generate findings: no sync bridge, no raw SQL, no stringly-typed error misuse (errors use explicit RuntimeError::Config/::Wasm variants, consistent with the #249 refactor), and the one hardcoded literal (`allow_anonymous: false` for the inspector at 469-472) is a runtime security default, not a domain-specific value belonging in a ConfigVar. It is a builder's terminal assembly step — inherently sequential wiring with real ordering deps that are heavily commented. The "untestable monolith" framing is overstated: build() is testable end-to-end, and the proposed &mut Wafer helpers would still have to be called in order. Real but minor maintainability smell, not a correctness or security risk: downgrade to low.

title: project_name parsing from Host header can be empty / attacker-controlled and is silently trusted
description: For /openapi.json and /.well-known/agent.json, server_url and project_name are derived directly from the untrusted Host header (host.split('.').next()). For a host like '.example.com' or an empty host this yields an empty project_name; for any spoofed Host it yields attacker-chosen values embedded in the generated OpenAPI/agent card and server_url. The split-and-unwrap_or pattern hides the empty-host case behind a fallback that only triggers when next() returns None (which it never does for split on a String — it always yields at least one element, possibly empty).
recommendation: Source project_name and server URL from a config var (e.g. SOLOBASE_SHARED__SITE_URL / APP_NAME) rather than the raw Host header, or validate/normalize the host and treat empty as the fallback. This also makes the discovery output deterministic across proxies that rewrite Host.

title: rebuild_streaming and collect_buffered_with_prelude duplicate the full StreamEvent match
description: Both functions enumerate every StreamEvent variant (Chunk/Meta/Complete/Error/Drop/Continue/Halt) with near-identical handling, and rebuild_streaming repeats the same seven-arm match twice (once for the peeked next_event at 269-302, once for the pump loop at 304-337). Adding a StreamEvent variant requires editing three nearly-identical match blocks; it is easy to update one and miss another. The 'peek then drain' logic is also subtle enough to deserve a single shared primitive.
recommendation: Build a single iterator/stream that yields the peeked event first then chains `rest`, and write one match arm set over it (rebuild_streaming already needs only the forwarding form). For collect_buffered_with_prelude the loop+peeked.take() generator pattern can be replaced by the same chained stream, eliminating the second copy of the variant match.

title: Audit-log field assembly is a hand-rolled HashMap of stringly-typed columns
description: The request_logs row is built by inserting eight individually-stringified keys into a HashMap<String, Value>, then stamp_created mutates it, then db::create writes it. The column names ('method','path','status','status_code','duration_ms','error_message','client_ip','user_id') are duplicated string literals with no shared schema link to the admin REQUEST_LOGS_TABLE migration, so a column rename only fails at runtime. status_label ('OK'/'ERROR') and status_code are also redundant representations of the same outcome.
recommendation: Define a typed RequestLog struct (serde::Serialize) owned by the admin block alongside REQUEST_LOGS_TABLE and serialize it into the data map, so the column set lives in one place and changes are compile-checked. Consider dropping the redundant status_label in favour of deriving it from status_code.

title: (status_label, status_code, error_message, reply) tuple is primitive obsession over an internal outcome type
description: handle_request matches the buffered terminal into a 4-tuple of (&'static str, i64, String, OutputStream) and then destructures it for logging + return. The big type annotation on the let binding and the parallel construction of four loosely-related values in every match arm is a smell — it is really one 'request outcome' value (status label, code, error, response stream). Several arms repeat the identical status-code extraction from buf.meta (lines 143-147 and 174-179).
recommendation: Introduce a small RequestOutcome { status_label, status_code, error_message, reply } struct (or named fields) returned by a helper that maps a TerminalNotResponse into it, and extract the repeated 'parse status code from meta' into a `status_from_meta(&[MetaEntry]) -> i64` helper used by both the Ok and Halt arms.

title: BlockState 'default enabled' fallback is duplicated in four places via copy-pasted struct literals
description: The 'block with no row defaults to enabled + empty migration state' value is constructed by hand in BlockSettings::state (96-100), in state_for's `missing` closure (136-140), and conceptually again in is_block_enabled's unwrap_or(true) (159). These must stay in lock-step (e.g. if a new BlockState field changes the default), but nothing enforces it. BlockState already derives Default but with enabled=false by field default — the #[serde(default = "default_true")] only applies on deserialize, so Default::default() and these literals disagree on `enabled`.
recommendation: Add a single `fn default_enabled() -> BlockState` (or impl a custom Default that returns enabled=true) and call it from state(), state_for(), and anywhere else needing the missing-row value, so the default lives in one place and matches the serde default. This also resolves the latent Default-derives-false-but-serde-defaults-true inconsistency.

title: block_settings RwLock .write().expect() can panic during builder configuration
description: SolobaseBuilder::block_settings acquires the RwLock write guard and .expect()s on poisoning during build configuration. A poisoned lock here is only possible if another thread panicked while holding the guard, which is unlikely during single-threaded builder setup, so this is borderline; but the builder API otherwise returns errors (build() returns Result) and a panic in a chained-setter is surprising. The expect message at least explains the cause.
recommendation: Low priority. If builder construction is guaranteed single-threaded, this is acceptable as-is (infallible-by-construction). If you want consistency with build()'s Result-based errors, store BlockSettings unwrapped until build() wraps it in the Arc<RwLock>, avoiding the lock entirely during setter chaining.

title: Cross-repo HKDF key derivation duplicated by hand with a fragile "must match" coupling
description: derive_block_jwt_key is a byte-for-byte reimplementation of wafer-block-crypto's Argon2JwtCryptoService::derive_block_key (same None salt, same `wafer-jwt|{block_id}` info string, same 32-byte output, same hex encoding). The doc comment says it 'must match' the other crate, but nothing enforces it — and the file's own history note (lines 219-224) documents that PR #170 silently reverted the block-id used here, breaking production auth without any test catching it. Two independent copies of a security-critical derivation that must stay bit-identical across repos is exactly the implicit-coupling/magic-mapping the conventions forbid; if wafer-block-crypto changes its info string or salt, solobase verification silently fails closed (every user logged out) or — worse if the change is subtle — could fail open.
recommendation: Expose the derivation once in wafer-block-crypto (e.g. a `pub fn derive_block_jwt_key(master: &str, block_id: &str) -> String` or a verify-with-block-id entry point) and have solobase call it, rather than maintaining a second copy. solobase-core already depends on wafer-run crates. If a public API can't be exposed, add a shared cross-repo test vector (fixed master+block_id → fixed expected key) asserted in BOTH crates so any drift fails CI on at least one side. The string-history comment is documentation, not a guard.
verifierNote: Verified against both files. The duplication is real and byte-for-byte: solobase-core/src/crypto.rs:159-164 and wafer-run/crates/wafer-block-crypto/src/service.rs:168-178 both do Hkdf::<Sha256>::new(None, secret), info="wafer-jwt|{block_id}", 32-byte OKM, lowercase-hex encode. The "must match" coupling (crypto.rs:150,154) is enforced only by a doc comment. The history note (crypto.rs:219-224) documents a real prior production regression: PR #155 (bcf96ce) set the block id to AUTH_UI_BLOCK_ID, PR #170 (d7107c4) silently reverted it during a Result-wrap refactor, and unit tests missed it because they sign with the master secret directly (only the fallback branch). The recommended root-cause fix is feasible and already half-present: solobase-core depends on wafer-block-crypto (Cargo.toml:142) and the CryptoService trait already exposes verify_for(block_id, token) / sign_for (service.rs:245-253) doing exactly the derive-then-verify that extract_auth_meta reimplements; bootstrap.rs:126 and solobase-browser/crypto.rs already construct Argon2JwtCryptoService directly. So extract_auth_meta could call Argon2JwtCryptoService::new(master)?.verify_for(AUTH_UI_BLOCK_ID, token) instead of hand-deriving the key — eliminating the second copy. This is a genuine architecture-smell under the conventions (no magic/implicit mapping layers; fix at root cause, no code smells). Two corrections that reduce severity from the claimed "high": (1) A regression test now EXISTS (crypto.rs:479-504) covering the block-id reversion — though it does NOT guard cross-repo HKDF drift, because it generates its token with the LOCAL derive_block_jwt_key, so an info-string/salt change in wafer-block-crypto would still pass solobase's test while breaking production; the reviewer's drift concern thus stands. (2) The reviewer's "could fail open" is unsupported: HKDF parameter drift fails CLOSED (derived keys diverge -> signature mismatch -> verify fails -> request continues unauthenticated / mass logout), a loud failure, not a silent auth bypass. No current incorrectness exists (both copies presently agree). Net: a real maintainability + cross-repo-coupling smell with documented prior breakage, worst realistic outcome fail-closed; medium, not high.

title: BLOCK_SETTINGS_TABLE name hardcoded in migration_helper, duplicating the single-owner const in admin::settings
description: The convention is that each repo module owns exactly one `pub const TABLE = "{org}__{block}__{name}"` and there are no duplicated table-name strings. Here the literal "suppers_ai__admin__block_settings" is re-declared as a private const to dodge a stated circular dependency on crate::blocks::admin — but admin::settings::BLOCK_SETTINGS_TABLE already holds the canonical value (and cache_key.rs correctly imports it). This is a third hand-typed copy of the same magic string (features.rs doc comments hold several more). A rename of the table would silently desync migration_helper from the rest of the codebase; the cache-key derivation and the actual writes would diverge.
recommendation: Break the circular dependency properly rather than papering over it with a copy: move the table-name const (and ideally MigrationState/BlockSettings access) into a low-level module that both migration_helper and admin can depend on (features.rs already hosts BLOCK_SETTINGS_CONFIG_KEY and MigrationState and has no admin dependency — put `pub const BLOCK_SETTINGS_TABLE` there and have admin::settings re-export it). Then migration_helper imports the one canonical const.
verifierNote:
Verified against the actual code. The finding holds up factually. migration_helper.rs:33 declares `const BLOCK_SETTINGS_TABLE: &str = "suppers_ai__admin__block_settings";` with a comment "Hardcoded here to avoid a circular dep on `crate::blocks::admin`." The canonical owner already exists at crates/solobase-core/src/blocks/admin/settings.rs:101 (`pub const BLOCK_SETTINGS_TABLE`), re-exported via admin/mod.rs:14, and consumed correctly elsewhere (cache_key.rs:9, pages/blocks.rs:7). So this is a genuine second copy of the table-name literal, which violates the explicit solobase/CLAUDE.md convention: "each repo module owns its own `pub const TABLE`... No central `*_COLLECTION` constants" and the workspace no-magic-code rule (one value, one definition).

Two corrections to the reviewer's framing: (1) The stated "circular dependency" justification is technically false as written. Rust permits circular *module* references within a single crate — `migration_helper` importing from `crate::blocks::admin::settings` would NOT be a compile error, even though `admin` already depends on `migration_helper` (settings.rs:573 calls upsert_block_settings_fields, migrations/mod.rs:27 calls apply_migrations). The comment papers over a layering preference (low-level helper shouldn't reach up into a block module), not an actual compiler-enforced cycle. (2) The reviewer's recommended home — features.rs — is verified clean: it has zero admin/blocks dependency (only `use std::collections::HashMap`) and already hosts BLOCK_SETTINGS_CONFIG_KEY, MigrationState, and BlockState/BlockSettings, all tied to this same table. Moving the const there and re-exporting from admin::settings is the correct root-cause fix.

Severity downgraded from medium to low: both constants are compile-time `&str` with identical current values (no live bug), and a future rename would fail LOUDLY at runtime (D1 list/create/update against a nonexistent table on next deploy), not silently corrupt data as the reviewer implies ("silently desync"). The cache-key path imports the canonical admin const, so a desync would surface as a hard table-not-found error, not divergent caching. Real convention violation, limited blast radius.

title: TtlCache::get_or_load is not single-flight — concurrent misses all run the fetcher (thundering herd)
description: The lock is dropped before `fetcher().await` and re-taken only to store the result. If N requests hit a cold/expired cache concurrently, all N observe a miss, all N run the (expensive D1/KV) fetcher, and the last writer wins. For the Cloudflare D1-read memoization use case this defeats part of the point of the cache during cold-start bursts and on every TTL boundary — exactly when load tends to spike. The doc comment markets this as a per-instance memoizer for D1 reads, so concurrent in-flight requests are plausible within one isolate.
recommendation: If single-flight matters for the D1 cost target, hold an async coordination primitive (e.g. tokio::sync::Mutex around an Option, or a OnceCell / shared in-flight future) so concurrent misses await one fetch. If the duplicate-fetch cost is deemed acceptable (Workers isolates are typically single-request), add a one-line doc note stating the cache intentionally allows concurrent refills, so the omission reads as a decision rather than an oversight.

title: invalidate_keys on update keys off the OLD row only — a changed identity column would leave the new key stale
description: invalidate_keys derives keys from a single row payload. The kv_cached_db update path (kv_cached_db.rs:298-313) pre-reads the existing row and passes its data, so only the OLD value of `block`/`block_name` is invalidated. If an update ever mutated the identity column, the cache entry under the NEW key would not be dropped and would serve stale data until TTL. Today these columns are effectively immutable identities so it doesn't bite, but nothing in the cache_key contract documents or enforces that invariant — it's an implicit assumption split across two files. The all-rows fallback for block_settings masks it there; variables has no such safety net.
recommendation: Document in invalidate_keys' doc comment that callers must invalidate both pre- and post-update rows when the identity column can change, OR assert at the contract boundary that block/block_name is immutable (e.g. reject updates that touch the key column in the wrapper). State the assumption explicitly so a future writer that allows key-column edits doesn't silently introduce stale cache reads.

title: split_statements does not handle string literals or block comments — a semicolon inside a quoted SQL value would mis-split a migration
description: The statement splitter only tracks `--` line comments; it splits on every other `;`. The module doc (lines 14-16) acknowledges `/* */` block comments and `;` inside string literals are unsupported and relies on 'the canonical .sql files don't use either'. That's an unenforced invariant on hand-authored migration files: the moment a block ships a seed INSERT with a value containing a semicolon (e.g. a default description or a CHECK constraint expression), apply_if_blessed will split mid-statement and the migration will fail in a confusing way at deploy time. The per-block statement-count tests (files=14, products=19, etc.) would catch a count change but not a silently-wrong split if the count happened to match.
recommendation: Either track single-quote string-literal state (and optionally /* */) in the splitter so it's robust to any valid migration SQL, or add a debug-assert / compile-time guard that the canonical .sql files contain no quoted semicolons. Given this runs untrusted-by-omission hand-written SQL at every deploy, making the splitter literal-aware is the root-cause fix and is small.

title: is_duplicate_column_error matches the over-broad substring "already exists", risking swallowing unrelated ALTER errors
description: The duplicate-column tolerance is gated on is_alter_add_column AND is_duplicate_column_error, but the latter matches the bare substring "already exists", not just the Postgres column-exists phrase. Postgres also says 'relation "x" already exists', 'constraint "x" ... already exists', 'type "x" already exists', etc. An ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN that adds a column with an inline UNIQUE/constraint whose constraint name collides would report 'already exists' and be silently swallowed as a benign no-op, masking a real schema conflict and then stamping write_state as if the migration fully applied. The gate's intent is specifically duplicate-COLUMN.
recommendation: Tighten the Postgres branch to the column-specific phrasing, e.g. require both 'column' and 'already exists' (`lower.contains("column") && lower.contains("already exists")`) instead of the bare 'already exists' substring, so only true duplicate-column errors are tolerated and other 'already exists' failures still propagate.

title: extract_auth_meta double-verifies every token (derived key then master fallback) — fallback exists only for test fixtures
description: Production tokens are always signed with the auth-ui-derived key, but extract_auth_meta runs the derived-key HMAC verify and, on any failure, runs a SECOND full HMAC verify against the raw master secret. The comment states the master-secret fallback exists 'for tokens signed without block derivation (e.g. unit-test fixtures)'. Carrying a second verification path purely to accommodate test fixtures is a code smell: it doubles the crypto work on every failed/anonymous request, and — more importantly — it widens the accepted-key set in production to include the raw master secret, which production tokens never use. That is a (small) attack-surface and clarity cost paid to avoid making the tests derive a key.
recommendation: Drop the master-secret fallback and update the few unit tests that sign with the master directly to sign with the auth-ui-derived key (the regression test at line 480 already shows the pattern). Production then verifies against exactly one key, the function does one HMAC per request, and the accepted key set matches what is actually minted.

title: Sidebar 'Security' nav item renders the wrong icon (lock falls through to package fallback)
description: The portal 'Security' nav item declares icon name "lock", but ui::sidebar::nav_icon() has no match arm for "lock" — it falls through to the `_ => icons::package()` fallback. So the sidebar shows a package/box icon for Security instead of the padlock, even though icons::lock() exists and is used directly elsewhere (userportal/pages/dashboard.rs:47). The silent fallback masks the typo, so it ships unnoticed.
recommendation: Add `"lock" => icons::lock(),` to nav_icon()'s match. More fundamentally, see the stringly-typed-icon finding: replacing the &str icon key with a typed enum (or having nav_icon return Option and asserting coverage in a test) would make this class of typo a compile error or test failure instead of a silent wrong-icon render.
verifierNote: Verified against the actual code. nav_groups.rs:60 declares `item("Security", "/b/userportal/security", "lock")`. sidebar.rs:9-30 `nav_icon()` match has NO "lock" arm (only layout-dashboard, users, shield, key, settings, file-text, package, shopping-cart, server, folder, user, globe, robot, network, hard-drive, bar-chart, dollar-sign, link) — so "lock" falls through to `_ => icons::package()` at sidebar.rs:28. icons::lock() does exist (icons.rs:202) and is used directly at blocks/userportal/pages/dashboard.rs:47 (`icons::lock()`), confirming the padlock is the intended icon. The render path is live: sidebar_grouped() calls nav_icon(item.icon) at sidebar.rs:82 for every group item. None of the nav_groups.rs tests assert the rendered icon, so the typo ships silently. The bug is genuine but purely cosmetic — the Security nav item shows a package/box icon instead of a padlock. No security, correctness, or data impact; the link/href is correct. Reviewer's "medium" is overstated for a one-icon visual glitch; adjusted to low. The fix is the one-line match arm `"lock" => icons::lock(),`; the deeper stringly-typed-icon concern (enum or coverage test) is a valid but separate refactor.

title: Stringly-typed icon dispatch with silent fallback (nav_icon)
description: NavItem.icon is a `&'static str` that is mapped to an icon function through a large hand-maintained match with a catch-all `_ => icons::package()`. This is primitive obsession: icon names are free strings checked nowhere, the match has implicit aliases (e.g. "file-text" | "logs", "package" | "products"), and any unrecognized/typo'd name silently renders the package box. This directly caused the 'lock' bug above. There is no compile-time or test guarantee that every name used in nav_groups.rs has a real arm.
recommendation: Replace the &str icon key on NavItem with a typed enum (e.g. `enum NavIcon { Dashboard, Users, Lock, ... }`) whose `markup()` method returns the SVG. Then nav_groups.rs can't reference a non-existent icon, the silent package fallback disappears, and the duplicate/alias arms become explicit variants. If a string key must stay (for config-driven nav), at minimum add a test asserting every icon name used in admin()/portal() resolves to a non-fallback icon.
verifierNote: Confirmed against the actual code. crates/solobase-core/src/ui/sidebar.rs:8-30 is exactly as quoted: nav_icon(name: &str) is a hand-maintained match with a silent `_ => icons::package()` catch-all and implicit aliases ("file-text" | "logs", "package" | "products", "folder" | "files", etc.). NavItem.icon is `&'static str` (mod.rs:112). The "lock bug" is real and currently shipping, not hypothetical: nav_groups.rs:60 sets the portal "Security" item icon to "lock", an icons::lock() function exists (icons.rs:202), but nav_icon has no "lock" arm — so Security silently renders a package box. The same Security concept is hardcoded as icons::lock() in dashboard.rs:47, confirming lock is the intended glyph. No compile-time guarantee (the `_` arm makes the match trivially exhaustive) and no test covers icon resolution — nav_groups.rs tests only assert labels/hrefs/order. This matches the CLAUDE.md prohibition on "magic code / implicit mapping layers" and the post-#249 push against stringly-typed dispatch. Two corrections to the reviewer: (1) the pure typed-enum recommendation cannot fully replace the string key because nav_icon has genuine data/config-driven callers — userportal/mod.rs:437 `nav_icon(btn.str_field("icon"))` and dashboard.rs:53 `nav_icon(&b.icon)` (b.icon is a String). The static NavItem path could become an enum, but the dynamic path must keep a string lookup; the reviewer's fallback recommendation (a test asserting every name in admin()/portal() resolves to a non-fallback icon, and not silently substituting package for unknown names) is the load-bearing fix. (2) Severity: keep medium — the architecture-smell framing is medium, but the reviewer understated that this is an active wrong-icon rendering bug, not just a latent smell; it remains capped at medium because the impact is cosmetic (wrong icon, no security/data/correctness-of-data impact).

title: Triplicated Accept-header content negotiation in status responses
description: forbidden_response, not_found_response, and server_error_response each repeat the identical `accept.contains("text/html") && !accept.contains("application/json")` branch and the same html-vs-json shape.
